I'm holding 4-6 offsuit, one of the weakest possible starting hands in poker. I'm in the Big Blind (worst position postflop), and both opponents are already in the pot (Flash raised to 300, Minni called 250). Playing 4-6o out of position against two opponents would be a chip bleed with virtually no upside. My equity against this range is minimal, and I'll be forced into poor decisions postflop. With a solid stack of 14,450, I should preserve chips for premium hands and spots where I have better equity. Folding is the mathematically sound decision here.

Flop: 7♣ 3♥ A♥. I have K♠T♦ — no pair, no heart, no meaningful draw. The button’s open range contains a lot of A‑x and broadways, so I’m almost always behind. If he bets small I can consider a call/float to probe the turn; I’ll fold to significant pressure.

I have flopped top pair with a strong Jack kicker. The board is relatively dry, although there is a heart flush draw present. My opponent checked to me after calling my pre-flop raise from the small blind. I should bet here to get value from weaker Aces (like A2-A9), pocket pairs, and to charge any potential heart draws. A bet of approximately 65% of the pot is standard in this situation to build the pot and protect my hand.

Board is A♥ 7♣ 3♥; I have K♠ T♦, out of position. Flash leads 450 into 1150 — a fairly small c‑bet likely containing a lot of A‑x value plus a wide mix of missed broadways and occasional bluffs. I’m behind most of the Ax combos and I don’t have a heart or an Ace blocker, so a check‑raise bluff here is thin and will be called or crushed too often. Calling 450 is cheap (I’d need roughly ~28% equity to justify it) and preserves my deep stack while allowing me to realize equity or float to take the pot on later streets if Flash shows weakness. Plan: call the flop, fold to significant turn aggression unless I improve (pair K/T or pick up a favorable action), and if checked to me on the turn re‑evaluate whether to bluff or take a free card. I will not raise here because I lack blockers and am OOP vs a wide continuing range.

Board on the turn is A♥ 7♣ 3♥ 9♥ and I hold K♠ T♦ — no pair, no heart, only 6 outs (~13% to pair on the river). Flash checked to me after c-betting the flop; his range is heavy in A‑x and heart combos and I don’t block A or hearts, so a turn bluff is thin and will get called by many of his continuing hands. Pot is 1,600 with deep stacks, so I want pot control and to avoid committing chips with very little equity. I’ll check back, realize my equity, and fold to large river aggression unless I pair or get a very favorable read.

I have top pair with a strong kicker, but the 9h on the turn is a scare card as it completes a possible heart flush. I missed my draws and have only K-high on A♥ 7♣ 3♥ 9♥ 6♠. Flash opened, c-bet the flop and then checked the turn — a line consistent with Ax / some medium hands and some missed broadways. I have no heart or Ace blockers, so my river bluff would be thin and will get called by most Ax/9x/pairs. The pot (1,600) is small relative to our stacks, and risking chips here for little fold equity is unnecessary. I’ll preserve chips for better spots and check to showdown.

I have top pair with a strong kicker (AJ). I believe my hand is still the best, and I can extract value from weaker Aces (like A2-A5, A8, A10) or potentially middle pairs that she is unwilling to fold in a three-handed game. A bet of 800, which is half the pot, is a standard value bet designed to get called by her marginal bluff-catchers.

Fold. Flash opened on the button, c‑bet the A♥7♣3♥ flop and then checked the turn when the 9♥ completed a three‑heart board — that line is consistent with lots of A‑x, some medium pairs (7x/9x) and heart flushes. His small river bet of 800 into the 1600 pot (pot shown 2400 including his bet) gives me ~25% pot‑odds to call (800/(1600+800) ≈ 25%), and K♠T♦ has almost no realistic equity vs that range: I have no pair and no heart blockers to deny flush/value combos. A bluff‑raise is thin (I lack blockers and am out of position), and calling is just a marginal hero‑call against a player who can easily have many value combos. With a healthy stack I’ll preserve chips for better spots and fold.

I have pocket 4s (4d4h) on the button with ~79bb effective in a 3-handed game. In 3-handed play this hand is worth opening for value and fold equity rather than just calling to set-mine; I want to take the initiative, narrow the field and avoid multiway flops where small pairs perform poorly. A standard 3x open to 300 is sized to pressure the blinds (and matches the table’s common sizing), keeps my range wide and gives me position postflop. Plan: c-bet on dry, favorable flops; if I hit a set I’ll build the pot; if I miss and face heavy resistance I’ll be prepared to fold. If either opponent 3-bets large, I’ll typically fold unless it’s a small enough 3-bet worth calling as a set-mine.

Folding 7-Jack offsuit from the small blind. This is a marginal hand that doesn't merit continued investment in this spot. Minni has raised from the button to 300, and I would need to call 250 more chips. Even with favorable pot odds (1.8:1), my hand is too weak to play out of position. 7-Jack offsuit performs poorly against a button raise, especially with the big blind still to act. I have a 14,400 chip stack with 125 hands remaining, so preserving chips by folding weak hands is the correct long-term strategy. This is standard pre-flop poker fundamentals.

I am in the Big Blind with T5s. Minni opened to 3x from the Button. This is a standard wide opening range. T5s is a playable defense hand in the Big Blind against a button open, as it has decent post-flop playability and equity. Folding is too tight, and raising is unnecessary given the stack depths and the strength of my hand. I will call to see a flop.

I have Ten-Five of Spades in the Big Blind. The flop comes Queen-high (Qh 4s 3h). I have no direct connection to this board except for a backdoor flush draw. I have a flopped set (4d4h) on 3h-4s-Qh in heads-up. Flash checked, so I should take the initiative to both extract value from Qx / weaker pairs and protect against overcards and runner-runner heart possibilities. I will bet ~2/3 pot to build the pot and charge draws while keeping worse hands in. If I get a small check-raise I’ll call and re-evaluate on the turn; against an extreme overbet/shove I’ll strongly consider calling given the strength of a set.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Five of Spades. The flop is Qh 4s 3h. I have no pair and only a very weak backdoor flush draw. Minni, the pre-flop aggressor on the Button, has continued for 450 into a pot of 650. This is a fairly standard continuation bet. I have no straight draws, no flush draws (other than backdoor), and overcards like the Ten are likely no good. Continuing here would be a mistake as I am out of position with a hand that will struggle to improve or bluff effectively on later streets. I will fold.

K3o (offsuit) is a marginal hand that doesn't meet the threshold for continuing from the button in this spot. While I'm facing 100 to call into a 150 pot (3:1 odds), K3o has limited playability:

1. **Hand Quality**: K3o has poor showdown value. If I make top pair, I'm easily dominated by stronger king holdings or flopped sets from either blind.

2. **Equity Concerns**: This hand has roughly 30-35% equity against two random holdings, which is insufficient given my tournament goals of maximizing stack growth over 150 hands.

3. **Postflop Difficulty**: Even if I call and hit a King, I'm in an awkward situation with a weak kicker that loses to KQ, KJ, KT, etc.

4. **Tournament Logic**: With 14,400 chips (healthy stack), I should be conservative with marginal holdings and wait for better spots rather than bleeding chips on speculative plays.

5. **Opponent Stacks**: Minni is short (8,175), which could lead to problematic dynamics postflop.

Folding preserves chips for better opportunities and maintains optimal tournament strategy.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Seven offsuit. With the Button folding, it's just me and Minni (Big Blind). J7o is a weak hand to play out of position. I am on the Button in a three-handed game with a decent starting hand (K5o). In this position, I should be opening a wide range of hands to put pressure on the blinds and take advantage of my positional advantage post-flop. I’m in the small blind with J♠4♦ facing a 3x raise to 300 from the button. Folding Ad4h from the big blind against Flash's button raise. Analysis: Ad4o is a weak hand with poor equity out of position. I'm on the button with Td6h (offsuit). Pot is 150 and it’s folded to me — this is a position to open a wide range. T6o is marginal but has postflop playability in position and good steal potential. AK is a premium starting hand requiring aggression. Minni's button raise to 300 is standard, but I have the best hand pre-flop and should re-raise for value. I am holding Ten of Clubs and Deuce of Spades in the Big Blind. Minni opened from the Button and Claude 3-bet from the Small Blind. I opened to 300 on the button with Td6h and Claude (SB) has 3‑bet to 850. Calling would cost me 550 to play a ~1,800 pot (pot odds require roughly 30–31% equity). T6o offsuit is a poor candidate to continue against a 3‑bet — it’s frequently dominated by a 3‑better’s value/broadway range, has weak playability and few useful blockers, and I don’t want to 4‑bet bluff out of position with this holding. Calling 100 from the Button with 94o. While it's a weak hand, positional advantage and 2.5:1 pot odds make this reasonable. I'll have last action post-flop against two committed opponents and can leverage position for potential profit.

I am in the Small Blind with JTo. Claude limped from the Button, which is quite passive. It costs me only 50 chips to call and see a flop in a pot that will be at least 300 chips (3:1 odds minimum). Three-handed with two limp calls ahead: A9 offsuit is strong enough to raise for value and fold equity. Both opponents have shown a tendency to limp/call and to fold to aggression; taking the initiative reduces multi-way chances and isolates one player. I size up to 700 total (about 7bb) — big enough to punish limps and get folds, but small enough to keep stacks manageable postflop. With 94o on the button facing Minni's 700 raise (600 more to call), this is a straightforward fold. While I called 100 preflop based on position and pot odds, Minni's aggressive raise to 700 represents significant strength. 94o is one of the weakest holdings in poker and cannot profitably call further aggression here. The pot odds (calling 600 into ~1,500) don't justify playing a hand this weak against a stronger opponent. Minimizing losses with trash hands is crucial in high-level play. This is a clear fold.

I am in the Small Blind with JTo. I initially completed the small blind for 50 chips, but Minni in the Big Blind has raised to 700. Claude has folded. Minni's stack is relatively short at 72.75 big blinds (7275 chips). A raise to 700 over a limp/complete shows significant strength or an attempt to capitalize on perceived weakness. While JTo is a decent hand, calling 600 more out of position against a player who has shown aggression (Minni frequently raises pre-flop in previous hands) is marginal. However, the pot odds are roughly 900:600 (1.5:1), which isn't great for being out of position. Given the tournament context and the need to protect my stack, I will fold and wait for a better spot rather than playing a bloated pot out of position with a dominated hand.

I am on the button with 3s 4c, which is a very weak starting hand. While I have the chip lead and positional advantage, this hand lacks the equity to justify a raise or a call against the blinds, especially since Claude has shown aggression in recent hands. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack.

I have 8♠5♥ — a weak, unsuited, disconnected hand. Only Claude (BB) is left and it’s cheap to see a flop, so I’ll complete the blind rather than build a bigger pot with a marginal holding. With deep stacks I can comfortably fold to strong aggression post‑flop if I miss, or continue if I hit a pair/draw. Raising here would risk bloating the pot with a dominated hand, so I call the additional 50 (100 total) to see the flop and reevaluate.

With 6-8 offsuit in the big blind facing a single opponent (Minni) who just called the small blind, checking is the optimal play. My hand is weak and doesn't warrant aggressive action. Since Minni merely called (rather than raising), she likely has a marginal holding as well. Getting a free flop allows me to realize my equity cheaply and make decisions based on the board texture. If I flop a pair, straight draw, or other improving holdings, I can proceed; if I miss badly, I can fold. This is a positional advantage on the flop, and the stack sizes don't warrant a pre-flop raise with this weak hand.

I have 8♠5♥ on T♥ 7♠ A♥ — no pair and only very remote backdoor outs (would need both turn and river hearts or very specific two-card runouts for a straight). I limped preflop so my range is weak and a c-bet here is unlikely to fold much of Claude’s continuing range (Ax, Tx, medium pairs, or one-card draws); Claude has shown a tendency to defend/call rather than fold. Out of position and with deep stacks, I want to control the pot and preserve chips for better spots. Check to see a free turn; if Claude checks back I can evaluate again, if he bets I’ll fold to realistic pressure and only continue with a clear improvement (pair/strong draw).

With 6-8o on a Th-7s-Ah flop, I'm holding a weak hand with only a gutshot straight draw (4 outs to make a nine). Minni checked from the small blind, indicating either weakness or a trapping line with a stronger hand.

Given my weak holding and the board texture, betting here is inadvisable. I'd be applying pressure with a hand that has limited equity against most of Minni's calling range. A bet gets called by many hands that beat me (any ace, ten, seven, or face cards) and potentially folds hands I have some equity against.

Checking allows me to realize my equity cheaply, see another card, and make better decisions on the turn if I hit my straight or if the board changes favorably. With 14,500 chips and a heads-up situation where my hand doesn't warrant aggression, taking a free card is the optimal play. This preserves chips and allows me to proceed based on turn development.

Check. The turn pairs my 8 (I now have second pair) but I’m out of position, the pot is tiny (200) and Claude has shown a tendency to defend/call rather than fold. Many hands that beat me (Ax, Tx) are in his continuing range and a c‑bet here will mostly get called rather than fold better hands. I want pot control and to keep the field honest — check to induce bluffs and keep the river decision cheap. If Claude fires a small turn bet (≈≤1/3–1/2 pot) I’ll call to see the river; if he barrels large I’ll fold to avoid bloating the pot with a vulnerable second pair. If he checks back I’ll re‑evaluate on the river and only invest more with improvement or a clear bluff catcher/plan.

Betting 150 on the turn with a pair of eights. Minni has shown weakness with consecutive checks (flop and turn), and she's short-stacked at 8,075. My pair is likely ahead of her checking range. This value bet applies appropriate pressure while keeping the pot manageable and allowing her to make a mistake if she calls with a weaker hand or folds.

I have paired my 8 (8♠5♥) on the turn but it’s a weak second pair with a poor kicker on an A‑T‑7‑8 board. Claude checked the flop and now fires a small turn bet (150 into 350, ≈43% pot). Given his tendency to defend/call wide, this sizing looks like a probe that can include lots of worse pairs, floats and some bluffs as well as value A/T hands. Raising OOP with a vulnerable hand and no real draw would be over-aggressive and likely gets called by better hands; folding is too weak given the small bet and the chance he’s betting a wide, non‑value range. I’ll call 150 to keep the pot controlled, see the river, and fold to large river aggression if I don’t improve. If I pair up or he shows weakness on the river I can proceed; otherwise I’ll preserve chips.

I have 8♠5♥ — second pair (eights) with a poor kicker on T♥‑7♠‑A♥‑8♣‑9♦. The pot is only 500 and there is no river bet to call. Claude’s small turn bet (150) and earlier lines indicate a fairly wide, calling/defending range that contains lots of Ax, Tx, medium pairs, floats and some bluff/semibluff combos. The 9 on the river also completes obvious straight combinations (J or 6), so a number of hands now beat me. Betting or raising OOP with this vulnerable second pair has very little fold equity and will often get called by better hands (and occasionally by worse), so it’s a negative‑EV way to grow the pot. I want to control the pot and realize my equity cheaply — check to induce a bluff or get to showdown; if Claude then bets a small amount I can call occasionally, but I’ll fold to large river aggression. Action: check to preserve chips and avoid bloating the pot with a marginal hand.

I hold a 10-high straight (6-7-8-9-10), which is a very strong holding on this board. Minni has shown consistent weakness throughout the hand with multiple checks and is now short-stacked at 7,925. Her river check suggests she has a marginal hand with no further aggressive intent. My straight beats all pairs, two-pair holdings, and most of her likely range. Betting 250 extracts value from hands like ace-high, weak pairs, or missed draws while being reasonable relative to her stack depth. This is a standard value bet with a strong made hand against a weak, passive opponent.

The river bet is small (250 into 750 → call 250 to win 1000, ~25% pot odds). I have second pair (8s) with a terrible kicker on A‑T‑7‑8‑9. Lots of hands beat me (any A, any T, 9x and straights with J/6), but Claude has shown a tendency to defend/call and his line (check flop, small turn stab, small river bet) looks like a thin value/blocked-value range mixed with occasional bluffs. Raising OOP with this vulnerable hand is negative EV and folding outright would surrender too much given the pot odds. I’ll call the 250 to realize equity and get to showdown; I’ll fold to any large shove but not to this modest probe.

I have A♦K♦ on the button — a premium, highly playable hand in position against the small and big blinds. With effective stacks (~75–170 BB) and only two opponents, I want to build the pot and isolate while retaining postflop maneuverability. A standard 3xBB open to 300 applies pressure to the blinds, gets value from worse Ax/Kx and suited connectors, and keeps the pot size reasonable if I face a 3-bet. Plan postflop: continuation bet most favorable flops, play aggressively when I connect (top pair/strong draws), and re-evaluate if I face significant 3-betting aggression.

Folding Q7o from the small blind against Minni's raise. This is a marginal hand with poor equity and I would be out of position post-flop. While I'm getting pot odds of roughly 1.8:1 (450 to call 250), Q7 offsuit has insufficient equity against Minni's typical raising range from the button. The combination of weak hand strength, positional disadvantage, and risk of getting into marginal situations makes folding the correct play to preserve my 15050 chip stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Deuce suited. Minni has raised to 300 (3x) from the Button. While I have decent pot odds to call (200 to win 450), T2s is a very weak hand to defend out of position. It frequently gets dominated by Minni's button-opening range, and the deuce kicker makes it difficult to play post-flop even if I pair my ten. Given the tournament status and the need to maximize my stack over 150 hands, folding here is the most disciplined play to avoid losing more chips in a marginal situation.

7c5c is a weak hand with no redeeming qualities - it's not connected enough to justify play, even from the button. While I have positional advantage with two relatively short-stacked opponents (Minni with 7,725 and Flash with 17,075), the hand itself is too marginal. Seven-five offsuit will struggle significantly in virtually any flop scenario, and calling 100 to win 150 (3:1 odds) doesn't provide sufficient equity for such a weak holding. I can fold here and wait for stronger spots where my button position will be more valuable. This is a disciplined fold of a speculative hand.

I have Ace-Queen offsuit in the Small Blind. This is a very strong hand, especially in a three-handed game. Claude has already folded from the button. I am facing the Big Blind, Minni, who has a shorter stack of 7725. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and take control of the hand, rather than just calling the additional 50.

Flash opened to 300 from the SB and the button folded. I’m in the BB with K♠2♠. Heads-up vs an opener in a 3-handed game, K2s is worth defending: it blocks some Kx combos, has flush potential and decent post‑flop playability. Pot odds are favorable (call 200 to win ~400), stacks are deep (~77bb) so I can see a flop and make informed decisions rather than committing with a light 3‑bet that’s often dominated. I’ll call and evaluate on the flop.

I have the Queen of Hearts, giving me the second-nut flush draw on a monotone board. Combined with my Ace-high, I have significant equity. I will lead with a continuation bet of 350 to maintain the initiative I established pre-flop and to protect against hands that might have a single lower heart or to fold out air. If called, I have plenty of outs to improve on the turn.

Flop: Jh 9h 3h. I hold K♠2♠ — no pair but a King blocker and backdoor spade potential. Flash opened from the SB and now makes a medium c-bet (350 into the ~600 pot). In 3-handed SB opening ranges are wide and this bet size is consistent with a high-frequency continuation bet that contains a lot of missed hands/bluffs plus some completed heart flushes. Pot odds to call are ~36.8% (350 to win 950), and with my hand vs a wide c-betting range my showdown equity is around that threshold. Stacks are deep (~75bb), so calling is cheap and lets me realize equity and keep weaker hands/bluffs in. Raising here would be a risky pure bluff on a three‑heart board (he folds only some hands but rarely his made hearts) and folding outright is too tight given the price. Plan: call the 350 to see the turn, then fold to significant aggression unless I pick up meaningful equity (a spade or a King) or can credibly bluff later.

I have the second-nut flush with the Queen of Hearts on a board that now shows four hearts. The only hand that currently beats me is the King of Hearts. Given the action so far, it's unlikely Minni is holding specifically the Kh, though it's possible. I will place a value bet of 600 into the 1300 pot to extract value from lower flushes (like the 10h or 7h) or potentially a straight or set that might be curious. This bet is large enough to get value but small enough to not immediately scare off everything but the nuts.

Board: J♥ 9♥ 3♥ 8♥, my hand K♠2♠. Flash opened from SB, c‑bet on flop and barreled the turn (600 into a 1900 pot). I’m facing 600 to call to win 1900 (need ≈24% equity). My raw outs are only the three Ks and three 2s (6 outs → 6/46 ≈ 13%), and two of those outs (K♥,2♥) are hearts that would give a heart‑holder a flush — so my effective equity is even lower. With four hearts on board his line heavily favors a made flush; I cannot realistically outdraw a flush on the river and raising would be a huge, thin bluff that’s unlikely to fold enough of his value range. Fold and preserve stack for a better spot.

I am on the button with Jack-Seven offsuit. While it is tempting to open a wide range in a three-handed game, J7o is a weak hand that often leads to difficult post-flop situations against active players like Minni and Claude. Given my comfortable stack lead, I will choose the disciplined path and fold this hand to wait for better opportunities.

I have A♠ J♣ in the small blind and it's heads-up vs Claude (big blind). A‑J is a strong heads‑up hand and I want to seize the initiative rather than limp/call out of position. I will raise to 300 total (≈3× the BB) — a standard open size at this table — to deny Claude good pot odds to see a cheap flop, build the pot with a premium hand, and gain fold equity. If called I'll continuation bet many favorable flops and evaluate further aggression; if Claude 3‑bets large I'll re‑evaluate (likely fold or call depending on sizing and range), but 300 keeps the pot manageable versus his deep stack while maximizing immediate EV.

This is 8-2 offsuit, the worst hand in poker. While I'm getting 3:1 odds (400 in pot, 200 to call), the hand is fundamentally unplayable against Minni's raise from the small blind. With 14,950 chips still in my stack, I have no need to take marginal spots with such a weak hand. Minni's raise action suggests strength, and there's no fold equity value or positive expected value to gain here. The correct play is to fold and preserve my chips for better opportunities.

I’m on the button with K♠J♣ (a strong, positionally advantaged hand) and stacks ~73 BB. The pot is 150 and it costs 100 to play; min-raise is 200 but I want to take the initiative and apply fold equity. I’ll raise to 300 total (3x the big blind) to isolate the blinds, deny good odds for loose calls, and play the hand in position. Against a large 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate (often fold to very large 3-bets, call small–medium 3-bets to use position postflop). If called, I’ll c-bet favorable flops and look to realize equity when I hit a K or J.

I'm holding pocket 9s from the small blind against Minni's button raise. This is a clear 3-bet spot. Pocket 9s is a strong medium pair that plays well when I take the initiative. 

Key factors:
1. Minni has been aggressively raising from late position throughout this tournament, likely with a wide range
2. My stack (14,900) is healthy, and Minni's stack (6,975) is significantly shorter, making them vulnerable to pressure
3. Position-wise, reraising gets me back to positional advantage post-flop or wins the pot now
4. Pocket 9s is ideally played aggressively pre-flop to define ranges and build the pot when I'm ahead or at parity
5. A 3-bet to 800 gives solid sizing (3x Minni's raise with some extra) and puts real pressure on both opponents

This is the standard strong play - I'm not calling out of position with a medium pair. I'm taking control with a properly sized reraise that benefits from Minni's shorter stack situation.

I have Ace-Queen offsuit in the Big Blind. Minni opened to 300 from the Button, and Claude 3-bet to 800 from the Small Blind. AQo is a strong hand, but being out of position against a 3-bet in a three-way dynamic requires caution. However, folding is too tight given the stack depths and the strength of the hand. Calling allows me to see a flop while keeping the pot manageable, although it caps my range. 4-betting is an option to exert pressure, but Claude's 3-bet from the SB into two players (the opener and the BB) usually indicates a strong range. I will call to see a flop and re-evaluate.

I opened to 300 on the button with K♠J♣ and Claude (SB) 3‑bet to 800 with Flash (BB) cold‑calling. I’ll call the extra 500 (800 total). Rationale: KJ offsuit is playable in position and I want to use my positional advantage postflop against two opponents. The 3‑bet size is moderate; I’m not strong enough to 4‑bet for value and a 4‑bet bluff will often still be called by the BB, creating a bloated multiway pot. Calling 500 into a 1900 pot (~20.8% immediate pot odds) is a cheap way to see a flop and realize equity. Postflop plan: c‑bet favorable boards (Kx, Jx, or good draw flops) for value/fold equity, exercise pot control on dry boards, fold to heavy aggression when I miss, and extract value when I hit top pair or better. Call to 800 total.

Checking the dry Kd 7s 6d flop with pocket 9s. The King is an overcard that hurts my perceived range after 3-betting pre-flop. Acting first out of position, a check is prudent - it avoids overcommitting with a vulnerable hand and allows me to see what my opponents do. This preserves equity and keeps the pot manageable while I gather more information. If checked around, I get a free look at the turn. If someone bets, I can re-evaluate based on bet size and stack considerations.

On a King-high board, the pre-flop 3-better (Claude) has checked. While the King usually hits a 3-betting range, being in a three-way pot changes the dynamic. My Ace-Queen offsuit has missed the flop, and I have no real draw. I should check to see how the button (Minni) reacts. Leading into the original aggressor and another player without a pair or a strong draw is unnecessarily risky. I'm looking to realize whatever showdown value I have or potentially catch an Ace or Queen on the turn for free.

I have top pair (K♠) with a good kicker (J♣) on K♦‑7♠‑6♦ and I’m in position. Both opponents checked, so this is a good spot to c‑bet for value and protection — there are multiple straight/diamond draw combos that I want to charge and deny free cards to. The pot is 2400; I’ll size a value/protection c‑bet to ~½ pot (1200) to get value from worse Kx, Jx and medium pairs while folding out or charging many draws. If I face a large raise (pot‑sized or bigger) I’ll re‑evaluate and lean to folding this vulnerable one‑pair hand; I’ll call smaller raises and continue using position on later streets.

Raising to 3,600 on the flop with pocket 9s against Minni's short stack (only 4,075 remaining after their bet). The King is an overcard but my pocket pair is likely ahead of most hands in Minni's aggressive range. This raise applies significant pressure on both opponents: Minni faces a tough call for their tournament life, and Flash must decide whether to get involved for 3,600 from the big blind. The play controls the pot, prevents Flash from squeezing me with position, and wins the hand immediately if both fold. Tournament-optimal aggression against a vulnerable short-stacked opponent trying to build my stack.

I currently hold Ace-Queen high on a King-high board (Kd-7s-6d). After checking the flop, Minni bet 1200 and Claude check-raised to 3600. Claude's check-raise into two players on this texture strongly indicates a powerful hand like a set, two pair, or a very strong King (like AK). My Ace-high has very little equity against Claude's range and I am likely drawing to only three Aces, which may not even be clean outs if Claude has AK or 77/66. Given the strength shown by both opponents, especially the check-raise, I must fold.

I have top pair K (K♠J♣) with a decent kicker on K♦‑7♠‑6♦ and I'm in position. Claude's line (3‑bet preflop → check‑raise flop to 3,600) is strong and polarized: it contains sets (66/77), strong Kx (AK/KQ) and some two‑card diamond semi‑bluffs. Folding is too passive — KJ IP still beats many bluffs/weak Kx — and shoving is too thin (I don't expect enough folds and will be called by many better Kx/sets). Calling 2,400 more (matching his 3,600 flop wager; total on flop = 3,600) keeps the pot controlled, uses my positional advantage, and gives me a chance to re‑evaluate on the turn. Pot odds are attractive (2,400 to win ~9,600 ≈ 4:1), so I’ll call and plan to fold to extreme turn aggression or continue for value if the turn is safe and villain checks or gives a weak reaction.

Checking on the turn with pocket 9s against a nearly all-in short stack. I've already applied maximum pressure with my flop 3,600 raise. The turn 5h is a blank. By checking, I invite Minni to shove their remaining 2,875, and I'll call with my medium pair getting excellent pot odds (roughly 4:1). This maximizes my chip gain while maintaining reasonable equity against their compressed range. The 5h doesn't significantly change dynamics for either hand, so no need to bet again here.

I have top pair (K♠) with a good kicker (J♣) but the pot is already huge (9,600) and my remaining stack is relatively small (2,875). Claude’s line (preflop 3‑bet then a large check‑raise on the flop) is very strong and is polarized toward sets (66/77), strong Kx (AK/KQ) and some two‑diamond semi‑bluffs. The turn 5♥ doesn’t remove many of Claude’s value hands and does introduce a few straight combinations, and he now checks — which looks like pot‑control with a strong hand or a disguised value hand waiting for me to act. Betting here would risk committing my stack against many hands that beat me (sets and strong Kx) for only thin value; a small bet could be raised and put me in an awkward all‑in decision. I prefer pot control: check the turn, see the river, and re‑evaluate. If Claude barrels a big river bet I’ll fold; if he checks or gives a small/weak action on a blank river I can extract value or call accordingly.

River check with pocket nines against short-stacked Minni on board K♦7♠6♦5♥5♦. My pair of nines has showdown value against most hands in Minni's compressed range (lower pairs, missed draws, weak broadway). Checking allows Minni to shove with their remaining 2,875 chips if desired, and I'll call with strong pot odds (~4.3:1). This maximizes expected value by getting Minni to commit while maintaining showdown strength. Tournament-optimal play to secure chips against a vulnerable opponent.

Claude 3‑bet preflop and then check‑raised my flop c‑bet very large (to 3,600) — his line is polarized toward sets (66/77), strong Kx (AK/KQ) and a few semi‑bluffs. I called in position to keep control and re‑evaluate. The turn 5♥ and the river pairing the board with 5♦ both increase the number of hands that now beat my one pair (trips with any 5x, improved two‑pair with 6x/7x, and strong Kx still in his range). The pot is already 9,600 and my remaining stack is only 2,875, so a river bet is thin for value (few worse Kx will call) and risks a raise that commits me with a marginal hand. Checking to showdown preserves my stack, avoids getting raised/committed by a polarized range, and gives me the best chance to win when Claude has a weaker K or a missed draw. I check.

Calling from the button with Js6s. Position is crucial in 3-handed poker, and at only 100 chips (less than 1% of my stack) to see a flop with position advantage, this is a reasonable investment. I can leverage post-flop positional play to win the pot if the board allows, or fold if it misses me badly. The suited broadway card gives some additional value.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Seven offsuit. The button has limped into the pot for 100. With a very good price of 50 chips to call into a pot that will be 300, I will complete the bet to see a flop. However, I must play cautiously post-flop as this hand is easily dominated and I will be out of position for the rest of the hand.

Facing two limpers (SB and Button) into my big blind with 4♦6♣ — a weak, low-equity hand. Pot is only 300 and I can see the flop for free; with deep stacks I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal holding. I’ll check to see a flop and only continue if I pair or pick up a clear straight/flush draw. If I face sizable aggression on the flop without improvement I’ll fold; if I improve I’ll extract value or turn the hand into a bluff in the right spots. Raising here would risk chips unnecessarily against wide calling ranges, so check and evaluate postflop.

I am in the Small Blind in a limped pot with Jack-Seven offsuit. The flop of K-T-4 rainbow does not connect with my hand in any significant way; I have no pair and no straight or flush draws. Since I am out of position against two opponents, and the board texture heavily favors a typical calling/limping range (which often includes Broadway cards like Kings and Tens), I will check. My goal is to see a cheap turn or fold if a significant bet is made, as I have zero equity here.

I checked preflop to see the flop and I’ve just paired the board with 4♦ (bottom pair). Board is K♥ T♦ 4♠ — two overcards that beat me and no strong draws to protect against. Pot is only 300 and both opponents limped (wide ranges). Against two players and with deep stacks, betting here bloats the pot with a vulnerable hand and will often get called by better hands or floats. I’ll control the pot and induce action by checking. Plan: call a small, non‑committal bet to see the turn, but fold to sizable aggression since my pair is easily dominated; if checked around I’ll go to showdown and re-evaluate on the turn.

Both opponents checked the flop, indicating weakness. With Js6s, I have a spade flush draw (9 outs) and a gutshot straight draw (4 additional outs) for approximately 12-13 improving outs and ~50% equity. Checking allows me to see the turn card for free while keeping opponents in the hand. If a spade hits, I'll have a much stronger hand. If a blank hits and they bet, I can fold and minimize losses. This is superior to betting now with a marginal hand that could fold out weaker holdings.

I have turned second pair (Jacks) on a King-high board. While the Jack of Hearts adds a backdoor flush possibility to the board, my Jack of Diamonds provides me with some showdown value. The pot is still very small (300) and both opponents checked the flop and have checked to me on the turn. Since I have a pair now, checking is a safe option to see the river and potentially catch a bluff or win at showdown, but I also want to protect my hand or get value from smaller pairs/straight draws (like 9-8 or Q-x). However, in a limped pot, checking through to control the pot size and realize equity is often the most prudent play, especially since I'm out of position.

Check. I have 4♦6♣ — bottom pair on K♥ T♦ 4♠ J♥. Pot is tiny (300) and both opponents limped wide preflop and have checked the flop/turn, so their ranges contain lots of Ks/Ts/Jx, Q9 straights, and broadway holdings that beat me. Betting bloats the pot with a very vulnerable hand and will often get called or raised by better hands; checking controls the pot and keeps weaker hands/bluffs in. Plan: check and see the river; call a small, non‑committal bet to chase a cheap river if the price is right, but fold to sizeable aggression or a raise since my pair is frequently second/third best. If checked through, show down and re‑evaluate on the river.

After the flop was checked through with marginal holdings, the turn Jh significantly improves my hand to top pair. Both opponents checked again, indicating weakness. Betting 200 (2/3 pot) extracts value from weaker hands and draws while building the pot for my additional outs (flush draw, gutshot straight draw, trips). Top pair on a coordinated board against checking opposition is a strong value-betting opportunity in this spot.

I have turned second pair with my Jack of Diamonds. In a limped pot where the flop was checked around, a pair of Jacks is a relatively strong hand. Claude's bet of 200 into 300 on the turn is likely a probe bet or a thin value bet with a Ten or a Jack. Since the board is somewhat coordinated (straight draws possible with a Queen or Nine), I will call to see the river and evaluate the final board, as folding second pair for this price in a limped pot is too conservative.

I have 4♦6♣ on K♥ T♦ 4♠ J♥ — just bottom pair with a 6 kicker and only two clean outs (the two remaining 4s). Claude bets 200 and Flash calls; the pot is 700 and I must call 200 to see the river (call 200 to win ~900 → ~22.2% equity required). My actual equity is far lower: ~4.3% to make trips on the river and only limited showdown equity when facing a bet/raise from two opponents. The line (raiser + caller) strongly suggests value holdings (Kx/TX/Jx or better) rather than a double bluff. Raising here with bottom pair would be reckless multi‑way. I’ll preserve chips and fold rather than paying 200 for a very low‑EV shot.

On the river, the board completes with the 3 of hearts. I currently have second pair (Jacks) with a weak kicker (7). The board is quite connected, with possibilities for straights (AQ, Q9) and now a back-door flush, although the flush is less likely given the turn action. In a limped pot, my pair of Jacks has decent showdown value, but it is not strong enough to bet for value, as I am unlikely to be called by many worse hands. Checking is the most appropriate action here to either see a cheap showdown if Claude checks back or to evaluate a potential bet from him based on sizing.

Betting 300 on the river with top pair (Jacks). Flash checked, indicating weakness throughout the hand (checked flop, called turn, checked river). This is a clean value situation against an opponent who has shown no strength. A bet of ~40% of the current pot is standard for extracting value from marginal hands while maintaining fold equity against complete whiffs.

I have second pair (Jacks) on a board that is somewhat coordinated. On the river, the 3 of hearts is relatively blank, though it completes a backdoor flush. In a limped pot, players often check top pair on the flop, but Claude's check on the flop and subsequent bet on the turn when the Jack arrived suggests he might have a Jack or be semi-bluffing with a straight draw (like Qx). My kicker is weak (7), but I am getting excellent pot odds (300 to call for a total pot of 1300, which is better than 4:1). Given these odds and the relatively small bet size, I have enough showdown value to justify a call to see if my Jacks are good against a missed draw or a weaker Jack.

I am on the button with 8-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Despite being in a 3-handed game where ranges are wider, there is no strategic value in playing this hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot to maintain my stack.

Heads-up vs Claude in the big blind with Q♠5♦. In heads-up play this is a playable hand and I want to take the initiative rather than let him see a flop for free. I’ll open-raise to 200 total (2xBB) — a standard, economical-sized raise that pressures his defending range while keeping the pot manageable with a mediocre offsuit queen. If he folds I pick up the blinds; if he calls I’ll continuation-bet favorable textures; if he 3-bets large I’ll often fold Q5o given poor equity. Amount is total chips committed (includes my posted 50).

Minni's min-raise from the small blind is a clear weakness indicator. KQ is a premium hand with excellent equity against their likely marginal range. The 3:1 pot odds are too favorable to pass, and I maintain position advantage postflop. This is a straightforward value call against a weaker hand.

I opened to 200 preflop with Q♠5♦ and Claude just called from the big blind. The flop K♦ A♣ 2♥ is very unfavorable for my hand: two overcards (A and K) that are well within his defending range and I have no pair or meaningful backdoor equity. This is not a good texture to continuation-bet — a c-bet will be thin and will frequently be called or raised by hands that dominate me (Ax, Kx, pairs). I’ll check to control the pot (current pot 400) and see what Claude does. If he leads out with a meaningful bet I’ll fold—my showdown equity is too poor to continue; if he checks back I get a free turn to try to improve (and can value-bet or bluff later only if I actually make a pair or hit a very favorable card).

Minni's check on the KdAc2h flop after min-raising preflop is a classic weakness indicator. I have KQ which gives me top pair with an excellent kicker on this board. While the Ace is concerning, Minni's passive check suggests they don't have a strong hand. 

I'm betting 250 (approximately 60% of the 400-chip pot) to:
1. Build value from Minni's weaker holdings (QJ, QT, small pairs, AJ/AT)
2. Apply pressure and define my hand strength
3. Maintain my initiative - I'm in position and have the aggression
4. Protect my hand against Minni catching an overcard on the turn

This is a standard value bet with a hand that's currently ahead on a flop where my opponent has shown weakness. The bet size is neither too timid nor overcommitting, allowing me to continue playing the hand profitably regardless of Minni's response.

I checked the K♦ A♣ 2♥ flop with Q♠5♦ to control the pot; Claude now bets 250 into the 400 pot. My hand has almost no showdown value and is dominated by the parts of his calling range (Ax, Kx, pairs, broadways). To call 250 I need ~27.8% equity; I have at best ~24% to make at least a pair by the river (3 queens + 3 fives), and many of those pairs still lose to Ax/Kx or two-pair. A bluff-raise here would be thin and likely to be called or get me in trouble out of position. With deep stacks and many hands left, preserving chips and waiting for a better spot is correct — fold.

I have Q♠3♣ on the button. Pot is 150 and it costs 100 to see the flop. Q3o is a marginal hand and often dominated preflop; raising here would invite 3-bets from aggressive opponents (Flash and Claude) and put me in difficult spots. Calling is cheap, keeps me in position to see a flop and realize equity; I can proceed if I hit (top pair/weak pair or good runout), and fold to heavy pressure when I miss. Prefer to preserve stack and use position rather than open-raise this marginal holding.

Folding 6-4 offsuit in the small blind. This is the weakest hand in poker, and I'm out of position with two opponents still in the hand (Minni called 100 from the button and Flash is in the big blind). While I've already posted 50 as the small blind and only need to call 50 more, proper bankroll management means avoiding marginal situations with trash hands. Even though the immediate odds are favorable (5:1), the hand is too weak to profitably play out of position. Minni's call suggests a wide range, but any flop improvement is unlikely with 6-4o, and I'll be vulnerable to aggression. This is a clear fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 9c 7d. Minni (the Button) has limped in for 100, and Claude (the Small Blind) has folded. The action is now on me. Since I am already in the hand for the big blind amount and there is no raise, I will check to see the flop for free with my medium-weak hand.

I have 9-high on a very dry, paired board (Ah-Jc-Ac). I have no equity and no real potential for a draw. Since I am out of position against Minni, I will check to see how she reacts. There is no reason to put chips into the pot with this hand.

Flop is Ah Jc Ac and I hold Q♠3♣ — no pair and a weak kicker. This board massively favors ranges that called/checked preflop (any Ace, Jx, or broadway K/Q hands) so I have very little equity and am easily dominated. Betting here would be a risky bluff against a deep-stacked opponent who can comfortably call or raise with hands that crush me. I’ll check to control the pot and avoid committing chips; plan is to fold to any meaningful bet and only continue to very small probes or if I improve on the turn (evaluate if I pair the Q or pick up a backdoor draw).

On a board of Ah-Jc-Ac-9h, I have turned a pair of nines. While this gives me some showdown value, the board is still dangerous with two Aces and a Jack present. Minni limped pre-flop and checked back the flop, suggesting she likely doesn't have a monster, but she could easily have a Jack or be waiting to bluff. I will check to keep the pot small and see the river, as my hand is now a decent bluff-catcher in a limped pot.

I have Q♠3♣ with no pair and no meaningful draw on Ah Jc Ac 9h. Flash checked flop and checked the turn, which makes it less likely he has an Ace (those would often be bet for value); he may have a jack, a small pair, or be floating. I have poor blockers and very little fold equity, so trying to bluff here is thin and likely to be called or raised by better hands. The pot is small (250) and stacks are deep enough that calling/raising risks unnecessary chips. I’ll check to see the river for free and fold to any meaningful bet — only continue if I pair or pick up a clear improvement.

The board is Ah-Jc-Ac-9h-9d. My hole cards are 9c-7d. My best five-card hand is a full house: Nines full of Aces (9-9-9-A-A). On this board, anyone with an Ace has a better full house (Aces full of Nines), and anyone with a Nine has the same full house (Nines full of Aces). If the opponent has neither, their best hand is the two pair on the board (A-A-9-9) with a Jack kicker. Since Minni limped pre-flop and checked the flop and turn, it is unlikely she holds an Ace. I will place a value bet, hoping to be called by a Jack or a player who thinks I am bluffing a missed draw. If she also has a Nine, we will split the pot as the kicker does not play with this specific full house.

I hold Q♠3♣ on a river of A♥ J♣ A♣ 9♥ 9♦. I have no pair and the five-card board already gives two pair (Aces and Nines) to everyone — my hand has essentially zero showdown value unless Flash is bluffing. Flash checked flop and turn then made a small river bet (150 into a 400 pot). Calling 150 to win 550 requires ~27% bluff frequency to be correct. Given the line and that thin value (A or J) or a slowplayed Ace/full-house candidate are very plausible, I’m not getting enough confidence in a bluff here. There’s no realistic improvement for me and raising would be reckless (no blockers, likely to be crushed). I fold and preserve chips for better spots.
Calling 100 with 9-5 suited on the button. Position is valuable, stack depths are deep, and the hand has playability post-flop with flush potential. Cheap way to see a flop from late position.

I am in the Small Blind with J4o. Claude has limped from the button, and it costs me 50 more to call. While the pot odds are 5:1, J4o is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Given the stack sizes and the likelihood of being outplayed post-flop, folding is the disciplined choice to avoid leaking chips with marginal hands.

Heads-up after Claude limped on the button and SB folded. I have 6♣3♦ — a very weak, offsuit hand with poor equity and I'm out of position postflop. Pot is 250 and there's no additional money to put in preflop, so a limp-raise here would be thin and unnecessarily bloating the pot early in the tournament. I will check and see a flop; only continue if I hit a meaningful pair or a strong draw, otherwise fold to significant aggression to protect my stack.

I have 6♣3♦ on J♣7♦3♠ — I’ve paired my 3 but it’s a very marginal, vulnerable pair with a weak kicker and I’m out of position. Claude limped preflop so his range is wide (Jx, overcards, small pairs, suited connectors). There’s no need to bloat the pot with a thin c-bet on this board. I’ll check to control the pot, see what he does, and only commit more chips if I get good odds or improve. Plan: call small-to-medium bets to see a turn (roughly up to ~1/2 pot); fold or heavily re-evaluate to pot-sized bets or raises since my pair is easily dominated.

With 9c 5c on Jc 7d 3s, I have only a backdoor flush draw and weak high card. Minni checked from the big blind, indicating she may not have a strong holding either. Taking a free card to the turn is optimal here - I can evaluate my hand strength further when I have more information. Pot control is critical with marginal equity, and betting would only get called by better hands or win a pot I already have a chance to see cheaply. Checking preserves chips while keeping the option to continue if I improve.

I have 6♣3♦ on J♣7♦3♠9♦ — a weak, vulnerable pair (trip blocker is irrelevant) with a poor kicker and I’m out of position versus a limp/caller. Claude checked the flop and again on the turn, showing a wide but generally passive range (overcards, small pairs, some Jx, occasional draws). Betting OOP here would bloat the pot with a marginal holding and expose me to raises from better hands (Jx, 9x, T8 straights, pocket pairs). I’ll check to control the pot and realize my equity. Plan: call small-to-medium bets (~≤1/2 pot ≈125) to see a river, fold to pot-sized bets or raises, and re-evaluate if checked through to the river (look to bluff or thin-value depending on the runout).

Betting 150 on the turn with a pair of nines. Minni's three consecutive checks signal weakness. As the button with position and a made hand, value betting is optimal here. This sizing applies pressure, likely wins the pot immediately, or extracts value if called. The bet sets up a manageable river scenario with a balanced line.

I have 6♣3♦ and paired the 3 on the flop (board Jc 7d 3s 9d). Claude limp‑called preflop and checked the flop, so his range is wide and often contains overcards and small/medium pairs. His turn bet of 150 into the 250 pot (pot now 400) means a call of 150 is asking for ~27% equity (150 / (400+150) ≈ 27%). Though I only have two clean outs to hit trips on the river (~4.3%), my current pair is still ahead of a large portion of his checking/limping range (overcards, many singletons, some small pairs), so I get the required pot odds to continue. Raising here would be thin and would bloat the pot out of position with a marginal hand; folding would concede too much equity for a relatively small price. Plan: call 150 to see the river and fold to a large river raise/bet unless I improve to trips or get a cheap river showdown.

I have 6♣3♦ — a very small pair (pair of 3s) on J♣ 7♦ 3♠ 9♦ 2♠. Claude limp‑called preflop, checked the flop and then made a smallish turn bet (150 into a ~250 pot) which he had called. That line represents a wide, mostly medium‑strength range (Jx, 9x, small pairs, occasional bluffs). My hand is vulnerable: I beat many unpaired overcards but lose to J/9/7 and any made two‑pair/sets or the rare T8 straight. Betting for thin value here risks getting called or raised by hands that already beat me while folding out worse hands I want to extract from. Pot control is the priority — I’ll check to keep the pot manageable and take a cheap showdown. If Claude leads into me I can call small‑to‑medium bets for pot‑odds (≈≤1/2 pot) and fold to pot‑sized or larger raises.

River value bet with a pair of nines. Minni checked consistently throughout (pre-flop, flop, river) and only called my 150 turn bet without aggression, indicating a weak hand. A pair of 9s likely dominates her range. Betting 250 extracts value from weaker hands while respecting showdown requirements on the river. This sized bet wins chips against her typical weak holdings (Ax, Kx, small pairs) while managing risk if she has an unlikely strong hand.

I will call 250 to see the showdown. I have a marginal pair (3s) on J♣7♦3♠9♦2♠ — vulnerable but ahead of a lot of Claude’s limp/call range (overcards and missed draws). Claude’s line (limp → check flop → small turn bet 150 → small river bet 250 into 800) looks like a wide, mostly medium-strength range that includes many hands I currently beat and some value hands that already beat me (Jx, 9x, 7x). Calling costs 250 to win ~1050 (≈24% equity required), which is within reason given my blockers and the opponent’s small sizing. Raising would be too thin and bloats the pot out of position with a weak kicker; folding concedes too much equity for a small price. Call and take it to showdown.

Holding Jack-Eight of spades on the button in a three-handed game is a standard opening range hand. I will raise to 300 to exert pressure on the blinds and take advantage of my position for the rest of the hand.

3-handed, I’m in the small blind with 7♣6♦ facing a standard button open to 300 (pot ~450, I must call 250; min reraise to 500). 7-6 offsuit is a marginal, easily dominated hand with poor postflop playability out of position. Calling here will often put me OOP against two players (Flash + Claude) and likely be multiway, which reduces my equity further; the immediate pot odds (~35–40% equity required) are not attractive for this hand. I could 3-bet as a light bluff, but 7-6o lacks useful blockers and a 3-bet will frequently be called or 4-bet, leaving me in a bad spot deep-stacked. Prefer to conserve my large stack and wait for a better, more playable spot or position — fold and take my 50 posted blind as the price of a correct fold.

Folding 9c8c out of position against an aggressive button raise. The hand is too marginal when out of position facing a wider range from Flash. While the direct odds are reasonable (2.25:1), the positional disadvantage post-flop is too significant with such a weak holding. Flash can control the action and I'll have to play defensively. Better to preserve chips for premium hands or positions with more equity.

I’m on the button with 6♠3♦ — a weak, unsuited hand but I have positional advantage and the price to see a flop is small (100 into a 150 pot). Raising with this junk risks getting 3-bet or playing a bloated pot out of position; folding surrenders an easy, cheap equity spot. Plan: call 100, see the flop, and fold to meaningful aggression unless I hit a strong pair/draw or realize good equity postflop.

Calling 50 more to complete small blind with K8o. The 6:1 pot odds justify seeing a flop with this marginal hand. If I hit top pair or find a favorable flop development, I can play the hand. If the flop is poor, I can fold and minimize loss. The stack depth supports this standard SB/BB dynamic play.

I am in the big blind with 8c 3c. Both the button (Minni) and the small blind (Claude) have limped in, and I have the option to check to see a free flop. While 8-3 suited has some post-flop potential in a multi-way pot, it is not strong enough to warrant a raise as a bluff or for value against two opponents who have already shown interest in the hand. Checking is the most prudent play.

Betting 150 (50% of 300-chip pot) with top pair on an ace-high flop. Flash's check from BB shows weakness, and I should take the initiative. This bet size gets value from weaker hands while maintaining proper pot control. If called, I'll reassess on the turn. If I check, I'm giving opponents free cards and allowing them to catch up.

I am in the big blind with 8c 3c. The flop is Ac 4h Kd, and the small blind (Claude) has led out with a bet of 150 into a pot of 300. I have no pair and no draw on this very dry board. While I have a backdoor club draw, it is not worth continuing against a lead on an Ace-King high board, especially with another player still to act behind me. I will fold.

I called preflop to see a cheap flop and planned to fold to meaningful aggression unless I connected. On A♣4♥K♦ I have complete air with 6♠3♦ — no pair and no real draws (only six outs to pair). Claude called preflop and now bets 150; Flash folded, so it’s heads-up. Pot is 450; calling 150 to win ~600 requires ~25% equity. My chance to hit by the river is ~24% and even a pair will often be second-best against Claude’s c-bet range (Ax/Kx/strong pairs). A bluff-raise here is unnecessary risk with deep stacks. Fold and preserve the stack for better spots.

Raising pocket 9s on the button in 3-handed play. This is a premium starting hand that warrants aggression from position. A 3x BB raise (300) is standard - strong enough to build the pot when ahead, but not overcommitted pre-flop. The button position gives me control post-flop regardless of how opponents respond.

I have been dealt Pocket Aces, the strongest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Claude has opened with a standard raise from the Button. As the Small Blind, I want to 3-bet to extract value immediately and narrow the field, especially since I'll be out of position for the rest of the hand. A 3.5x raise to 1050 is a standard sizing that builds a significant pot while giving my opponents room to make a mistake by calling or 4-betting.

Facing a button open to 300 and a SB 3-bet to 1050 with 4♣8♥ in the big blind. Pot is 1450 and it costs 950 to call, so break-even equity = 950 / (1450+950) ≈ 39.6%. 4♣8♥ has very poor equity and is heavily dominated by a typical open+3-bet range; it plays badly out of position and offers little postflop potential. A min 4-bet to 1800 as a bluff would be thin and unnecessarily commit chips. Fold and conserve my stack for a better spot.

Calling with pocket 9s (99) in this spot. Analysis: 99 is a premium hand (top 5%) and I have excellent position on the button. Flash's 3-bet from the SB to 1050 (3.5x my initial raise) could indicate a premium hand, but given Flash's aggressive play pattern throughout the tournament, this could also be a light 3-bet with a wider range like AT+, 88+, AJ+, or KQ. 

Against Flash's likely 3-betting range, my 99 has good equity. Calling preserves chips while keeping the hand in play, and my button position gives me a significant post-flop advantage. After calling the 750, I'll have 9700 remaining with position to apply pressure on favorable flops. A 4-bet would commit me too deeply (min-raise to 1800 leaves just 8650) and doesn't gain much since Flash will likely fold lighter hands and go to war with premium holdings anyway. Calling maximizes my flexibility and value extraction post-flop.

I have Pocket Aces on a board of King-Queen-Deuce. While this board hits a 3-bet calling range fairly well (hands like KQ, KJ, QJ, and JT are possible), my Aces are still ahead of almost everything except a set of Kings, Queens, or two pair (KQ). I want to bet for value and to protect my hand against potential straight draws like JT. A bet of around 40% of the pot (900 chips) is a good size to extract value from Top Pair or Middle Pair hands while keeping the pot manageable if Claude has hit something very strong.

Calling with pocket nines (99) on a K-2-Q flop. While the board has hit Broadway combinations, I have decent equity against Flash's likely 3-betting range, which includes both premiums and lighter hands. My button position is critical - I can apply pressure on the turn if a low card comes or if Flash shows weakness by checking. Pot odds of 4.4:1 are acceptable. Calling preserves my 8800 remaining stack and maintains maximum flexibility for the turn while controlling the hand's progression.

The turn brings a second King, pairing the board. My Pocket Aces are now vulnerable to any King in Claude's range (like KJs, KTs, or KQs). While I was ahead on the flop, the board pairing the top card is a significant change. Checking here allows me to control the pot size and see how Claude reacts. If he checks back, I likely still have the best hand against his Queens or mid-pairs. If he bets, I can evaluate based on his sizing, but I generally want to avoid bloating the pot now that I'm potentially behind trips.

Betting 2000 on the turn after Flash checked. Flash's 3-bet range likely includes JJ+, AK, AQ which many beat my 99, but the turn check indicates weakness. As the button with position, I should exploit this by applying pressure rather than giving a free card. The bet is ~50% of the pot, reasonable sizing that gets folds from missed hands while still being manageable relative to my 8800 stack. This maintains initiative and exploits positional advantage.

I am holding Pocket Aces, but the board has become extremely dangerous with the second King appearing on the turn. My check was intended to control the pot size and induce bluffs, as any King in Claude's range now has me beat. Claude's bet of 2000 into a 4000 pot is a polarized sizing. He could be value betting a King (like KJs or KTs) or a full house (KQs), or he could be semi-bluffing with a straight draw like JT or perhaps turning a Queen into a bet for protection. Folding Aces here to a single bet after checking would be too tight and exploitable, especially given the pot odds (3:1). I will call to see the river, but I must be extremely cautious if he continues his aggression on the final street.

The river has completed a board of Kc-2d-Qh-Ks-Qd. This runout is extremely unfavorable for my Pocket Aces. While I hold the best possible non-full house hand (Kings and Queens with an Ace kicker), a significant portion of Claude's range that calls a pre-flop 3-bet and a flop continuation bet has now made a full house. Specifically, hands like KQ, KJ, KT, AQ, QJ, and QT all beat me. The only hands I beat are pocket pairs lower than Queens (like JJ or TT) or missed straight draws like JT. I also chop with any other Ace-high hand like AJ or AT. Betting into this board offers no value, as I will only be called by hands that beat me or hands that I chop with. Checking is the only viable move to reach showdown as cheaply as possible or to potentially catch a bluff from a missed draw, although a bluff is unlikely on such a connected board.

Checking on the river with 99 on a K-K-Q-Q board. Flash's river check indicates weakness after playing aggressively pre-flop and on the flop. My 99 is likely in reasonable shape against his checking range, which likely includes hands like AT, AJ, weaker aces, and lower pairs. Betting here offers limited value (weak hands fold, strong hands call), and with only 6800 chips remaining, it's prudent to take the showdown rather than risk additional chips unnecessarily. Flash's weakness is apparent, and checking allows me to win pots cheaply when ahead.

I have Ace-Ten offsuit on the button in a three-handed game. This is a very strong starting hand in this configuration. I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300 to exert pressure on the blinds and take control of the hand. My stack size allows me to play aggressively against the shorter stacks of Minni and Claude.

Facing a button open to 300 with the pot at 450 and 250 to call. I hold Td7c — a weak, offsuit ten with poor playability out of position. Calling 250 into a 450 pot would make the pot 700, giving me ~2.8:1 odds (need ~26% equity) but I’ll be out of position to the raiser and can also face a defend/3-bet from the big blind (Claude). A min-3bet/isolation to 500 is unjustified with this hand. Against an aggressive button and a capable BB, this is a marginal spot better folded to preserve stack and wait for a cleaner opportunity.

I'm in the Big Blind with 5-7 offsuit, one of the weakest hands in poker. Flash raised to 300 from the button, and Minni folded. While I'm getting 2.25:1 pot odds (450 chips in pot, 200 to call), my hand has minimal equity against Flash's raising range. With no premium strength, poor position post-flop, and a vulnerable hand that won't play well in a heads-up situation, folding is the mathematically correct play. Preserving chips for premium spots is essential in tournament poker, especially with 109 hands remaining.

I'm on the button with K♥T♠ — a playable, broadway hand with good post‑flop playability and position against two blinds. Pot is 150 and it's 100 to call. I prefer to open‑raise here to seize initiative, build fold equity, and avoid playing a multiway pot out of position. A standard 3x BB sizing to 300 accomplishes that: it pressures the blinds (especially the shortish SB) but doesn't overcommit with a marginal hand. Plan: if I'm called I'll use position to c‑bet favorable flops and extract value; if I face a significant 3‑bet from Flash (an aggressive, deep-stacked player) I'll re‑evaluate — fold to very large 3‑bets but consider calling small/standard 3‑bets given deep stacks to see a flop. Raise to 300 to open the pot from the button.

2-10 offsuit is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. While I'm in the small blind with only 250 to call (decent pot odds), this hand has minimal equity and I'd be playing out of position post-flop against Minni's raising range and the big blind still to act. My stack is healthy enough (6650 chips) that I don't need to take marginal spots. This is a straightforward fold, conserving chips for better opportunities. With strong table image and solid positioning through most recent hands, I can afford to be selective.

I am holding 2s 4h, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Minni has raised from the button, and although I am getting pot odds of 3.25 to 1 to call from the big blind, this hand plays very poorly post-flop and I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand. Given my substantial stack lead, there is no reason to defend the blind with trash hands. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable long-term play here.

4-9 offsuit is a weak hand that doesn't warrant playing from the button. While I have positional advantage, the hand quality is below the threshold for profitably engaging in this situation. The pot is only 150 and I would need to call 100, but 4-9o is unlikely to be ahead of either opponent's typical blind range and lacks potential to hit strong combinations. With a moderate stack of 6650 and 106 hands remaining, I should preserve chips by folding marginal hands and wait for stronger holdings in favorable positions. This aligns with the disciplined approach demonstrated in my previous hands where I've been selective with weaker holdings.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Nine of clubs, which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game, especially when the button has folded. Raising allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the pot immediately if the Big Blind folds, or build a pot with a hand that has good post-flop playability and equity.

Facing a 300 open from the small blind heads-up with Jc3d in the big blind: this is a weak offsuit hand but heads-up I should defend a wide range. Pot is 400 and it costs me 200 to call (I’ll have 300 committed total), giving ~33% pot odds to see a flop. 3-betting J3o would be a pure bluff and creates difficult out-of-position decisions with deep stacks; folding is too tight here. I’ll call to see a flop and then proceed cautiously — fold to heavy aggression on dry boards, continue only with a pair (preferably a jack), strong draws, or favorable board texture where I can realize equity or apply pressure.

As the pre-flop aggressor, I have the initiative on this relatively dry flop of Queen-Five-Six. My Ten-Nine of clubs provides me with two overcards to the middle cards of the board and a backdoor flush draw. I will place a standard continuation bet of 300, which represents half the pot, to put pressure on the Big Blind and potentially take the pot down immediately if they haven't connected with the board.

Heads-up vs a small SB c-bet (300 into a ~900 pot) on Q♠5♥6♣ with J♣3♦. Out of position and missed the flop, but the bet is small so defending a wide range is correct — calling costs 300 for ~25% pot odds. I don't have much showdown equity, but I can realize equity, see a cheap turn, and fold to significant turn/river aggression. Raising OOP with J3o is too thin. Plan: call the flop, and fold to large raises or continue only if I pair a Jack/3 or pick up meaningful improvement/draw.

The turn card pairs the top of the board (Queen). This is a card that often favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, but since I have no actual hand or draw with Ten-high, continuing to bet (double-barreling) is a pure bluff. While the paired board makes it less likely Minni has a Queen, her call on the flop suggests she has some piece of the board (like a 5 or 6) or a draw. I will check to see how she reacts. If she checks back, I get a free river card; if she bets, I will likely fold as I have no equity to continue.

Board Q♠ 5♥ 6♣ Q♦ — I hold J♣3♦. I have no pair and only ~6 outs (~13%) to improve to a single pair; many of those still lose to Qx or any paired 5/6. Flash checked the turn after showing earlier aggression, which looks like weakness or pot control. Betting OOP as a thin bluff here is suboptimal — I don’t want to bloat the pot with very poor equity and give him an easy call. Checking preserves chips, gives a free river to try a bluff or realise equity, and keeps options open: fold to sizable river aggression, call small river bets sometimes, or bluff the river only if it’s a clean card and villain shows weakness. Therefore I will check.

The river is a blank (2 of diamonds), and the board is paired with Queens. I have Ten-high, which has almost no showdown value. In the previous street, both Minni and I checked, suggesting she likely has a marginal hand like a small pair (5s or 6s) or Ace-high. Since I represented a strong hand pre-flop and on the flop, I will place a bet of 600 (half the pot) to represent a Queen or a slow-played overpair, attempting to force a fold from her marginal showdown hands.

On this paired board (Qs 5h 6c Qd 2d) I have Jc3d — no pair and no showdown value, but Flash’s river sizing is small: 600 into a 1800 pot. Calling 600 would make the total pot 2400, so I need ~25% equity to break even. Flash’s line (raise pre, c-bet flop, check the turn, small river bet) is consistent with both thin value hands (single Q, small pair) and some thin probe bluffs from missed overcards/Ax-type hands. There aren’t many clean bluffs on this runout, but the tiny sizing means he’s likely targeting thin value rather than polar. Raising OOP with no showdown value is far too thin. Folding denies myself the 25% break-even spot, but calling is cheap enough to catch the occasional bluff and avoid being over-folding to small river bets. I’ll call the 600 to reach showdown — fold only to a significant raise.

In a 3-handed game, opening ranges from the button should be quite wide to put pressure on the blinds. 7c 8s is a decent speculative hand with some connectivity that can be played profitably in position. I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300. If either the small blind or big blind 3-bets, the hand is weak enough to fold comfortably, but if they call, I have the advantage of acting last on all subsequent streets.

Facing a standard button open to 300 while I'm in the small blind with 4♦9♦. This is a weak, low-suited hand with poor playability out of position — I'd be first to act post-flop against the opener (and the big blind still to act now). Calling 250 to see a flop puts me OOP in a multiway/3-handed pot where I'll often be forced to fold to continuation bets. 3-betting as a bluff with 4♦9♦ is too loose here given deep stacks and a competent opener. Preserve chips for better spots — fold.

I'm in the big blind with K♥8♥ facing Flash's 300 raise from the button. After Minni folded, I'm heads-up with the chip leader. My stack is the shortest at 6550, and I need to play proactively while in position post-flop. K♥8♥ is a reasonable holding with multiple ways to improve - I can hit top pair with the King, or make a flush with the heart draw. Flash has been playing aggressively throughout and likely has a wide raising range from the button. The pot odds (450 in the pot, 200 to call) are favorable enough to call and see a flop. Folding would be too passive given my stack situation, while raising would be over-committing with a marginal hand. Calling allows me to keep my chips in play and evaluate my hand strength on the flop.

I'm heads-up with Flash on the flop after calling his 300 pre-flop raise. My hand K♥8♥ on a board of 7♠ 6♥ 8♣ gives me middle pair with solid overcard potential. While I have a decent holding, I'm facing the chip leader in the tournament while short-stacked at 6350.

Checking is the optimal play here for several reasons:
1. **Information gathering**: Flash has been aggressive throughout the tournament. By checking, I can gauge the strength of his hand - if he checks back, I get a free card to potentially improve (K or another heart completes outs). If he bets, I'll have more information about his holding.

2. **Pot control**: With my short stack and a moderately strong but vulnerable hand, keeping the pot manageable is important. This preserves chips for spots where I'm more confident.

3. **Flexibility**: Checking allows me to respond to Flash's action rather than committing chips without information. I have multiple ways to improve (pair of Kings, heart-hits) and can re-raise or call based on his action.

4. **Stack preservation**: I have limited chips (6350), so investing wisely in spots with better information is crucial.

This is a patient play that keeps me in the tournament while maintaining my ability to play the hand profitably on the turn.

I have flopped top two pair (Eights and Sevens) on a very coordinated board (7s 6h 8c). While this is a very strong hand, the board texture is dangerous as any 5 or 9 completes a straight. Since Claude checked from the Big Blind, I need to bet for value and protection. I want to get paid by one-pair hands like an 8, 7, or 6, and I want to charge draws like a 9 or a 5. A bet of 450 into a 650 pot (approximately 70% pot) is a standard sizing to build the pot while the hand is likely ahead.

I'm calling Flash's 450 bet on the flop. Here's my analysis:

**Hand Strength**: K♥8♥ on a 7♠ 6♥ 8♣ board gives me:
- A pair of 8s (moderate strength)
- A flush draw with 10 outs (any heart completes my flush)
- An overcard King that could make top pair
- Multiple ways to improve

**Equity Assessment**: Against Flash's likely range (continuation bets, broadway cards, or weaker pairs), I have approximately 40-50% equity. This is solid for a call.

**Pot Odds**: The pot is 1100 and I'm calling 450, getting 2.44:1 odds. My equity of 40%+ justifies the call mathematically.

**Stack Considerations**: While I'm short-stacked at 6350, I need to play hands with positive equity. Folding here would be too conservative given my situation. After this call, I'll have 5900 left with a chance to hit my draw or improve my pair.

**Strategic Rationale**: By calling, I:
1. Keep alive with genuine equity to win the pot
2. See another card before making a larger commitment decision
3. Stay engaged in the tournament rather than folding away opportunity
4. Maintain fold equity if my next card improves the situation

This is the right balance between aggression and prudence at my stack depth.

With middle pair (8s), an overcard King, and 10 heart outs to the flush, I'm in a position with moderate equity but a vulnerable holding. The 2♦ turn improved neither hand significantly. At 5,900 chips (short stack), I should control the pot and gain information rather than commit more chips preemptively. Checking accomplishes multiple goals: (1) allows me to see the river cheaply if Flash checks back, (2) provides information about Flash's hand strength, (3) preserves chips for better spots, and (4) keeps my fold equity intact if Flash makes a large turn bet. This is sound stack-preservation poker that aligns with my position as the big blind with limited chips.

I have top two pair (Eights and Sevens) on a board that is still quite coordinated (6-7-8). The 2d on the turn is a complete brick and does not change the board texture. Claude checked again, which often signifies a mid-strength hand or a draw. I must continue betting for value and to protect my hand against straight draws (any 9 or 5). A bet of 1000 into a 1550 pot (about 65%) is large enough to extract value from one-pair hands like an 8, 7, or 6, while making it expensive for drawing hands to continue. Claude's stack is around 5900, so building the pot now sets up a potential all-in on the river if the board remains safe.

Calling Flash's 1000 turn bet with K♥8♥ on a 7♠6♥8♣2♦ board. I have middle pair (eights) plus 10 heart outs to make the flush, plus an overcard King. Estimated equity is 40-45% against Flash's likely button-raise and continuation-bet range. Pot odds of 3.55:1 support this call mathematically (need only 22% equity). As a short-stack player in a tournament, I must play hands with positive expected value. After this call, I'll have 4900 chips remaining, which commits me to seeing the river, but the equity justifies the decision.

Flash has checked on the river. With K♥8♥ on a board of 7♠6♥8♣2♦T♣, I have a modest pair of eights with a King kicker. This is a relatively weak holding on a completed runout.

**River Decision Analysis:**
