With A8 offsuit on the button, I have a strong, playable hand that merits aggression. I'm in excellent position and can apply pressure to the blinds. A standard raise to 3x the big blind (300 total) is appropriate here. This accomplishes several objectives: (1) it isolates the action and allows me to potentially win the blinds immediately, (2) if called, I have good position for post-flop play, and (3) it maintains aggression as the button, which is standard tournament poker. My stack of 5000 can afford this raise comfortably. Given that Minni (big blind) has been involved in many pots and Flash has a large stack, there's good fold equity here while maintaining hand strength if called.

I am in the Small Blind holding 9-6 offsuit. Claude, who is the short stack at the table with 4,700 chips, has raised to 3x the big blind from the Button. 9-6 offsuit is a very weak hand to play out of position, and there is no strategic reason to defend this hand against a Button open. My goal is to maximize my stack over 150 hands, and calling here would likely lead to losing more chips in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Heads-up vs Claude (button) who opened to 300. I'm in the big blind with A♠4♥. Heads-up A4o is a defendable hand — it has decent equity vs a wide opening range and can make the best hand often on the flop. A small 3‑bet here would bloat the pot while I remain out of position and could commit me to a big pot with a weak kicker (and may induce a shove from Claude’s ~4.7k stack). Pot odds to call are attractive (200 into a 450 pot ≈ 30.8% equity needed). Plan: call and see a flop; if I hit an Ace I can extract value but play the weak kicker cautiously; if I miss I’ll proceed conservatively (check/fold to big pressure or float and take later streets when appropriate).

Check. I have A♠4♥ and missed the dry 7♥-3♠-T♦ flop — no pair, no real draw and I’m out of position vs a BTN open (Claude) who opened wide. Checking controls the pot and lets me realize equity without bloating a pot I’m unlikely to win at showdown. Plan: if Claude checks back I take a free turn; if he makes a small continuation bet (roughly ≤1/3 pot) I’ll consider calling/float to probe the turn; if he barrels medium/large (≈1/2 pot or bigger) I’ll fold and preserve my deep stack unless I pick up a pair or clear equity on the turn.

Continuation bet on the flop with A8. I raised pre-flop, and this dry flop (7-3-T) likely missed Minni's range. His check indicates weakness. Position is strong, fold equity is favorable, and proper bet sizing (60% of pot) applies standard tournament pressure. This maintains initiative and should win the pot frequently while keeping chips in play if called.

Claude opened from the button and I defended with A♠4♥; I checked the flop. The board 7♥-3♠-T♦ is very dry and Claude’s bet of 400 into the ~650 pot is a medium/large c-bet (~60% pot). To call 400 I’d need ~27.6% equity (400 / (1050+400) ≈ 27.6%). A4o only has ~6 outs to pair (~12.8% to hit on the turn, ~24.1% to hit by the river) and much of Claude’s c-bet range contains hands (Tx, 7x, Ax with a better kicker) that dominate me. Calling OOP here invites turn shove/pressure from his ~4.3k stack and can commit me to a big pot with a weak kicker. I’ll fold, preserve my deep stack, and wait for a clearer, more profitable spot.

I have pocket Jacks (Jh, Jc) on the button, which is a premium starting hand, particularly in a three-handed game. My goal is to build a pot and take advantage of my position. A standard raise to 300 is consistent with my previous play and exerts pressure on the blinds while allowing me to easily call or evaluate if Claude (the short stack) decides to shove.

I have A♦4♥ in the small blind facing a button open to 300 (I already posted 50; to call I need 250 more). I’m deep (≈151bb) so I can play postflop, but A4o is a marginal, often-dominated Ace and I’ll be out of position against the button and possibly the big blind. A tiny 3-bet to the min (500) won’t buy much fold equity and a larger 3-bet is too thin with this kicker. Calling keeps the pot controllable, lets me realize equity and proceed cautiously postflop (look to continue only when I hit a real piece or get favorable board action). I'll call the raise and evaluate on the flop.

3-7 offsuit is the worst starting hand in poker with virtually no showdown value. Flash has shown consistent aggression, Minni called from the SB, and I'm out of position. My 5250-chip stack is healthy enough to fold trash hands. There's no justification for calling 200 chips with this hand. Folding preserves chips for premium opportunities.

I have A♦4♥ and the flop is 2♥-7♦-4♠, so I’ve hit a pair of fours with the Ace kicker. The board is fairly dry and my hand has decent showdown value but is vulnerable to virtually any higher pocket pair or an A‑X that the button opens. I’m out of position and the pot is small relative to our deep stacks, so I want to control the pot size and induce bluffs or get to a cheap showdown rather than bloating the pot OOP. Plan: check and call a standard continuation bet, re-evaluate on a raise (fold to large aggression unless pot odds/read justify calling), and if opponent checks back consider a thin value bet on safe turn runouts or simply show down. Check is the lowest-variance, highest-expected-value line here.

I have an overpair (JJ) on a very dry, low-card board (2-7-4 rainbow). Minni's check likely signifies either a weak hand or a marginal holding looking to get to showdown cheaply. I will place a standard continuation bet of 400 (just over half the pot) to extract value from pocket pairs, gutshot draws like 5-6, or Ace-high hands that might call one street.

I checked the flop with A♦4♥ and hit a pair of fours (4♥ + board 4♠) — a vulnerable but decent showdown hand OOP. Flash (button) opened wide preflop and now made a relatively small flop bet (400 into a 1100 pot), which is consistent with a wide c-bet range containing many bluffs and weak Ax hands. Raising OOP with this marginal made hand is unnecessary risk: I’m out of position, my kicker can be dominated by many A-x, and a raise will bloat the pot when I’m often behind to overpairs or two-pair draws. The correct low-variance + +EV line is to call the 400: I get good pot odds (~26% to continue), keep worse hands and bluffs in, and can re-evaluate on the turn. Plan after the call: if the turn is a safe card (low, non-A, non-7) and villain checks I can take a free card or bet for thin value; if villain barrels large on turn I’ll fold unless I improve or pot odds justify calling. So I will call the 400.

I have A♦4♥ on a 2♥-7♦-4♠-8♣ board — second pair with the best possible kicker for my pair but easily dominated by any A‑x and vulnerable to overpairs or two‑pair. Flash opened wide from the button and c‑bet the flop small (400); I called to keep bluffs and weaker Ax in. The turn 8♣ is a relatively neutral card (not an A or 7) but it does bring some extra connectivity and I am out of position facing a deeper-stacked preflop aggressor. With ~14k behind and the pot only 1.5k I want to control the size of the pot and avoid creating situations where I’m forced off a marginal made hand by a raise. Checking is the lowest-variance +EV line: it keeps worse hands and bluffs in, avoids bloating the pot OOP, and lets me call a reasonable turn bet or fold to sizable aggression. Plan: check here; call small turn bets and fold to large raises unless pot odds or reads justify calling/raising; if villain checks back I’ll consider a thin value bet on safe rivers or proceed to showdown.

I currently hold an overpair (JJ) on a dry board of 2-7-4-8. Minni's check on the turn suggests she is likely in check-call mode with a marginal hand like a mid-pair (99, TT) or a top pair on the board (7x, 8x). The 8c is a relatively safe card, though it does complete a straight for 56, which is a small part of her range. I will continue betting for value to extract chips from her bluff-catchers and to charge any remaining draws. A bet of 1000 into a 1500 pot is a strong value bet that keeps the pot building while I have the best hand.

I hold A♦4♥ — second pair (4s on the board) with the nut Ace kicker. BTN (Flash) opened wide preflop, c-bet the dry 2♥-7♦-4♠ flop small (a wide range of value hands and bluffs), and now raises the turn (8♣) to 1000 into a ~2500 pot. Calling 1000 to win ~3500 gives me ≈22% pot equity required. Against a wide button raising range I beat many bluffs and worse A‑x hands and lose mainly to sets, 7x, 8x and big overpairs — so I have sufficient pot odds and showdown value to continue. Raising OOP with a marginal second pair would needlessly bloat the pot and put me in tough spots; folding is too passive given the price and the likely frequency of bluffs in his line. I will call 1000 to control the pot, keep his bluffs/worse hands in, and re-evaluate on the river (fold to a large shove or fold if a very adverse river and big bet; value-bet or check-showdown depending on river texture and opponent action).

I have A♦4♥ — second pair with the nut Ace kicker on a dry 2♥-7♦-4♠-8♣-6♣ board. Flash opened wide and barreled (small flop c-bet then a sizable turn raise), so his range is polarized between strong value (sets, two-pair, overpairs) and bluffs. A thin value bet here will often fold out worse hands and only get called by better; checking preserves showdown equity and induces bluffs. I’ll check and let him have the opportunity to bluff or show down; no reason to bloat the pot OOP with a marginal made hand.

I have an overpair with Jacks on a board that has become increasingly coordinated. The river 6c completes a potential straight for any hand containing a 5 (4-5-6-7-8). Minni has check-called two streets of value, which suggests she has a made hand like 99, TT, or a pair of Sevens or Eights. While I likely have the best hand, the presence of the straight possibility and her sticky behavior makes a third street of value risky. Checking back ensures I win the pot against her medium-strength hands without having to face a potential check-raise or losing additional chips to a slow-played straight or set.

I'm on the button with premium pocket Aces (Ah As) in a 3-handed spot. Pot is 150 and it costs 100 to call. Opponents: Claude (SB) is relatively short (5,200) and Flash (BB) is deep (21,200). I want to build the pot and narrow the field while giving the short-stacked Claude the opportunity to shove (which is +EV for me) and still leave flexibility against Flash. A raise to 1,200 (12 BB) charges speculative hands, induces shoves from the short stack, and still lets me handle a 3-bet from Flash comfortably with the best starting hand. If Claude jams I will call; if Flash 3-bets I will continue (likely call and proceed to extract value), so raising now maximizes expected value compared with just calling or min-raising.

Jd3c is a weak hand lacking any strong equity. Out of position from the small blind against an aggressive button raiser (Minni), this is a clear fold. With 5200 chips remaining, preserving capital for better spots is the priority. J3o doesn't have sufficient equity to justify calling 1150 chips. Folding minimizes losses and keeps powder dry for premium opportunities.

Minni has made an unusually large open to 1200, which is 12x the big blind, compared to her standard 300. My hand, Td 4c, is very weak and does not justify calling such a significant raise out of position, even as the chip leader. The pot odds are not favorable given the strength of my hand versus a range that is likely polar or very strong given the sizing.

I'm on the button with 9-Q offsuit, a moderately strong hand for this position. With 52 BBs in my stack, I need to play positionally and maintain aggression. The small blind (Flash) has 21k chips and the big blind (Minni) has 13.5k, so both have healthy stacks. A standard 3x BB raise to 300 total puts pressure on both opponents' blind defense ranges, and I retain position post-flop. 9-Q plays well in position and can win many pots uncontested or see a flop with equity. This is more effective than the passive call, as it controls the pot and prevents two opponents from seeing a flop cheaply against my medium stack.

I am in the Small Blind with J6o. Claude has raised from the Button to 300. J6o is a weak hand that does not play well out of position, especially against a button opening range. With a healthy stack of over 21,000 chips, there is no need to defend with such a marginal hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I have A♣K♠ in the big blind facing a standard raise to 300 from the button (Flash folded). Pot is 450 and it’s only 200 to call, but AK is strong enough to take an aggressive line. I want to isolate Claude, extract value from worse Ax/Kx and get fold equity against his wide opening range. Claude’s stack (~4,900) is deep enough that he’ll often fold or call; if he 4-bet shoves I’m willing to call with AK. A 3-bet to ~1,200 is a standard, constructive size here—big enough to earn folds and build the pot when I’m likely ahead, but not so large that it jams out all action. Therefore I will raise to 1,200.

Minni has 4-bet me from the big blind to 1200 after my 300 raise. This is a significant re-raise that indicates strong holdings - likely QQ+, AK, or possibly wider given Minni's aggressive tendencies throughout these hands.

My 9d-Qs is a decent hand in isolation but falls short against Minni's likely 4-bet range. Equity analysis:
- Against QQ+/AK: ~30-35% equity
- Pot odds needed: 1550/(900) = ~36% equity needed
- I'm below breakeven on this call

Stack considerations are critical:
- I have 4900 chips (49 BB) - getting short
- After calling, I'd be left with 4000 chips (40 BB)
- A fold preserves flexibility and avoids putting my remaining stack in jeopardy from a likely unfavorable situation
- With healthy stacks remaining (Flash has 211 BB, Minni has 124 BB), I should be patient and selective

While 300 chips already invested creates sunk cost pressure, tournament poker requires folding marginal hands when facing aggression with short stacks. This preserves my tournament life for better spots with premium holdings or favorable positions against the remaining 76 hands.

I am on the button with Qs7s, which is a strong opening hand in a 3-handed game. I have a significant chip lead and want to put pressure on the blinds. Claude is the short stack with 4800 chips, so a standard 3x open allows me to see how they react. If I win the blinds, it's a small gain; if they call, I have position throughout the hand.

Fold. I’m in the small blind with 6♦5♠ — a weak, offsuit, out-of-position hand facing a button open to 300. Calling 250 into the 450 pot gives me ~35.7% pot-odds (250/700) to break even, but 6-5o has far less equity vs a standard BTN opening range and is frequently dominated — and Claude (BB) has a short stack (4800) so this can easily become a multiway pot where my hand fares even worse. A tiny raise to 500 is thin and unlikely to achieve favorable postflop positions against an aggressive, deep-stacked opener. With a healthy 139BB I’ll preserve chips and wait for a better spot.

This is 5-2o (the worst starting hand in poker) in the big blind. Flash raised to 300 from the button and Minni folded from the small blind. While I'm getting 2.25:1 pot odds (200 to call into 450), my hand has virtually no equity against Flash's button raising range. 

With a relatively short stack of 4800 chips and such a weak holding, calling here would be results-oriented and poor long-term strategy. I have almost no realistic winning scenarios even if I flop something. This is a straightforward fold - I should reserve my chips for premium hands where I can play positively. Against aggressive opponents like Flash, position control matters, and Big Blind is a difficult spot to defend wide ranges, especially with trash hands.

I’m on the button with 4♠4♦ and two players to act (Claude SB 4750, Flash BB 21200). This is a standard spot to take the initiative: a 3x open (to 300 total) gives fold equity, isolates the blinds more often than simply calling, and still keeps the pot small relative to my 139bb stack. Calling 100 to set-mine is viable, but raising is better to win the blinds outright or play heads-up where my pair has more value. If either opponent 3-bets I’ll re-evaluate (fold to a large shove or continue carefully vs a small 3-bet), but the default is to open-raise to 300 now.

5-10 offsuit is a weak hand with insufficient equity (~25-30%) against Minni's raising range. While pot odds are 1.8:1, I don't have the ~35% equity needed to profitably call. With a short stack (4,750), I should be selective and avoid marginal situations. This hand is best folded to preserve chips for premium opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind with 8d 3h, which is an extremely weak hand. Minni has raised from the Button to 300. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (200 more into a pot of 450), 83o plays very poorly out of position and is well below the threshold for a standard defense against a button open. Given my current chip lead, there is no reason to risk chips with a bottom-tier starting hand.

With 9s4h from the button, this is a weak hand that doesn't warrant investment. While my position is excellent and I have a short stack (4,750), 9-4 offsuit is simply too weak to defend profitably even against two blind opponents. The hand has poor equity and minimal potential. At roughly 48 big blinds, I'm not yet in desperate short-stack mode where I need to start pushing marginal hands. I should preserve my chips for premium hands or better spots. Folding here is the disciplined play that maximizes my expected value over the remaining 73 hands of this tournament.

I am in the Small Blind with 8h 4c, a very weak starting hand. The Button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Minni). While it only costs 50 more to call, 8-4 offsuit performs poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Minni has shown significant aggression in recent hands. Given my chip lead, there is no reason to enter a pot with such a weak hand against a capable opponent. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with 8c 7h, a mediocre connector. The stack sizes are a major factor here: Claude is very short with only 46.5 big blinds (4650), while Minni has about 140 big blinds. My stack is healthy at over 210 big blinds. While 87o is usually a fold or a mix from the button, in a three-handed game against relatively tight/passive blinds, I can occasionally open. However, Claude's short stack makes him more likely to shove over an open, and Minni has been active. Given the tournament context and my goal to maximize stack over 150 hands, folding this marginal hand to wait for better spots is the lowest variance and most disciplined play.

I’ll raise to 200. Heads-up on the button (small blind) I’ll be last to act postflop, so opening a wide stealing range is standard. J♠4♣ is marginal but acceptable as a button open — a 2BB raise applies pressure to Claude’s BB, creates fold equity, and keeps the pot manageable if he calls. Claude has ~46BB and will defend sometimes, but with position I can play postflop for value or pot control. If he 3-bets large I’ll fold this holding (poor equity vs a 3-bet). Total commit: 200.

I'm in the Big Blind with Ad8s facing Minni's 200 raise from the Small Blind. Flash folded on the button, making this a heads-up situation. 

Key factors:
1. Pot odds: I'm getting 3:1 to call (100 to win 300), which is very favorable
2. Hand strength: Ace-Eight off-suit is decent in heads-up play, especially against a small blind raiser who could be raising with a wide range
3. Position: I'll have position post-flop as the aggressor can't act last
4. Stack consideration: At 4650 chips, I'm the shortest stack but not in immediate danger. Folding good hands now would be too passive

Minni has been quite aggressive from early positions throughout this tournament, so their SB raise could contain many weaker holdings. With strong pot odds and a reasonable Ace-kicker, calling to see the flop is the correct play. I can reassess on the flop based on how it develops.

I missed this K‑3‑2 flop with J♠4♣ — no pair, only a backdoor club/straight. I opened preflop with a wide steal so I still have initiative and position; a small continuation bet pressures Claude’s wide BB calling range and can take the pot down. The pot is 400, so I’ll bet ~1/3 pot (150) to keep the pot manageable and retain fold equity. Plan: fold to a sizable check‑raise, call small/medium calls and proceed cautiously on the turn — only continue aggressively if I pair, pick up a club, or read clear weakness.

Calling 150 with A8o on a K-3-2 flop against Minni's small probe bet. Pot odds of 3.67:1 are favorable, and my Ace-high has decent equity against a wide SB raising range. The small bet size suggests Minni may not have a super-strong hand, making this a profitable call with reasonable showdown value.

Check. The turn (8♥) doesn’t help J♠4♣ — I have no pair and essentially no meaningful draw (only 3 jacks + 3 fours = ~6 outs to river, ≈13% equity). I c‑bet small on the K‑3‑2 flop and Claude called; that call indicates a range with many hands that already beat me (Kx, small pairs, 3s/2s, or a sticky float). My fold equity on a second barrel is thin, and facing a raise or a committed call from Claude would be costly with very poor equity. Pot control is the prudent line: check and fold to a sizable bet, or call only a small probe if the price is right to try to catch a pair on the river. If Claude checks back I’ll re‑evaluate the river and consider a small bluff only on a favorable card — but for now I check to avoid getting further committed with a bluff that has low expected value.

Betting 350 on the turn with a pair of 8s and Ace kicker. Minni's check after c-betting the flop strongly suggests weakness—either a weak King or an abandoned bluff. My pair is very likely the best hand here. A value bet of 350 chips extracts maximum value while maintaining proper stack-to-pot ratios. This bet size puts pressure on Minni while remaining financially reasonable if called. The turn 8 has helped my hand considerably, and taking down the pot or building a favorable position for the river is the right approach.

Fold. My J♠4♣ missed this K‑3‑2‑8 board with only 6 clean outs (3 jacks + 3 fours) ≈ 6/46 ≈ 13% to pair on the river. Claude’s turn lead (350 into a 1050 pot) requires roughly 350/(1050+350)=25% equity to make a break‑even call — I’m well below that. Claude called my flop c‑bet and now barrels the turn, a line consistent with Kx, middle/small pairs, or other hands that dominate me. Raising or calling to float here is negative EV given the odds, his range and his stack; I’ll preserve chips for better spots and fold.

On the Button with A♦9♦ — a strong, playable suited ace. Pot is 150 and it costs 100 to see the flop; I want to take the initiative, apply pressure to the small blind (50bb) and the aggressive big blind (very deep). Standard open to 3x the BB (300 total) gives good fold equity, isolates heads-up or keeps the pot manageable if flashed 3-bets. If Flash 3-bets to a small/standard sizing I'll consider a call and play in position; if faced with a large 3-bet or commit-shove from Claude I'll re-evaluate and often fold. Raising to 300 maximizes my immediate EV while preserving postflop maneuverability.

Folding K6o from the small blind. While Minni on the button is likely raising with a wide range of hands, K6 offsuit is simply too weak to defend or play out of position in this spot. The hand has poor equity against a button raise, and getting involved with marginal holdings from early position depletes my 5,050 stack inefficiently. At this stage of the tournament (hand 79/150), I should be selective with my marginal holdings and fold weak hands to preserve chips for stronger spots. The cost to call (250) isn't worth the poor equity situation.

I am in the Big Blind with K d 5 d, which is a strong suited king. Minni raised to 300 from the button, and Claude folded. The pot currently contains 450 chips, and it costs me 200 more to call. Given the pot odds (roughly 2.25 to 1) and the playability of a suited King in a heads-up situation against the button, a call is standard. I have a significant chip lead and want to maintain pressure while seeing flops with hands that have good post-flop potential.

On a flop of J-5-4, I have bottom pair with a King kicker. I am first to act. Checking is the standard play here to see how Minni, the pre-flop aggressor, responds. I have a piece of the board and some showdown value, but I don't want to build a large pot yet with just bottom pair. I will check and likely call a standard-sized continuation bet.

Flop is 5♥ J♥ 4♠ and I hold A♦9♦ — Ace-high with no hearts but a useful Ace blocker. Flash checked; his calling preflop indicates a wide range (Jx, small/medium pairs, heart draws, broadways, suited connectors). I want to take the initiative with a standard continuation bet to get fold equity against overcards and small holdings and to charge heart draws; a half‑pot sizing keeps the pot manageable while still folding out lots of marginal hands. Pot is 650, so I’ll bet ~325. If he folds I collect; if he calls I’ll reassess on the turn (check/protect vs heart turns, continue only on safe cards). If he raises I’ll give up most of the time — I have only Ace‑high and no nut draw.

I have bottom pair (Fives) with a King kicker. Minni's continuation bet of 325 into a 650 pot is a standard size for a button opener on this board. I am getting 3-to-1 odds to call, and my hand has sufficient equity against a wide button range to continue to the turn. I will call to see if the board improves my hand or if I can reach a cheap showdown.

I have bottom pair with a King kicker on a board that just got more coordinated with three hearts and some straight possibilities. I am first to act. Checking is the most prudent play here to control the pot and see how Minni reacts to the turn card. My hand has some showdown value, but it is not strong enough to bet for value or to turn into a bluff at this stage.

I have A♦9♦ on 5♥ J♥ 4♠ 8♥. I c‑bet the flop and was called; the turn 8♥ completes a very wet texture (three hearts) and greatly increases the chance my opponent has a made flush or at least strong hearts/Jx. I have no pair and only 6 raw outs to a pair (three aces + three nines), many of which will still lose to better made hands. Pot is 1300, stacks are deep, and Flash called the flop with a wide range that includes many heart combos. Betting here is thin and vulnerable to raises or value calls; the safest + most EV‑positive line is to check and see the river for free. If Flash bets the turn I will fold to meaningful value-sized bets (and only consider calling a very small probe that looks like a pure bluff). If he checks back I can reassess on the river.

On the river, the board is J-5-4-8-Q. I have bottom pair with a King kicker. Minni checked back on the turn after betting the flop. This check-back on a three-heart board often indicates a hand with some showdown value that didn't want to build a pot (like a Jack or an 8) or a missed draw. The Queen on the river is an overcard, but it's unlikely to have helped her if she was semi-bluffing the flop unless she had exactly Q-x of hearts or a gutshot. I have very little value in betting here, as I'm only likely to be called by hands that beat me. Checking is the best option to get to a cheap showdown or potentially catch a bluff if she decides to represent the Queen or the flush.

Board: 5♥ J♥ 4♠ 8♥ Q♠. I hold A♦9♦ — ace‑high with no heart and no pair. I c‑bet the flop (was called) and both players checked the turn after the 8♥ completed a three‑heart texture, which greatly increases the chance the caller has a heart flush or at least a strong Jx/paired hand. Flash checked the river as well. Betting here is thin — a bet will fold only pure air but will get called by any pair/Jx and lose to hearts; a sizable bluff is risky given deep stacks and the line taken. With very little showdown value and good reason to believe many hands that beat me will call, I will not invest more chips. Check to showdown and accept the result.

Q-9 is a solid hand on the button in 3-handed play. I should raise to apply pressure to the blinds rather than call passively. A raise to 250 wins the blinds immediately against weak holdings or maintains my positional advantage if called. This sizing is appropriate given my stack depth and opponent tendencies observed in prior hands.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Four offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Claude, on the button, has raised to 250. Given my position and the low equity of my hand, there is no reason to defend or bluff here. Folding is the most standard and disciplined play in this situation.

I’m in the big blind with 5♥3♠ facing a button open to 250. Pot is 400 and it costs me 150 to call — roughly 150/550 ≈ 27% pot odds (I need ~27% equity). 5‑3 offsuit is a weak hand but the raise is small and the button’s opening range is likely fairly wide, so a flat-call is the lowest-variance +EV line here. I don’t want to 3‑bet as a bluff OOP with a hand that plays poorly postflop; if I miss the flop I’ll give up to strong aggression, but I’ll continue when I pair, make two pair/sets, or hit a strong draw. Call to see the flop and keep pots controlled.

I’m out of position on a dry, paired flop (Js Jc 4h) with 5♥3♠ — no pair and only a tiny backdoor heart. Claude opened from the button, so his continuing range contains many Jx and strong broadway hands that beat me, and he can c-bet a lot of missed overcards as well. Betting OOP with this junk is a losing, high-variance line: if he calls or raises I have almost no equity or fold equity. The correct low-variance play is to check and concede to any meaningful aggression, keeping the pot small. If he checks back I get to a cheap showdown; if he bets I will fold unless the sizing is extremely tiny and I suspect a frequent c-bet/float.

Continuation bet on the flop. I raised preflop with Q9 on the button, and Minni called from the big blind. The J-J-4 flop is relatively dry and his check indicates a weak holding. A bet of 325 (59% of pot) applies pressure and wins the pot when he misses. This is a standard aggressive play leveraging position and preflop initiative. If he has a jack, he'll likely raise and I can fold. If he has nothing, I win the pot now.

I checked the flop with 5♥3♠ intending to fold to meaningful aggression — the hand has almost no showdown or draw equity on Js Jc 4h (only a tiny backdoor heart). Claude's continuation bet of 325 into a 875 pot requires roughly 37% equity to justify a call, and 5‑3o can't come close vs a reasonable c-bet range (Jx, broadways, some pocket pairs and value hands). Raising as a bluff OOP with this junk is high-variance and unnecessary; the low-variance +EV line is to concede the pot. Fold and wait for a better spot OOP or a hand with real equity.

On the button in a 3-handed game, Queen-Eight offsuit is a standard opening hand. I have a significant stack lead and want to maintain pressure on the blinds, particularly Claude who is the shortest stack at approximately 52 big blinds. Raising to 300 follows my established betting pattern and aims to win the pot pre-flop or play in position against one of the blinds.

Facing a standard button open to 300 in a 3-handed pot with J♦9♥ in the small blind I will defend by calling. I’ll put a total of 300 into the pot (I’ve posted 50 SB, so I add 250). Pot odds are good (250 into a 450 pot → ~35.7% break-even), and J9o has reasonable equity vs a wide button opening range and plays well postflop. I don’t want to fold too frequently out of the SB in 3-handed play, and I don’t want to 3-bet this hand out of position — J9o is a poor 3-bet bluff and a small min-raise won’t achieve isolation reliably. Plan: fold to a large preflop 3-bet, proceed cautiously on dry flops (check/fold to large pressure), and play aggressively if I hit a pair or strong draw (bet/raise as appropriate).

2-8 offsuit is the worst starting hand in poker. Out of position versus two opponents with stronger ranges. Short-stacked tournament requires hand selectivity. Folding to preserve chips for premium hands in better spots.

Flop: 2s 9s 2d — I have J♦9♥, so top pair (nines) with a decent J kicker. Versus a button-opening range in a 3‑handed pot I’m ahead of most of Flash’s holdings (A/K/Qx, many Jx, weaker pairs and broadways) and only behind 99, 22 and the rarer overpairs (TT+). The board is fairly dry (only two spades on board; a real flush draw requires two spades in hand), so I want to extract value and deny free cards. I’ll bet for value about half the pot to get called by worse pairs and broadways and to charge the few backdoor/spade combos. Plan: bet 350. If Flash raises big (pot‑sized or more) I’ll re‑evaluate and likely fold this kicker‑vulnerable hand; if he just calls I’ll continue for value or proceed cautiously on scary turns.

On this paired board of 2s-9s-2d, Minni has led out for a half-pot bet (350 into 700). My hand, Queen-Eight high, has no direct connection to this board and lacks any significant draw. While Minni's 'donk bet' could represent a wide range including air or small draws, I am currently behind any pair or a Nine. Given that I have a significant chip lead and Claude is the short stack, I do not need to contest this pot with a weak hand that has very little equity. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

On the button with Qc7h I’ll open-raise to 300 (3x BB). In position I can open a wide range; Q7o is marginal but fine to raise for fold equity and initiative. Both blinds have deep stacks but Claude (SB) is shorter and Flash (BB) defends a lot — a 3x sizing gives good chance to pick up the blinds and reduces multiway callers. If I face a substantial 3-bet I’ll fold this hand; if called I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops and control the pot on dry boards, committing further only when I have real equity or a clear read.

K2o is too weak to defend from the small blind, especially out of position with Flash's large stack still to act in the big blind. This is a clear fold to avoid playing a marginal hand out of position. Preserving my 5200 chip stack for better spots is the correct strategy.

I am in the Big Blind with Kd 7d, a strong suited King in a three-handed game. Minni opened from the Button to 300. K7s is well ahead of a typical Button opening range and provides good playability. With a pot of 450 and 200 to call, the pot odds (2.25:1) are more than sufficient to see a flop. While a 3-bet is an option, calling keeps the pot controlled while I am out of position.

On this paired board of 9c-5d-9s, I am the caller out of position. I have King-high with a backdoor flush draw. Standard play here is to check to the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) to see how they react to this board texture. King-high holds some showdown value against her air hands and I don't want to turn it into a bluff immediately.

Preflop I opened from the button (3x) and got a single caller from the BB. Flop comes 9c 5d 9s — a paired board that interacts much better with the BB’s defending range (pocket pairs, 9x, 5x, lots of floats) than with my raising range. I hold Qc7h: no pair, one overcard and only a very remote backdoor club possibility. My fold equity on a c‑bet is low because Flash defends and calls widely, so a continuation bet will be called far too often by hands that beat me. With deep stacks and a small pot (650) I prefer pot control and using position to get information. Plan: check and evaluate turns — if he bets I will fold most realistic bets (I’m not getting correct odds to call), if he checks back I can see a free card and potentially take a stab on a later street only if the card or action gives me a real chance to fold out better hands. So: check.

On the turn 8h, the board is 9c-5d-9s-8h. I have King-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 6). Minni checked back the flop, which suggests she likely doesn't have a 9 or a strong pocket pair, or she's playing a marginal hand like Ace-high or a small pair cautiously. Checking again is the most prudent move. It allows me to see a river for free, potentially improving to a pair of Kings, a pair of Sevens, or a straight. If she bets, I can re-evaluate based on the sizing, but for now, I want to keep the pot small with my current showdown value and draw.

Check. On 9c 5d 9s 8h I hold Qc7h with no pair and no meaningful draw. The board heavily interacts with a calling/defending range (pocket pairs, 9x, 5x, floats) and Flash has checked both flop and turn, so I have very little fold equity — a c‑bet will be called too often by hands that beat me. With deep stacks and a small pot I prefer pot control: take a free river and fold to a realistic bet. If Flash bets the river I will fold most of the time unless a card gives me real showdown equity (e.g. a queen) or the sizing/read suggests a pure bluff; if he checks back I can consider a thin bluff or showdown depending on the river card.

On the river, the board is 9c-5d-9s-8h-Th. I have King-high (K-10-9-9-8). Minni, the pre-flop aggressor on the button, has checked back both the flop and the turn. This pattern suggests she has a weak hand, perhaps a small pocket pair, a weak Ace, or complete air. Since the board paired nines and now shows a potential straight (6-7-8-9-10 or 7-8-9-10-J), my King-high has very little showdown value if she has any piece of the board or even an Ace. However, there's no reason to turn my hand into a bluff here because most hands that would fold to a bet (like lower high-card hands) I already beat, and hands that beat me (like a 5 or an 8) are unlikely to fold on this board after checking twice. I will check and hope to see a cheap showdown or fold if she bets significantly.

Check. I have Qc7h with no pair and only one club on 9c 5d 9s 8h Th. Flash defended preflop and has checked flop and turn, and his calling range (pocket pairs, 9x, 5x, floats) dominates my hand. Pot is small (650) and stacks are deep, so betting here would be a thin bluff with very low fold equity and poor expected value — most reasonable hands that beat me will simply call. I’ll exercise pot control and take the free showdown; if Flash bets a realistic sizing on the river I will fold (only calling a tiny unblock/block bet or a sizing/read that screams bluff).

K6o is too marginal to play when short-stacked at 50BB. Despite having button position and reasonable pot odds, this hand lacks the strength to justify building pots. Priority is preserving stack for premium holdings where I can win larger pots and make deep tournament runs.

Holding Ten-Three offsuit in the small blind, this hand is extremely weak and has very poor playability post-flop. Even though it is only 50 chips to call, doing so would put me out of position against the big blind with a marginal hand. I will fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

I am on the button with a strong starting hand (QTo) in a 3-handed game. As the chip leader, I want to maintain pressure on the blinds. QTo is well within the opening range for this position. A standard 3x raise is appropriate here to either take down the blinds or play the hand in position against a caller.

I’m in the small blind with Q♦7♠ facing a button open to 300 (I’ve posted 50; to call would cost 250). Q7o is a weak, frequently dominated hand and is hard to play out of position against the opener. The big blind still acts and can create a multiway or 3‑bet pot that puts me in awkward spots. Calling for poor equity (effective pot odds ~35%) or making a light 3‑bet are both suboptimal here. Preserve a healthy stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

Pocket 8s is a strong hand and likely ahead of Flash's button raising range. I'm reraising to 700 (a 500 raise from my BB position) to apply pressure, define hand strength, and build a pot when I likely have the best hand. This sizing (roughly 2.3x the raise) is strong but not excessive given stack depths and should get folds from many weaker holdings while allowing reasonable value if Flash has a premium pair or Broadway cards. Out-of-position post-flop play would be difficult, so taking a stand now is superior to calling and playing in the dark on later streets.

I opened to 3x from the button with QTo, a standard play. Claude in the big blind has 3-bet to 700. This is a very small sizing, requiring me to call only 400 more into a pot of 1,050. With pot odds of nearly 3:1 and being in position for the rest of the hand, QTo is more than strong enough to continue. I will call and evaluate the flop, as Claude's short stack (45 BBs) makes the post-flop play relatively straightforward.

I have pocket eights, flopping two pair (88 with 99 on board). This is a strong made hand on the flop 9c-9h-6s. Flash just called my 700 pre-flop reraise from the button, which suggests a fairly wide range - he could have an overpair (TT, JJ, QQ), broadway cards (AK, KQ), or possibly a nine. I need to define the strength of my hand by betting here rather than checking. A bet of 850 (approximately 60% of the pot) accomplishes several things: (1) it builds value with my current best hand, (2) it applies pressure to hands like AQ, AK, KQ that have significant equity but aren't made, (3) it's not so large as to become pot-committed, leaving me with 3650 chips, and (4) it maintains a balanced betting range. This sizing is consistent with normal continuation betting but also represents genuine hand strength. I'm out of position post-flop, so building the pot now while ahead is preferable to allowing Flash a free turn card with position.

Claude's pre-flop 3-bet and lead on a paired board (9-9-6) indicate significant strength, likely representing an overpair or a strong Ace-high that currently dominates my Queen-high. Given Claude's relatively short stack (approximately 2,800 remaining after this bet), he is heavily incentivized to follow through on the turn, making a 'float' with Q-high unprofitable. Since I have limited equity against a typical 3-betting range on this specific board texture, I will fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I’m on the button with pocket tens (Td Ts) in a three-handed game — a strong, standard open-raise spot. I want to build the pot and isolate the small blind while keeping fold equity and post‑flop position advantage. A raise to 3x the big blind (to 300 total) is a conventional sizing here: large enough to apply pressure to Claude (SB, 5900) and narrow Flash’s (BB, 20975) defense range, but not so large that I overcommit against a 3-bet. If I face a small 3-bet I can continue in position; against a large 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate (pot odds, villain tendencies, and effective stacks). Committing 300 keeps the initiative and maximizes expected value with this hand and position.

Raising to 700 with As9s in small blind. This is a solid hand in 3-handed play against Minni's button raise. The raise commits about 12% of my short stack while applying pressure and allowing me to play for value. The hand has decent equity against Minni's wide raising range, and I maintain flexibility depending on Flash's response.

I am in the Big Blind with 9c 5h. Minni raised from the button and Claude 3-bet from the small blind. My hand is very weak and does not play well post-flop, especially in a 3-bet pot while out of position against two active players. Folding is the only sensible play here.

I opened to 300 on the button with TT and Claude (SB) made a small 3‑bet to 700. In a three‑handed pot TT plays very well in position and calling keeps his range wide while preserving my positional advantage. Pot is 1100 and I need to call 400 more (≈26.7% pot odds); TT has good equity versus a typical SB 3‑bet range (mix of pairs, broadways and some bluffs). A min 4‑bet to 1100 would be too small to credibly fold out hands and a larger 4‑bet would commit too many chips without a clear exploitative read. I’ll call, see a flop in position, and proceed based on texture (value/pressure on dry boards, caution on A/K highs and heavy action).

Betting 1200 on the flop with As9s (flush draw + overcards). This represents about 80% pot-sized bet, which is standard aggressive play. I'm applying pressure to Minni's calling range while maintaining my aggressive preflop raise narrative. With a short stack and heads-up play, getting value from my equity or taking down the pot here is ideal. The flush draw and ace-high give me decent equity if called, and the bet puts Minni in an uncomfortable spot with most of his range.

I have trips (Td Ts with Th on the flop) on a Th–6d–Kh board. Claude 3‑bet preflop and has now bet 1,200 into a 2,700 pot — he’s representing a lot of the value part of a 3‑betting range (Kx, AK, overpairs, medium pairs, or a polarized / bluff line). Trips is far ahead of most of that range, so I want to build the pot and extract value while keeping hands like Kx and big pairs in. A small/standard raise (min‑raise to 2,400 total) accomplishes that: it prices weaker Kx and broadways to call, keeps his calling frequency high, and still leaves him with chips behind so he’ll pay off on later streets if he has a top pair. I don’t want to slowplay (risk letting hands check through) or shove unnecessarily (which would fold out some value or isolate me against only the very strongest hands). I’ll raise to 2,400 total to charge his range and re‑evaluate on the turn if he jams or calls.

All-in with premium drawing hand. As9s gives me a flush draw (9 outs), one overcard (Ace), and approximately 35-40% equity against Minni's likely holdings. As a short stack (4050 chips) at tournament midpoint, committing fully with a strong draw is optimal. The all-in applies maximum pressure—Minni likely folds mid-pair hands and bluffs (estimated 25-30% fold equity), and if called, I have legitimate equity to improve on the turn or river. The remaining stack (2850) after a call would force the same commitment on the turn from a worse position. This is sound short-stack tournament play in a critical spot with decent fold equity plus reasonable draw equity.

I have a set (TdTs with Th on board) — a very strong hand versus Claude’s 3‑betting/continuation range, but he’s shown a lot of aggression on the flop (bet 1,200, I raised to 2,400, he reraised to 4,050). The pot is 7,950 and I only need to call 1,650 more (1650 / (7950+1650) ≈ 17.2%); that’s a cheap price to keep KL/medium pairs/top-pair hands and bluffs in the pot. Claude only has ~1,200 behind, so re-raising to the minimum (5,250) would mostly just force him all‑in for a small extra amount or fold the very hands I want to extract value from. I want to keep his range wide and let him commit with worse Kx, pairs and bluffs on later streets, so I’ll call the 1,650 and re-evaluate on the turn.

With a short stack (1200 chips into 9600 pot) and pair of nines + spade flush draw, checking the turn is optimal. The favorable turn card (9d) gave me a pair and 4-card flush draw, but going all-in here offers my opponents 10:1 pot odds they will readily accept. Checking preserves my chips and keeps the hand alive to improve on the river to a flush (any spade) or improve to a better full house. This approach maximizes my equity realization by avoiding overcommitment with a hand that benefits from seeing another card.

I have a very strong hand — a set of tens on the turn (Th 6d Kh 9d). Claude is short (≈1,200 behind) and just checked. With so little behind a small sizing that effectively commits his stack is optimal: it extracts value from his Kx, one-pair hands, medium pairs and thin calls/bluffs while giving him correct pot odds to call light. The pot is 9,600 now; a 1,200 bet that he can (and likely will) call creates a ~12,000 total pot if called (he risks 1,200 to win ~12,000 — roughly 10:1 pot odds), so this size is highly profitable. Checking allows a free river card that could give draws or chop equity back and reduces value extraction. I choose to bet 1,200 to price him in and maximize value; if he jams or shoves I will call with my set.
No thought provided

I am on the button in a heads-up situation with 7c 8c, which is a very strong speculative hand. In heads-up play, opening from the button with suited connectors is standard to exert pressure and take advantage of position. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and maintain initiative.

Heads-up vs Button open to 300. I’m in the big blind with K♥6♥ — a defendable, playable hand heads-up with suitedness and decent showdown equity. 3‑betting here is marginal (K6s is often dominated by Kx and a small min‑3bet gives little fold equity while bloating the pot out of position). I'll call the 200 (total 300 committed) to see a flop and play post‑flop: proceed aggressively if I hit top pair or strong draws, otherwise be prepared to fold to heavy aggression and control pot size.

Out of position on A♥ T♦ J♠ with K♥6♥ — I have no pair, only king-high and a very weak backdoor heart/straight. The button’s open-raise is wide, but this board heavily favors hands with an A, T, J or broadway connectors; leading out OOP with a bluff is high risk and will often get raised or called by better A/K/Qx. I prefer pot control: check and see a turn card. Plan is to fold to heavy aggression on the flop (or a check-raise), call only a small c-bet to realize equity, and only invest further if I pair the K/6 or pick up a real draw.

On the flop of Ah-Td-Js, the board is highly coordinated and likely connects well with a lot of the big blind's calling range (broadway cards, pairs, and gutshots). While I am the pre-flop aggressor, my 8-high has very little equity here and almost no direct draws. Checking back is the safest play to control the pot size and take a free turn card while in position, as a continuation bet is likely to be called by many hands that Minni defends with pre-flop.

Turn Q completes a Broadway straight for me (A‑T‑J‑Q + my K = A‑K‑Q‑J‑T). Villain checked flop and turn after opening wide preflop — that line contains many one‑pair/medium hands and some backdoor diamond draws; I should be extracting value and denying equity. I will bet for value and to charge any diamond draws while still keeping the sizing reasonable against a check‑raise. Bet a sizable, but not overcommitting, amount (~2x pot) to get called by worse pairs/two‑pair and deny correct odds to draws; if I face a raise I’ll strongly consider calling with the made straight.

On the turn of Qd, the board is now Ah Td Js Qd. This board is extremely coordinated, and any King makes the nut straight. I hold 8-high with no draw and no pair. Minni has lead out with an overbet of 1200 into a 600 pot. This bet size suggests extreme strength or a polarized bluff on a board that likely hits her pre-flop calling range hard. Since I have no equity and no reason to believe a bluff-raise would be successful against an overbet on such a wet board, the only sensible move is to fold and preserve my stack.

Heads-up on the button with A♣T♠ — a strong, positionally advantaged hand. Calling 50 is too passive: I want to take the initiative, build a pot when I'm likely ahead vs a wide BB range, and gain fold equity. With deep stacks a standard 3xBB open (to 300 total) applies pressure but keeps postflop flexibility. Plan: c-bet favorable textures (A/T or dry boards) for value, and play more cautiously on coordinated/flushy boards. Raise to 300.

In a heads-up situation, Q7o is a strong enough hand to defend the big blind against a standard 3x raise from the button/small blind. With a stack of over 20,000 chips, calling 200 more to see a flop is a mathematically sound decision as I only need roughly 33% equity, which Q7o maintains against a wide opening range.

I have flopped top pair with a Queen kicker. However, the board is monotone (all clubs) and I do not hold a club myself. This makes the board texture very dangerous. As I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check to them. If Minni bets, I will likely call one street to see if the turn is a safe card, but I need to be cautious about flushes or higher overpairs.

The flop is very wet and monotone (7c 2c 6c). I don’t have a made hand — just A♣T♠ — but I do have the nut-flush draw (A♣) and I block the nut-club combos. Flash called my preflop open, so his calling range is club‑heavy and often already has a made club or a pair. C-betting here will get called too often and can put me in a tough spot; I’ll play this cautiously for pot control. Check to realize equity: call small-to-medium bets to chase the nut flush, fold to large overbets, and if Flash checks back I’ll reevaluate the turn (look to seize initiative on safe turns or extract value if I complete the flush).

The turn brings a fourth club to the board, which is extremely coordinated. I currently hold a pair of sevens (middle pair) but do not possess a club, meaning I have no potential to improve to a flush or even have a redraw. Minni checked back on the flop, but with four clubs out now, the board is very dangerous. Any club in Minni's hand (which makes up a large portion of a button opening range) currently beats me. I will check to see how Minni responds to the four-flush board. Betting would likely only get called by better hands or a club, and checking allows me to control the pot size with a marginal made hand that has very little equity against a flush.

I have the nut flush: A♣ with 7c-2c-6c-Tc on board (plus a pair of tens), so I’m almost never beat. Flash’s passive check-check line keeps a lot of clubs and medium pairs in his range. I should extract value rather than check and hope to induce — a 2/3-pot value bet targets second-best clubs, sets/trips and weaker hands that will call. Bet 400 into 600 to get called by worse flushes and maximize value while not overbetting and folding out thin callers.

The turn has brought a fourth club to the board, creating a very dangerous situation. I have a pair of sevens, but I do not hold a club in my hand. In poker, especially in a heads-up situation, a four-flush board is extremely polarizing. Since I have no club, I am losing to any single club in Minni's hand. Minni checked back on the flop but is now betting 400 into a 600 pot. This bet suggests she either has a club or is representing one. Given that I have no redraws and no club blocker, I have very little equity to continue. Calling here would be a 'pure' bluff catch, and on a four-flush board, this is mathematically precarious. It is better to fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.
I am on the button with QTo, which is a strong starting hand in a heads-up situation. I will raise to 300 to take control of the hand and put pressure on the Big Blind.

Facing a 300 open from the button, T♣8♥ is a marginal but defendable heads‑up hand. Flash opens very frequently from the button so my hand has reasonable equity and playability (can hit a ten, straights, etc.). I’m out of position, so I prefer to call and keep the pot manageable rather than 3‑bet light; I’ll see a flop and fold to heavy action without improvement or continue when I connect. Calling the 200 more (300 total committed including my BB) is the standard defensive play here given pot odds and implied odds.

Out of position with Tc8h on A♦2♦6♥ — I have no pair and essentially no realistic draws (with only one heart I can’t make a flush; only a runner‑runner straight is possible). Flash opens and C‑bets very frequently heads‑up, so this is a classic spot to check and conserve chips rather than firing a bluff out of position or calling down with air. Plan: check and fold to any meaningful bet; if Flash checks back we take a free showdown or can decide on the turn with more information.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and the flop has come Ace-high (2d, Ac, 6h), which fits my perceived range better than Minni's. Minni checked to me, which is expected. I will place a standard continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to represent the Ace and attempt to take the pot down immediately. There are very few draws on this board, so a call from Minni would likely indicate an Ace or a pocket pair, allowing me to reassess on the turn.

Call — I checked the flop with T♣8♥ and Flash fires a small c-bet (300 into a 900 pot). On A♣2♦6♥ I have missed: no pair and no realistic draws (with only one heart a flush is impossible). Flash is the aggressive button who c-bets very frequently heads‑up, and this small bet gives me ~25% break‑even pot odds. My two pair‑outs (10s and 8s, ~6 outs to river) put my raw equity very close to that threshold and I prefer to see the turn OOP rather than fold a lot of marginal equity or shove/bluff without blockers. Plan: call 300 to see the turn, fold to significant turn pressure if I don't improve, and if I hit a 10/8 re‑evaluate (check‑raise or extract value depending on action).

I have T♣8♥ on A♣2♦6♥J♣ and I’m out of position. I missed the turn — no pair and no realistic draw (the only way to improve is a ten or an eight: 3 tens + 3 eights = 6 outs → 6/46 ≈ 13% on the river). Flash is the aggressive button who opens and c-bets very wide heads‑up, so he will barrel a lot, but the Jack gives him plenty of hands (AJ/Ax, Jx, KJ/QJ) that now have real showdown value. Pot control is the priority: I don’t want to invest more with ~13% equity and no fold equity out of position. I’ll check and preserve my stack — plan is to fold to any meaningful turn aggression and only continue to the river if I improve or get a free/very cheap showdown.

I have a gutshot straight draw (King) and two overcards to the board's lower cards. Minni called my flop continuation bet on an Ace-high board, which strongly suggests she has an Ace or a medium pocket pair. The Jack of clubs on the turn is a card that could have improved her range (AJ, J6, etc.). By checking back, I take a free card to realize my equity and keep the pot manageable since my current hand only has Queen-high value.

I have T♣8♥ on 2♦ A♣ 6♥ J♣ Q♣ — I completely missed: no pair, no straight, no flush (Tc is a club blocker but doesn’t give me a made hand). The pot is 1,200 and it’s checked to me on the river. Flash opened wide preflop and c‑bet the flop often, but he checked the turn which looks like a capped or medium‑strength line (Ax, Jx, Kx, or missed hands). A river bluff would need reliable fold equity to be +EV; small bets need ~20–33% fold freq to break even depending on size, and Flash shows a tendency to call down light here. I’ll follow my earlier plan to preserve stack and take the cheap showdown — check. If Flash now fires a meaningful bet I’ll fold rather than invest more with no showdown value.

On the river, the board is 2d-Ac-6h-Jc-Qc and I have improved to a pair of Queens with a Ten kicker. Reviewing the action, I raised pre-flop and followed up with a continuation bet on the Ace-high flop, which Minni called. Both of us checked the turn. Minni has checked again on the river. While my pair of Queens is a relatively strong hand in heads-up play, the presence of the Ace on the board is significant. Minni's call on the flop suggests she likely has an Ace or a Jack. If she has an Ace, she beats me; if she has a Jack, I beat her. Furthermore, the river completed a possible club flush (Ac, Jc, Qc). Given that Minni has checked three times, she is likely in showdown mode with a weak Ace or a Jack. Betting now is unlikely to fold a better hand (like a weak Ace) and might only be called by hands that beat me or the occasional Jack. Checking back allows me to realize my showdown value and win the pot against her weaker pairs or missed draws without risking more chips against a superior hand.

Heads‑up on the button (SB) with 5♥8♠ — a marginal but playable hand in position. Pot is 150 and it costs me 50 more to see a flop (I’ve already posted 50), so calling (committing 100 total) gives excellent pot odds (~4:1) and keeps the pot small with deep stacks. A min‑raise to 200 is thin with this offsuit hand and risks facing a 3‑bet or bloating a pot I’m not thrilled to play out of position post‑flop. I’ll call and use my positional advantage to evaluate the flop, c‑bet favorable textures or fold to strong resistance.

I have King-Nine suited in the Big Blind. Minni, on the Button/Small Blind, just completed (limped) into the pot. K9s is a very strong hand in a heads-up or short-handed situation, significantly ahead of a standard limping range. I will raise to 400 to build the pot and take control of the hand, capitalizing on my card strength.

Heads‑up on the button with 5♥8♠ — marginal but playable in position. Flash iso‑raised to 400 after I limped, the pot is 500 and it costs me 300 more to continue (I’ve already posted 50). With deep stacks and positional advantage, calling to keep the pot manageable and see a flop is the best EV play here. A raise would be thin and risks bloating the pot out of position or getting a 3‑bet; folding surrenders too much equity. Plan: play the flop in position — C‑bet small on favorable low/connected textures or take a free showdown / fold to heavy aggression on dry high‑card boards; pursue straights/two‑pair when I connect, otherwise control the pot.

I have King-high with two overcards and a backdoor flush draw on a dry, low board. As the pre-flop aggressor, I maintain the range advantage here. A standard continuation bet of 50% of the pot is appropriate to apply pressure on Minni's limping range and potentially end the hand immediately.

I have 5♥8♠ on 6c 2h 7s — an open‑ended straight draw (8 outs: any 4 or 9). Villain bet 400 into a 1200 pot; calling 400 gives me 400/(1200+400)=25% immediate pot odds. My equity to make the straight by the river is ≈31–32%, so a call is +EV. Stacks are deep, so implied odds are favorable if I hit. Raising would bloat the pot and commit me with just a draw; folding surrenders too much equity. Plan: call to realize equity and play the turn in position — if I complete the straight I’ll extract value; if I miss and face heavy turn pressure I’ll fold, otherwise continue cautiously.

On the turn, the board has become increasingly connected with the 5d, bringing potential straight possibilities. I currently hold King-high with an inside straight draw (needing an 8 to complete a 5-6-7-8-9 straight). Minni has checked to me after calling my pre-flop raise and flop continuation bet. Given the board texture and the fact that my hand currently lacks a pair, checking back is the most prudent play to control the size of the pot and take a free card to see if I can improve on the river.

I hold 5♥8♠ on 6c 2h 7s 5d — that improved me to a pair of fives and I still have the open‑ended straight draw (4 or 9, ~8 outs, ≈31% to hit by river). Flash checked the turn, showing weakness/pot‑control. Pot is 1,600 and I face no bet. Betting is the highest‑EV line here: it extracts value from worse hands (A‑x, K‑x, smaller pairs, some turned draws) and charges pure draws while giving me initiative. I size to ~2/3 pot (1,000). After a 1,000 bet the caller must call 1,000 to win a final pot of 3,600, giving them ~27.8% pot odds — slightly below my ≈31% equity to hit, so this sizing denies some correct odds to draws. Plan: if called I’ll reassess the river (if I miss and face big aggression I’ll likely fold; if I hit my straight or the board pairs favorably I’ll look to get value). If checked to me on the river I’ll often value bet smaller; if I face a raise I’ll respect it as strength and fold most non‑made hands.

On the turn, the board has become quite coordinated (6-2-7-5), making straights like 3-4 or 8-9 possible. I checked to control the pot with my King-high and gutshot straight draw (needing an 8). Minni has responded with a significant bet of 1000 into a 1600 pot. My direct pot odds to call for a gutshot (about 8.7% chance to hit) are not favorable against a bet of this size (requiring roughly 27% equity). Given that my King-high has very little showdown value and the board texture heavily favors hands that called the flop, I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Holding 96s on the button in a heads-up situation is a standard opening hand. I will raise to 300 to maintain my aggressive pre-flop strategy and capitalize on the hand's post-flop playability and potential to hit flushes or straights.

Heads-up vs the button who opened to 300 (3bb). I’m on the big blind with T♦3♣ — a marginal but defendable hand against a wide opening range. Calling 200 more (putting 300 total) is cheap relative to my ~19.7k stack and gives me ~3:1 effective pot odds to see a flop. I’ll defend to keep the price low and see a flop, then play cautiously out of position: look to continue only with a T/3/top-pair/strong draw, otherwise concede to heavy continuation/turn aggression. 3-betting or folding here is too extreme with this holding.

Heads-up out of position on 4♣ 7♥ 7♣ with T♦3♣: I have no pair, only an overcard (T) and a very small backdoor club draw (need both turn+river, ≈4.2%). There are 3 tens left so I only have ~12.5% to pair by the river — not enough to charge chips profitably against a checked pot. Flash checked the flop after opening pre, which can be weakness but can also be a trap with a 7 or a set. With deep stacks (~19.5k) and the pot only 600, I want to control the pot and realize equity rather than bluff into potential strength. I’ll check; plan is to fold to a meaningful turn bet unless I improve (pair the Ten or pick up a real draw), and only pursue delayed bluffs later when board/runout and blockers justify it.

On a flop of 4c 7h 7c, I have a flush draw with my 9c 6c. Minni has checked to me. While the paired board is slightly better for the Big Blind's range, I have enough equity with the flush draw to justify a continuation bet. This allows me to take down the pot immediately if she missed or build the pot for when I hit my flush. I will bet 350, slightly over half the pot.

Board is 4♣ 7♥ 7♣, I hold T♦3♣. Villain opened and now bets 350 into a 950 pot. Calling 350 to win 1,300 requires ~26.9% equity. I have no pair and only weak outs: ~12.5% to hit a Ten by the river and only a small backdoor club chance — total realistic equity is well below the break-even point. I’m out of position and have poor blockers; with deep stacks this is not a spot to chase with T3o. Fold to preserve my stack and wait for a better, more justified spot to invest chips.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with 2♣5♣ — a weak but suited holding. The additional cost to continue is only 50 into a 150 pot, so I’ll call and see a flop rather than open-raise and risk a 3-bet with a marginal hand. Calling keeps my range wide, preserves stack depth, and lets me use position postflop: play aggressively on favorable flops (flush/straight draws or a pair) and pot‑control or fold on dry, high-card boards.

In a heads-up situation, A5o is a strong hand to hold when my opponent limps from the small blind. I will raise to build the pot and take the initiative, as my hand is likely ahead of a standard limping range.

Heads‑up on the button with 2♣5♣. I already completed to 100 and Flash reraised to 400; I must call 300 more. With deep ~19.4k effective stacks and position post‑flop, 2♣5♣ is playable as a speculative, suited hand — it can make a disguised flush or straights and fares reasonably vs a wide raising range. Calling 300 into the 500 pot (pot will be ~800 after my call) requires ~37.5% equity (300/800) to break even; with position and implied odds I can realize enough equity on favorable flops. A 3‑bet is too polar and folding is overly tight versus an aggressive opponent who raises wide. Plan: see the flop, barrel or seize initiative with strong/decent draws or top pairs, and fold to heavy resistance on dry/unfavorable boards. Therefore I will call and see the flop.

In this heads-up situation, I have top pair on an Ace-high flop after being the pre-flop aggressor. Although my kicker is weak, top pair is a very strong hand in heads-up play. The board has a flush draw (clubs) and some straight possibilities (J9, KQ, etc.), so I will place a continuation bet to gain value from weaker hands like a Ten, a Seven, or draws, and to protect my equity.

Flop: 7♥ T♣ A♣. I hold 2♣5♣ — a four‑flush to clubs (9 outs). Pot is 1200 and Flash has bet 400, so calling 400 into a 1600 pot requires ~25% equity (400 / (1200+400)). A 9‑out club draw on the flop has roughly ~34–35% to make a flush by the river, so calling is +EV. I’m on the button (in position) so I can realize equity, control pot size, and see one more street before committing more chips. Raising as a semi‑bluff is possible but risky with a low non‑nut draw (many club cards give me a second‑best flush versus higher clubs) and would commit too much against Flash’s wide/aggressive range. Plan: call the 400, monitor the turn — if a club hits I’ll extract value/cautiously bet; if a blank and Flash continues heavy, I can fold and preserve stack.

I currently have top pair with an Ace on a board of 7h-Tc-Ac-3h. Although my kicker (5) is weak, top pair is a very strong hand in heads-up play. On the turn, the 3h is mostly a blank card that doesn't significantly change the board texture, although it does introduce a second flush draw. I will continue with a value bet of 800, which is half the pot, to charge any draws (like clubs or hearts) and get value from weaker made hands like a Ten or a Seven that Minni might be holding.

Turn summary: I’m on the button with 2♣5♣ vs Flash (BB). Board: 7♥ T♣ A♣ 3♥. Flash bet 800 into a 2400 pot; to call I must invest 800 to win 3200 (break‑even equity = 800/(2400+800) = 25%). Raw outs: 9 clubs for a flush (9/46 ≈ 19.6%) plus three non‑club 4s for a wheel gives 12 outs total (12/46 ≈ 26.1%). That looks marginally above pot‑odds, but many of those outs aren’t “clean” — several club cards will still lose to a higher club in Flash’s hand if he holds two clubs or a higher single club combined with another club, and Flash’s line (preflop raise, flop c‑bet, turn bet) is consistent with strong one‑pair/value hands as well as made clubs. Raising as a semi‑bluff is too risky here (will often be called or 3‑bet by better hands and I have weak showdown value). I planned preflop/flop to fold to heavy turn action on a blank — that disciplined fold preserves stack and lets me wait for a better spot. Action: fold.

I am on the button with 97o. This is a mediocre hand, but in a heads-up situation, I should be playing a wide range from the button to apply pressure. The pot is small, and I only need to call 50 more to see a flop or I can raise to take control of the hand. Minni has been relatively active, but a standard raise here is a good way to test the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop. I'll raise to 300.

Flash opened to 300 from the button (SB). I’m in the big blind with Q♣7♣ — a suited queen is a defendable heads‑up holding. The pot is 400 and I must call 200 more, which makes the total I’d commit 300. Calling gives me 200/(400+200)=33% required equity; versus a wide SB opening range Q7s has roughly that equity plus good implied odds with deep stacks (18625). I’m out of position so a light 3‑bet isn’t ideal with a marginal hand — I’ll defend and see a flop, then play aggressively when I connect (top pair, strong draws) and pot‑control or fold to heavy pressure when I miss.

I defended preflop with Q♣7♣ and missed this heart‑heavy flop (8♥ J♥ 6♥). I have only an overcard (Q) and no heart draw, so my equity vs a standard button c‑betting range is poor. With deep stacks and heads‑up dynamics this is a pot‑control spot: check and see what Flash does. Plan: fold to a large continuation bet, call small bets to float and attempt to improve or take the pot on a later street, and if Flash checks back I’ll take a free turn to potentially fire if I pair my Q or pick up backdoor club equity.

On a monotone board of 8h-Jh-6h, I hold the 7h giving me an open-ended straight draw and a flush draw. As the pre-flop aggressor, I should continuation bet here to keep the initiative and put pressure on Minni. If she has a single heart or a pair without a heart, she will find it difficult to continue. A bet of half the pot (300) is appropriate to build value for my strong draws while also serving as a semi-bluff.

Board: 8♥ J♥ 6♥. I hold Q♣7♣ with no hearts — no immediate made hand but two overcards and backdoor club potential. Flash opened wide from the SB and his flop bet is small (300 into a 900 pot ≈ 25–33% pot). That size gives me decent pot odds (~25%) to continue against a wide c-betting range. Raising as a bluff is too thin here (I have almost no fold equity vs made heart hands and little equity myself), and folding to such a small bet would be too nitty given deep stacks and heads‑up dynamics. I’ll call 300 to keep the pot manageable, float and see a turn, and then commit only if I improve (pair Q or pick up meaningful backdoor equity) or if villain shows weakness. I’ll fold to large turn/river aggression if I miss.

I have Q♣7♣ on 8♥ J♥ 6♥ 9♦. I missed the heart‑heavy flop and still only have queen‑high; with one card to come I have roughly 6 clean outs (three Qs, three 7s) so very poor improvement equity. Flash opened wide preflop and c‑bet the flop (small), but checked the turn — that line looks like weakness/pot control or a trap. Betting the turn would be a pure bluff with limited fold equity versus a range containing many hearts and Jx, and it risks a check‑raise into a huge pot when I have almost no showdown value. With deep stacks and no real outs, the prudent, +EV play is pot control: check back the turn, see the river, and only invest more if I actually pair or if villain shows clear weakness on the river. I will fold to large river aggression if I miss.

On the turn 9d, I have improved to a pair of nines, and I still maintain an open-ended straight draw (5 or 10) and a flush draw with the 7h. The board is very wet with three hearts, meaning any heart in Minni's hand or a made flush already beats my current pair. Minni checked to me after calling on the flop. By checking back, I control the pot size and ensure I see a free river card to potentially realize my significant equity (straight or flush). My pair of nines also has some showdown value if the river is a blank.

River: 8♥ J♥ 6♥ 9♦ 4♦. I hold Q♣7♣ — completely missed, no heart and no meaningful showdown value. Flash opened wide from the button, c‑bet small on the flop (300) and then checked the turn; that line is consistent with either a medium/strong made hand (hearts, Jx, 8x) pot‑controlling or a missed c‑bet that he gives up on. I have no heart blockers and very little fold equity as a river bluff, so bluffing here is low EV. The correct move is to keep the pot small and not invest further with queen‑high: check and fold to any meaningful bet. If he checks back we lose a small pot but preserve most of our stack against a likely better range.

On the river 4d, the board remains very dangerous with three hearts and multiple straight possibilities. My hand improved to a pair of nines on the turn, which provides decent showdown value in a heads-up pot. Minni has checked for the third consecutive time (flop, turn, and river). While it's possible she is trapping with a flush or a straight, her passive line often suggests a weak made hand or a missed draw. A pair of nines is unlikely to get value from worse hands if I bet, but it is strong enough to win at showdown against her range of missed draws or smaller pairs. Therefore, checking back to realize my showdown value is the most prudent play.

Heads-up on the button with 9♦7♦ — a clear positionally profitable hand: suited, connected, good equity against a wide BB calling range and plenty of postflop playability with very deep stacks (≈180bb). Pot is 150 and it’s only 50 to call, but I want initiative and fold equity, so I’ll make the standard min‑raise to 2x the big blind (total 200). This raise isolates, builds a pot I can play in position, and forces the BB to defend more often. Plan: if he flats I’ll C‑bet appropriate textures and use position to realize equity; if he 3‑bets small I’ll consider calling and playing postflop; if he 3‑bets a large, polarizing sizing I’ll fold — 9‑7s is playable but not worth getting committal against a big 3‑bet. Raise to 200 maximizes expected value here.

In Hand #93, I'm in the big blind with Kh 5d and have 21,775 chips. Minni, on the button, has raised to 200, making it 100 more for me to call into a pot of 300. This 2:1 price is quite attractive for a hand like King-high, especially since I'm out of position but have a significant chip lead. Calling allows me to see a flop and potentially take control on later streets, as Minni has shown a tendency to fold to aggression or check back on certain boards in previous hands. Given the current stack depths and the pot odds, calling the extra 100 is a standard defensive play.

On this dry, unconnected board of J-8-2 rainbow, my King-high has very little value and no real draws. Standard play from the Big Blind is to check to the pre-flop aggressor to see their reaction. I will check and likely fold to any significant bet unless the price is extremely favorable, as this board hits a typical opening range better than it hits my defensive calling range.

Heads‑up on the button after I opened preflop — I have positional advantage and range advantage. The flop 2♣ 8♥ J♠ is very dry and missed a large portion of the BB’s calling range. 9♦7♦ has limited showdown value (only a small gutshot to a 10 and some pair outs) but is perfect as a continuation‑betting bluff to take down the pot or to charge weaker overcards and floaters. I’ll make a standard, modest C‑bet (half‑pot) to apply pressure while keeping future play flexible. If he folds I win the pot; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate the turn and continue only when I pick up equity or the turn is favorable — fold to large raises.

The flop of Js-8h-2c is quite dry and does not connect with my King-high (K5o) at all. Minni has made a standard half-pot continuation bet of 200. While the pot odds of 3:1 are somewhat tempting, I have no real equity or draws on this board, and I am out of position. Continuing with air against a frequent bettor like Minni is likely a losing play in the long run. I will fold and preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 8h 5c. This is a very weak hand, but it's heads-up and I only need to call 50 more chips to see a flop. However, 85 offsuit is near the bottom of my range. Considering I've been quite active, I can afford to fold here and wait for a better spot to put chips in, or I could try a small raise to steal, but against a solid opponent, folding is the most disciplined play with this trash hand.

Heads-up on the button with Q♥T♥ — a very strong, playable hand in position. Opponent (BB) will defend wide, so I want initiative and fold equity rather than giving a cheap flop. Min-raise to 200 is too small and invites too many cheap looks; I’ll raise to 300 total (≈3x BB) to build a pot in position, isolate the BB, and allow profitable postflop play. Plan: c-bet favorable high-card/flop textures, realize equity on connected/heart boards, call smaller 3-bets to play postflop and fold or 4-bet depending on large 3-bet sizing and opponent tendencies.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack of Clubs and Four of Hearts. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. J4o is a weak hand, especially out of position.
