While I am getting 2:1 odds to call, this hand is easily dominated and lacks connectivity or suitedness, making it difficult to play profitably post-flop. Given that I have a chip lead and we are at hand 95 of 150, I will choose to play conservatively and fold this hand to wait for a better opportunity.

I am holding Pocket Aces, the strongest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. My strategy is to build a large pot while maintaining a standard betting appearance. I will raise to 300 to extract value from Minni's range.

Heads-up vs the button’s open to 300, Qh5s is marginal but defendable. Pot is 400 and I only need to put in 200 more (total committed 300) — the pot odds (~33%) and the button’s likely wide opening range heads-up justify a call. Stacks are deep, so I’d rather keep the pot manageable and realize equity postflop than 3‑bet a speculative hand out-of-position postflop. Plan: call to see the flop; if I pair the Queen or pick up good draws I’ll play aggressively for value, otherwise I’ll give up to heavy, logical pressure on dry boards.

I defended preflop with Q5s to see a cheap flop. The board (2s 6c Jc) is dry and I’ve missed — no pair, no meaningful draw (flush impossible for me, no straight). Betting OOP here would be a thin bluff that will get called or raised by a lot of the opener’s wide range, putting me in a tough spot with very little equity. With deep stacks and the pot already small, I’ll preserve stack and pot control: check and fold to meaningful aggression. If Flash checks back I get a free turn to improve; if he makes a small probe bet I may call very narrowly for pot odds but will fold to larger bets (rough guideline: fold to bets > ~1/3–1/2 pot). If I pair a Queen or pick up a real draw on a later street I’ll switch to value/aggressive lines.

I hold pocket aces on a 2s-6c-Jc board, giving me an overpair to the board. This is a very strong position. Minni has checked to me after calling my pre-flop raise. I will place a standard continuation bet of 300, which is half the pot, to extract value from weaker hands like a Jack, mid-pairs, or club draws, while protecting my equity.

Heads‑up on the button Flash opens wide — I defended preflop with Q5s to see a cheap flop. The flop (Jc 6c 2s) misses me: I have one overcard (Q) and a weak backdoor; no pair or meaningful draw. Flash's bet is 300 into a 900 pot (one‑third pot), which gives me ~25% pot odds to call. Hitting a queen by the river is only ~12.8% (3 outs), but versus a wide HU c‑betting range my practical equity is higher (I estimate ~25–35%), and the probe is small enough to justify seeing another street to realize equity. I’ll call the small bet to keep the pot controllable and fold to significant turn aggression unless I improve. If I pair the Queen or pick up a real draw on the turn I will move to value/aggression; otherwise I’ll give up to larger bets.

Check. The turn Ace (As) is a very bad card for my Q5s — I missed and now have only 3 outs to pair the Queen on the river (~3/46 ≈ 6.5%). The Ace also strongly improves the preflop raiser’s range (many A‑x, A‑broadway and strong Jx combos), so betting OOP as a bluff is thin and will be called or raised by a lot of hands that dominate me. With deep stacks and a 1,200 pot I want to keep the pot small: check and fold to meaningful turn aggression. I’ll only call a very small probe (rough guideline ≤ ~1/3 pot ≈ 400) to see the river; otherwise fold. If Flash checks back I’ll reevaluate the river and only barrel for value/bluff-catch with a Queen or a clear improvement.

I have turned a set of Aces, which is almost certainly the best hand. The board contains a flush draw and some straight potential (though unlikely). I will bet 800 into the 1200 pot to extract value from Minni's likely holdings, such as a Jack, a flush draw, or even a weaker Ace she might have just paired.

Board: 2s 6c Jc As. I hold Qh5s and have essentially no meaningful equity — only three outs to a Queen on the river (~3/46 ≈ 6.5%). Facing an 800 bet into a 2000 pot requires ~28.6% equity to justify a call (800/(2000+800)), so a call is a clear negative EV. The turn Ace also heavily improves the preflop raiser’s range (many A‑x, A‑broadway and strong Jx combos) and Flash has shown aggression on earlier streets, so this sizing looks value‑heavy or a polarizing shove. Raising as a bluff from out of position with Q5s is too risky and unlikely to work enough. I’ll fold and preserve my ~17.9K stack for better spots.

Heads-up on the button with A6o is a hand I should play aggressively — good equity and positional advantage postflop. Flash (BB) is deep-stacked and fairly active, but that’s another reason to take the initiative rather than simply calling 50. I raise to a standard 3x BB (300 total) to seize initiative, deny BB good odds to see a cheap flop with weak holdings, and retain flexibility to c-bet favorable flops in position. If Flash 3-bets big I can re-evaluate (fold marginally, call or shove depending on sizing), but versus a call I’ll have position and a playable Ace for value/protection.

I am in the Big Blind with Qd7c. Minni has raised to 300 from the Small Blind. Given the pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 600), Q7o has sufficient equity to defend against a standard heads-up opening range. While out of position, the hand is strong enough to see a flop in a heads-up match.

On a board of Td-9h-4s, I have Queen high and a weak backdoor straight draw. In heads-up play, the Big Blind (Flash) acts first post-flop. Since I am out of position and do not have a strong made hand or a significant draw, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) to see how she reacts to this texture.

I'm the preflop raiser (A♥6♣) in position on a relatively dry flop T♦-9♥-4♠. Flash checked, and heads-up I should continue a high percentage of the time: this board misses a lot of his calling range (broadways, weak pairs, suited connectors) and my A-high has decent showdown value and blocks a lot of A-x. I will lead for ~1/2 pot to apply pressure without bloating the pot against calls — pot is 600, so I bet 300. Plan: if he folds I take it down; if called I re-evaluate on the turn (bet for value if I pair the A, pot-control or check back on unhelpful cards; fold to a large check-raise since I currently have only Ace-high).

On a flop of Td-9h-4s, I currently hold Queen high with no significant draws or backdoor potential. Minni, as the pre-flop aggressor, has followed up with a 300 chip bet into a 600 chip pot (half-pot). This board texture connects well with a standard small-blind opening range (broadways, pairs, and connectors). Since I am out of position and have very little equity to continue, folding is the most disciplined and profitable play to preserve my chip lead.

I am on the button with KJo (King of Hearts, Jack of Clubs), which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play. My opponent, Minni, is in the big blind. Standard strategy here is to raise to build the pot and take control of the hand, especially given my stack advantage and the strength of my hand relative to a random big blind range.

Heads-up against a standard 3bb open (300). K♦2♠ is marginal but worth defending from the big blind: facing a 200-chip call into a 400-pot gives ~33% pot odds, and versus a wide button opening range my hand has reasonable equity and playability. Stacks are deep, but I’m out of position so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a light 3-bet — calling keeps the pot manageable and lets me realize equity or fold to heavy action post-flop. Plan: call to see the flop; continue aggressively if I pair the K or 2 (or pick up strong redraws), otherwise control the pot and fold to significant pressure on unfavorable boards.

Heads-up on A♣Q♦6♦ board (I have K♦2♠). I have no pair and only a backdoor flush draw that needs both turn+river diamonds (~4% to make). The preflop raiser’s range caps with lots of A and Q combos that connect with this flop far more than my king-high. Out of position, betting as a pure bluff here will be called by most A/Q/medium pairs and won’t fold enough better hands to justify bloat. Better line is to control the pot: check and see what Flash does. Plan: check — if Flash checks back I get a free turn to re-evaluate; if Flash bets small I can call with pot-odds and my backdoor equity or fold to big pressure; if Flash barrels large I will fold. This keeps the pot manageable with a weak hand and preserves stack equity for better spots.

The flop of Ah-Qd-6d is very favorable for my pre-flop raising range, as it contains two high Broadway cards. Minni has checked to me, which is expected. Although I have not connected with the board, my King-high has decent showdown value against some of Minni's air, but more importantly, this is an ideal board to continuation bet. I will bet 300 (half the pot) to represent an Ace or Queen and put pressure on the opponent's medium-strength hands and draws.

Flash has bet 300 into a 900 pot (pot will be 1,200). I have K♦2♠ on A♥Q♦6♦ — no pair, only a backdoor diamond that needs both turn+river (~4%) and ~6.4% to hit a K on the turn. The bet is small (≈1/3 pot) and in heads‑up a wide c‑bet range is likely, so calling to realize equity and keep the pot controllable out of position is the best line. Raising as a bluff is too risky with poor equity; folding concedes good pot odds when villain might be barreling light. Plan: call the 300, re‑evaluate on the turn and fold to heavy pressure unless I improve (pair K or diamond turn).

Board: A♥ Q♦ 6♦ 4♣. I hold K♦2♠ — no pair and my flop backdoor diamond draw is now dead. On the turn I face a checked pot of ~1,200 with deep stacks. I only have 6 clean outs (3 kings + 3 twos) ≈ 13% to improve on the river, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a bluff/lead here. Flash’s flop c-betting range is wide; his check on the turn shows some caution and gives me an inexpensive free card to try to hit. Best line is to check and see the river — call small river bets when pot odds justify, fold to large river pressure if I miss, and only invest more if I actually pair up. This preserves stack equity and keeps the pot controllable in a marginal spot.

The turn card (4c) is a blank that doesn't change the board texture significantly. After my flop continuation bet was called, Minni's check on the turn suggests a marginal made hand (like a Queen or a Six) or a draw (like diamonds). While I have King-high, it is unlikely to be best if the pot grows larger. By checking back, I control the size of the pot, see a free river card, and keep my options open for the final street depending on what develops.

Board: A♥ Q♦ 6♦ 4♣ 3♠. I hold K♦2♠ — no pair, missed my backdoor diamond. Flash opened and c-bet the flop, then checked the turn and river; that line shows weakness or a missed draw/marginal hand. Pot is 1200; a tiny bluff of 100 has a breakeven fold frequency of 100/(1200+100) ≈ 7.7% — very low. Betting 100 is a low-risk attempt to steal the pot from many medium-strength hands and missed draws; if called I only lose a small amount, and if he raises I can release. I will make a small bluff to try to pick up the pot.

Minni's river bet of 100 into a 1200 pot is a very small lead (about 8% of the pot), often referred to as a 'blocker bet' or a 'probe' with a marginal hand like a weak pair (6, 4, or 3) or even a missed draw. While my King-high (KhJc) loses to any pair, the pot odds are extremely favorable; I only need to be correct about 7% of the time to justify a call. Given that the board has several missed draws (like diamonds) that I beat with my K-J high-card strength, and that this price is small enough to provide valuable information on Minni's betting habits without significantly impacting my stack, calling is a sound decision.

Heads-up on the button with 7d7s and deep stacks (~174 BB). This is a strong, playable hand heads-up: often ahead of the big blind’s wide defending range and with set-mining value. I want initiative and to build a pot while retaining postflop maneuverability, so I’ll raise to the standard 2x BB (total 200). That sizing gives fold equity, isolates the opponent, and keeps postflop decisions straightforward — c-bet many flops, pot-control on dangerous overcard boards, and pursue set/straight/river value when appropriate. I will call small-to-medium 3-bets to play postflop, but fold to very large 3-bets/shoves when pot odds and effective stacks make continuing unprofitable.

In Hand #99, I am in the Big Blind with 6d 4c. Minni, who is currently the only other player in this heads-up match, has raised from the Button (Small Blind) to 200. This is a minimum raise since the Big Blind is 100. I have to call 100 more into a pot of 300, giving me 3-to-1 odds. While 64o is a weak hand, in a heads-up situation with such favorable pot odds and a very deep stack (over 200 big blinds), calling to see a flop is a standard defensive play. I will call the additional 100 to see if I can improve on the flop.

I am in the Big Blind and have reached the flop in a heads-up pot. My hand, 6d 4c, has a gutshot straight draw (needing a 5) on a board of 10-3-2. This is a very dry board where the pre-flop raiser (Minni) likely has a range advantage. Standard play here is to check to the aggressor and see how they react. If she bets, I will evaluate based on the size, but for now, checking is the most appropriate move.

Heads-up I raised preflop with 7♦7♠ and the Big Blind checked the dry flop 2♠ T♥ 3♣. In position with a medium pair against a wide defending range this is a very good spot to continuation-bet — the board misses a large portion of his calling range and I can get value from worse pairs, weak tens, and unpaired hands while folding out equity-heavy air. I’ll make a pot-pressure c-bet ~60% to 65% to charge draws and deny equity: bet 250 into the 400 pot. If he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn (pot-control on scary overcards, barreling for value on blanks); if he raises big I’ll be inclined to fold given stack depth and vulnerability of middle pair.

I have a gutshot straight draw with 64o on a 10-3-2 board. Minni has bet 250 into a 400 pot, which is a fairly strong sizing (over 60% pot) on such a dry board. While I have a gutshot, the direct pot odds (approx 27.7%) are significantly higher than my equity of hitting the straight on the turn (approx 8.5%). In a heads-up match with deep stacks, I prefer to wait for better spots rather than peeling with a weak hand that has no overcards or backdoor flush potential. This board texture favors Minni's pre-flop raising range, which includes many pocket pairs and strong Tens.

I am holding T9o on the button in a heads-up situation. T9o is a strong enough hand to open for a raise, as it has good playability and is well above the average hand strength in a 1-on-1 match. Raising to 300 is a standard open that allows me to take control of the pot and apply pressure to Minni in the Big Blind.

Heads-up vs a 3x open to 300 from the button. I’m in the big blind with 2♠3♠ — a low suited connector with good flop playability and strong implied odds given very deep stacks (≈175bb). Calling 200 more (300 total committed) gives me 3:1 pot odds (need ~25% equity) and keeps my range wide; a small 3-bet OOP with this hand is unnecessary and will often put me in tough spots. Plan: call to see the flop and play based on texture — continue with made hands, pairs, or strong draws; fold to heavy pressure on dry boards when I miss.

I called pre with 2♠3♠ as a speculative, deep-stacked hand. Flop 7♥ 8♠ 6♣ is very coordinated and largely hits the raiser’s range (pairs, 9-10, 4-5, suited connectors). I have no pair and only backdoor outs: backdoor flush (~4.2% to hit both spades) and a runner-runner 4+5 straight (~1.5%) — combined equity to improve is tiny. Against a wide but aggressive opener, I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with essentially air. I’ll check to control the pot and see what Flash does; plan is to fold to substantial pressure (large c-bet or raise), and only consider calling a small c-bet as a float when sizing is favorable, or continue if I pick up a real piece on the turn (pair or a strong draw).

I have flopped a 10-high straight (6-7-8-9-10), which is the absolute nuts on this board texture. Since Minni checked to me, I must bet to begin extracting value and building a large pot. This board is very connected, meaning Minni likely has a pair, two-pair, or a draw that will call a significant bet. A bet of 400 into the 600 pot is a strong value sizing that encourages a call while maximizing my potential return.

I’m OOP with 2♠3♠ on 7♥-8♠-6♣ and Flash c‑bets 400 into a 1000 pot. My hand has almost no real equity — only runner‑runner possibilities (backdoor flush ≈4.2% and runner‑runner straight ≈1–2%), so total improvement equity is ~5–6%. Calling 400 to win ~1400 requires ~28.6% equity, far more than I have. Raising OOP with air is a losing line and will commit chips with very poor equity. I fold and preserve my deep stack for better, more profitable heads‑up spots.

Heads-up on the button with Js Th — a strong, playable hand with good postflop equity and position. Pot is 150 and it’s only 50 to call; I want to take the initiative and deny the BB cheap flops, so I’ll open to a standard size (300 total). That size matches table norms (~3x BB), builds a pot I can play in position, and gives a clear range advantage. Plan: if called I’ll c‑bet many flops and use position to realize equity; if Flash 3‑bets big I’ll re‑evaluate (likely fold to very large 3‑bets, call or 4‑bet selectively depending on sizing and reads).

In heads-up play, Q7o is a standard hand to defend from the Big Blind against a 3x open. I am getting 2-to-1 odds to call, and while I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand, the hand has sufficient equity against a wide button raising range to justify seeing a flop.

On this monotone board of NINE OF CLUBS, FOUR OF CLUBS, and KING OF CLUBS, I hold the QUEEN OF CLUBS, giving me the second-best flush draw. As the Big Blind, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor to see how they react to the board texture. My hand has significant equity due to the flush draw, and holding the Qc also serves as a valuable blocker. I plan to check-call most reasonable bet sizes to realize my equity.

Check. The flop Kc‑9c‑4c is very hostile for my Js‑Th: I have no pair and no club, only ~6 pair outs (J/T) plus 4 Qs to make the Broadway straight — a lot of those outs are compromised by clubs and opponent holdings. Flash's check is ambiguous (could be weak or a trap with clubs/Kx), and with deep stacks I don't want to build a big pot out of position with a basically air hand. A c‑bet here would be a bluff into many club hands and Kx/9x combos that will call or raise; checking controls the pot and keeps decisionmaking simple. Plan: call small bets to realize equity and fold to large raises (re‑evaluate if the turn gives me the Q or pairs the board in my favor).

On the turn, I have improved to third pair (sevens) while maintaining my second-nut flush draw with the Queen of clubs. Minni's check back on the monotone flop suggests a lack of a made flush or a very strong King, likely holding medium-strength showdown value or air. Checking here allows me to control the pot size while retaining the ability to call a bet to realize my equity or reach a cheap showdown. If Minni checks back again, I get a free card to hit my flush or improve further.

Check. The flop and turn are very club‑heavy (Kc 9c 4c 7s) and I have no pair and no club — only 6 clean pair outs (3 J + 3 T) ≈ 13% to improve on the river. Flash checked both streets, which is ambiguous (weak Kx, a slow‑played club, or a dry holding), but with deep stacks I have very little fold equity if I fire — a bet will be a bluff into many club and Kx hands that will call or raise. Checking controls the pot (600) and lets me realize my equity free of build‑ups I don’t want to face out of position. Plan: see the river for one card; if I pair (J/T) I can value bet or call small bets, if I miss I’ll fold to meaningful aggression and only consider a thin river bluff if the opponent shows clear weakness.

On the river, the board is 9c-4c-Kc-7s-8h. I have a pair of sevens (7h) with a Queen-high flush blocker (Qc). The hand has been checked through on both the flop and the turn, which strongly suggests that Minni has a marginal showdown hand or has given up on the pot. My pair of sevens has some showdown value against her missed draws or low Ace-high hands. Betting here wouldn't accomplish much, as better hands like a 9 or a King are unlikely to fold, and worse hands are unlikely to call. I will check and hope to win at showdown or fold if facing a significant bet, as my hand is quite weak.

I have J♠T♥ and the river (8h) completes a 7-8-9-10-J straight — a very strong made hand heads-up. The main hands that beat me are club flushes (Kc-9c-4c on board), but Flash checked flop/turn/river, which makes a slow-played flush possible but less likely. Checking back surrenders value to Kx/9x/pair hands that will call a reasonable size. I’ll make a modest value bet to extract from those worse hands while controlling risk against a possible club flush. Bet ~half-pot (300) to get called by Kx, 9x and other medium hands; fold or re-evaluate only if Flash raises very large (which would strongly indicate a flush).

On the river board of 9c-4c-Kc-7s-8h, I have a pair of sevens. The action was checked on both the flop and the turn, which typically indicates that neither player has a strong hand like a King or a completed flush. Minni's 300 bet into the 600 pot on the river represents a polarized range: she either has a marginal hand she's finally betting for value (like a Nine or a recently improved Eight) or she is bluffing with missed high cards. Given that I only need to be right 25% of the time to justify the call and that my pair of sevens beats all her missed draws and many of her potential 'air' bluffs, I will call to see the showdown.

Jack-Nine offsuit is a solid starting hand for heads-up play from the Small Blind/Button. I will maintain my aggressive posture and open with a standard raise to 300, which has been my consistent sizing throughout the tournament. This allows me to take control of the pot early or see a flop with the betting lead.

Heads-up in the big blind with 5♣6♥ facing a button open to 300 (I need to call 200). Versus a wide SB opening this hand has reasonable equity and plays well on low/connected boards — and calling gives me strong pot odds (call 200 into a 400 pot; ~33% breakeven). Raising to 500 with 56o OOP bloats the pot with a marginal hand and folding surrenders correct pot odds. I'll call to see a flop and then proceed cautiously: look to realize equity and bluff/raise only when the board and read justify it; fold to heavy turn/river aggression if I miss.

Check. I missed this A♠ Q♥ T♣ flop with 5♣6♥ — no pair, no realistic backdoor draws (with only one club in hand it’s impossible to make a five-card club flush by river), just small pair outs (5s/6s). The button’s open-and-fold/cbet range hits this board very hard (Ax, Qx, Tx, broadways, pocket pairs), so barreling as a bluff is thin and calling a standard c-bet is a long-term loser. I’ll control the pot by checking: fold to a sizable c-bet, consider calling an exceptionally tiny probe to see a turn, and only look for a delayed bluff on safe runouts or if the opponent shows clear weakness.

I have an open-ended straight draw on a board that significantly favors my pre-flop raising range (Ace-Queen-Ten). By betting here, I exert pressure on Minni's likely middle-pair or weaker holdings while maintaining the initiative. If called, I have eight outs to the nuts or a very strong hand. A bet of 350 into a 600 pot is a standard continuation bet size in this situation.

Board A♠ Q♥ T♣ vs a button c‑bet of 350 (pot 950). With 5♣6♥ I have no pair and only 6 clean outs (three 5s + three 6s). Probability to hit by river ≈ 24.1% (6/47 + (41/47)*(6/46) ≈ 24.15%), while calling 350 into a 950 pot requires ≈26.9% equity to break even (350/1300). My outs are often dominated by Ax/Qx/Tx and pocket pairs in his range, so even if I hit I’m frequently second best. Raising OOP as a bluff here is speculative and high variance. I’ll fold and conserve chips for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button with K♠6♦ and deep stacks. In heads-up play Kx is strong enough to open — I prefer to take the initiative rather than limp. I’ll make a small standard raise to 200 total (2x the BB) to get fold equity and define the hand while keeping the pot controllable. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet with this marginal offsuit K, but if Flash calls I have position and will c-bet favorable flops (top pair, K or 6, or textured boards with backdoor equity) and check/control on dry misses to realize equity and keep pots manageable.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Queen offsuit, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. My opponent, Minni, has made a minimum raise to 200. I will 3-bet to 700 to build the pot for value and take the initiative out of position.

Facing a standard 3-bet to 700 after my 200 open. I have K♠6♦ — marginal off-suit but in heads-up play with position it's worth continuing. The call is cheap relative to the stacks (pot is ~900; calling 500 commits 700 total and builds a 1,400 pot — about 2.8:1 pot odds), and I can use position to c-bet or control the pot postflop. I will not 4-bet with this hand; I’ll fold to a large 4-bet/shove but call to see a flop and play postflop aggressively when I connect and cautiously on dry boards.

I am the pre-flop aggressor after 3-betting to 700. The flop is 8h-7d-2d, which doesn't directly hit my Ace-Queen but is a board where I maintain a range advantage with high pocket pairs and overcards. I will place a continuation bet of approximately 43% of the pot to apply pressure and potentially win the pot immediately or see where I stand. A bet of 600 is appropriate in this 1400 pot.

Heads-up with position and a small c-bet into a ~2000 pot. I hold K♠6♦ — one overcard to the board and a backdoor diamond possibility (needs runner-runner). Calling 600 into 2000 requires ~23% equity, and versus a wideish 3‑bet/c-bet range in heads‑up I have enough equity to continue and use position to control later streets. Raising as a bluff is too thin here (I don’t block enough of his value range and risk getting isolated by stronger hands). Plan: call the 600, see the turn, and only apply aggression if I pair the King, pick up a diamond, or see clear weakness; otherwise pot‑control or fold to heavy pressure.

The turn 2s pairs the board, which is generally a neutral card but unlikely to have improved Minni's range unless she's holding a speculative hand like A2s. Since Minni called my pre-flop 3-bet and my flop continuation bet, she likely has a made hand like a pair of 7s, 8s, or a medium pocket pair. With only Ace-high, I don't want to bloat the pot further without a made hand. Checking here allows me to pot-control, see a free river if she checks back, or evaluate her reaction to my perceived weakness.

Check. I have K♠6♦ with no pair and only a weak backdoor diamond; Flash’s line (3-bet pre, c-bet flop, then checking the paired turn) shows either missed overcards/draws or pot‑control/value with a medium hand or trap. I don’t want to build a bigger pot with a marginal hand and risk facing a shove or a river decision out of position. With position I’ll check and see the river — if he barrels a small bet I can call light sometimes, if he fires a large bet I’ll fold unless I pair the King or pick up clear equity. If he checks back we go to showdown cheaply or can bluff a safe river spot later when appropriate.

On the river 4h, the board remains relatively safe as the diamond draw did not complete. After the turn went check-check, it is likely that Minni has some showdown value like a weak pair (7x, 8x, 55, 66) or was on a draw. My Ace-Queen high has significant showdown value in heads-up play, but it is unlikely to get better hands to fold or worse hands to call if I bet. Therefore, checking is the most prudent action to reach showdown for free and potentially win against her missed draws or weaker Broadway cards like KJ or KQ.

Flash checked the river after c-betting the flop and checking the turn — a line that often represents missed overcards, busted diamond draws, or pot-control with a medium pair. I have K♠6♦ and no pair, but my 6♦ blocks some diamond draws and this is a good low-risk bluff spot. The pot is 2,600; a small bet of 900 (~35% pot) applies pressure to many of his checked-back hands (A‑high, missed draws, some thin showdown hands) while keeping my loss limited if called. I will bet and fold to any raise — I don’t want to overcommit with no showdown value, but I want to exploit his passive river check with a compact bluff-sized wager.

Minni's bet of 900 into a 2600 pot (approx. 35% pot) follows a check-check turn. This sizing often represents either thin value from a weak pair (7x, 8x, or small pocket pairs) or a bluff with missed draws such as diamonds or straight draws (65, 96). My Ace-Queen has showdown value against her bluffs and potential high-card air. Given the pot odds are roughly 4:1, I only need to be correct approximately 20% of the time to make this call profitable. In a heads-up match, Ace-high is frequently the winning hand in this specific line.

In a heads-up situation on the button, I hold Jd 4d. This is a suited hand that performs well in a wide opening range. Standard strategy is to raise a high percentage of buttons to put pressure on the big blind. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot pre-flop.

I’m in the big blind with 3♦2♠ facing a Button open to 300. Pot is 400 and it costs me 200 to call (200/(400+200) = 33.3% break-even equity). 3‑2 offsuit has very poor showdown equity vs a Button’s open range, has almost no backdoor flush potential (offsuit) and will be dominated by the kinds of hands the Button raises with. Post‑flop I’d be out of position and likely face frequent C‑bets from an aggressive opener — this hand plays terribly multi‑street OOP. A 3‑bet bluff is unattractive with these holdings (vulnerable to 4‑bets and thinly called). Preserve the healthy stack and wait for a better spot — fold here.

Heads-up on the button (I’ll have position postflop) with A♦2♦ — a strong heads-up hand: top-card ace, nut-flush potential and decent playability. Pot is 150 and it’s only 50 to call, but against Flash’s frequent wide opening/defending ranges I want to take the initiative and build the pot with position. I prefer a standard 3x BB open rather than a min-raise to gain fold equity and charge weaker hands while keeping the pot manageable with deep stacks (my ~153 BB vs his ~245 BB). If he folds I pick up the blinds; if he calls I’ll play A-high and flush/straight draws in position; if he 3-bets I can reasonably fold or re-evaluate depending on sizing. Raise to 300 is a balanced, low-commitment value/aggression play.

J9o is a solid hand to defend the big blind against a button raise in heads-up play. The pot odds are 2:1, which is favorable for a hand with this much playability. I will call the raise and see the flop.

I have flopped middle pair (Nines) on a King-high board. In a heads-up situation, middle pair is a solid hand, but given the presence of an overcard (King) and the fact that Minni was the pre-flop aggressor, checking is the most standard and prudent play. This allows me to see how Minni reacts to the board and helps control the pot size. If Minni bets, I will likely call to evaluate the turn, as my hand is too strong to fold to a single continuation bet but not strong enough to check-raise for value against a range that includes many Kings.

Flop: 9♦ 3♦ K♣. I hold A♦2♦ — nut-flush draw plus ace-high. I raised preflop, Flash called and checked this flop, so I have position and initiative to continue. This is a high-equity semi-bluff: ~19% to hit the flush on the turn and ~35% to make the flush by river; ~12% to pair an Ace by river, so ≈45% to either make the flush or pair an Ace by the river. Betting here achieves three things: (1) gets fold equity vs many of Flash’s wide checking hands, (2) charges inferior draws and weak pairs, and (3) builds a pot when I have the nut draw in position.

Sizing: pot is 600. I’ll make a half‑pot c-bet (~300). That size is big enough to fold out some marginal hands and small enough to keep worse hands and drawing hands in to realize my equity. Plan after action: if he folds I collect; if he calls I continue to realize my ~35% flush equity (and re-evaluate on the turn — barrel on favorable cards, pot-control on blanks); if he check-raises a large amount I’ll call reasonably sized raises with this nut draw (and re-evaluate/shove if the raise commits me), since the draw plus Ace-up has strong equity vs his raising range.

I have flopped middle pair (Nines) with a Jack kicker on a King-high board. In heads-up play, middle pair is a very strong hand that cannot be folded to a single continuation bet. The board also has a diamond flush draw, which Minni could be semi-bluffing with. By calling, I keep the pot controlled and allow myself to re-evaluate on the turn. My hand is strong enough to call but not strong enough to raise for value given the presence of the King.

On the turn, the Queen of Hearts is an overcard that improves many of Minni's potential pre-flop raising hands like KQ, QJ, or even J10 which now makes a straight. My pair of Nines has effectively been demoted to third pair. In this position, checking is the most appropriate action to control the pot and see how Minni proceeds. Betting would likely only get called by hands that have me beat, and checking allows me to potentially see a cheap river or evaluate a bet from Minni.

Turn: Q♥ checked to me. I hold A♦2♦ (nut diamond draw + Ace-high) and the pot is 1,200. I will bet 800 (~2/3 pot) as a semi-bluff and pot-building line. Rationale: (1) Equity — from the turn I have 9 diamond outs + 3 remaining aces = 12 outs, 12/46 ≈ 26% to improve to a flush or pair on the river, plus some current showdown equity vs floats and worse pairs. (2) Fold equity — a bet of 800 risks 800 to win 2,000, so I need Villain to fold >40% (800/(1200+800)=0.40) for a pure bluff to be profitable. Given Flash checked the turn after calling the flop, he likely has a number of marginal pairs and floats that will fold often enough to make this +EV. (3) Build value — if he calls I still have strong equity to realize on the river; if I hit a diamond I can extract a large value on the river. (4) Position & initiative — betting keeps pressure, denies free cards to hands that currently beat me, and leverages my button advantage. Plan: bet 800 now; if called, value-bet big on a diamond river; on a blank river re-evaluate (pot control or a small bluff depending on reads). If he check-raises a small/medium amount I will likely call with the draw; fold to an overly large shove if it represents a committed made-hand range that I can't reasonably beat.

On the turn, the Queen of Hearts is a poor card for my hand as it hits a significant portion of Minni's pre-flop raising range (KQ, QJ, QT). My pair of Nines, which was already behind a King, is now also behind any Queen. Minni's bet of 800 into a 1200 pot represents a strong range or a well-timed semi-bluff. Given the board texture and the presence of two overcards (K, Q) to my pair, the risk of being drawn out on or being significantly behind is too high to justify a call. I will fold to preserve my stack and wait for a more favorable situation.

In heads-up play, Jack-Nine offsuit is a strong hand that is well within the top half of starting hands. As the chip leader on the button, I will put in a standard raise to 300 to take control of the pot and pressure the big blind.

Heads-up vs the button (Flash) who opened to 300 from SB. I hold Kh Js — a strong heads-up hand (top/broadway potential) that's ahead of a lot of a button's wide opening range but can be dominated by the very strongest hands. Versus a 3x open I want to seize the initiative and get value/fold equity rather than just calling and allowing the button to c-bet for free. A 3-bet to 1000 (~3.3x the open) is large enough to fold out weaker broadways/suited connectors and isolate heads-up, yet small enough that I can comfortably fold to a very large 4-bet or proceed postflop with deep stacks (15.9k vs 23.7k). Min-raising to 500 would be too small to achieve meaningful fold equity. If Flash calls I'll have position-dependent postflop decisions and can continue on favorable boards; if he folds I pick up the pot immediately. Plan: 3-bet to 1000 for value and fold equity, fold to a strong 4-bet.

I am holding Jack-Nine offsuit on the button and opened for a standard raise of 300. Minni has responded with a 3-bet to 1000. While Jack-Nine is a decent starting hand in heads-up play, it struggles against a 3-bet range that typically includes superior high cards and pocket pairs. Minni has shown in previous hands (like Hand #74 and Hand #103) that she is willing to use larger sizing with strong holdings or to put pressure on me. Given the 3.3x size of her raise and the fact that I would be calling with a hand that is likely dominated, I will choose the disciplined route and fold, preserving my chip lead and waiting for a better spot to engage.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with 4♠10♣ — a marginal hand but playable in position. Pot is 150 and I only need to add 50 to complete to the BB (total commitment 100), which is ~3:1 pot odds and worth seeing a flop. Flash (BB) defends widely and is capable of aggression; I don't want to bloat the pot with a weak offsuit hand by open‑raising. I call to realize equity and take advantage of positional advantage on the flop. Postflop plan: continue only with a ten/4 or strong draws; c-bet thin on very dry boards for fold equity but control pot size on connected/overcard textures and fold to large resistance when missed.

I am in the Big Blind with 6-2 offsuit. Minni, on the Button/Small Blind, has just called the 100-chip blind. Since I am already in for the full 100 and there is no additional cost to see the flop, I will check to see if I can improve my hand for free. Raising with such a weak hand would be unnecessarily risky.

I have bottom pair (sixes) on a King-high board. In a limped pot, this is a reasonable hand to take to a showdown, but not strong enough to bet for value. Checking is the standard play here to keep the pot small and see what Minni does.

Heads‑up on the button with T♣4♠ versus a checked big blind on K♣7♠6♣. I have no pair and only a very weak backdoor possibility (one club in hand with two clubs on board — runner/runner only), but in heads‑up play the BB defends a very wide range and checks a lot. A half‑pot c‑bet (100 into 200) buys the pot frequently, keeps the pot small if called, and preserves fold equity while representing a range that includes many Kx and club combos. Plan: bet to take it down now; if Flash calls I proceed cautiously and only continue with a ten or a club on the turn; if Flash check‑raises I fold given my poor equity.

I have bottom pair (sixes) on a King-high board. In a limped pot, this is a reasonable hand to continue with against a small probe bet. Minni's bet of 100 into a 200-chip pot is small, and I am getting 3-to-1 odds to call. Raising here would likely only be called by better hands (Kings or Sevens), so calling is the most efficient way to maintain pot control and see if I can improve or reach showdown.

I have bottom pair (sixes) on a board with two overcards (King and Queen). In a pot that was limped pre-flop and where the action has been relatively passive, my hand has some showdown value but is not strong enough to bet. Checking is the most appropriate move to control the size of the pot and see how Minni responds.

I check. I c-bet small on the flop and was called by a very wide BB range. The Q♦ on the turn is a blank for my continuation plan — it’s not a 10 or a club — so my earlier criteria to continue aren’t met. I have no pair and only a runner‑runner club possibility, so betting now would be a pure bluff with very little fold equity and would bloat a pot I can’t comfortably win. With deep stacks and a 400‑chip pot, pot control is the correct play: check and see the river. Plan for the river: fold to significant aggression unless I pair my Ten or the river gives a real draw/clear bluffing spot; otherwise use thin value or check to showdown depending on the card.

On this board of K-7-6-Q-A, my pair of sixes is now the fourth pair. Minni limped pre-flop, bet the flop, and checked back the turn. This suggests she might have a marginal hand like a seven or a weak king, or perhaps she was on a draw. Given the Ace on the river, it is unlikely I can bet for value, as I will only be called by better hands. However, I have some showdown value against missed draws or total bluffs. Checking is the most prudent play to see a showdown for free in this small pot.

Board: 7♠ 6♣ K♣ Q♦ A♥. I hold T♣4♠ — no pair, no meaningful draw. Flash (BB) called my small flop bet, so their range contains a lot of Kx/Qx/Ax, small pairs (7/6), and missed club draws; many of those hands beat me at showdown. The turn was checked by both and the river A is another overcard that improves a large portion of their calling range while not giving me any extra equity. Betting here would be a thin bluff with limited fold equity against a wide but mostly beating range, and would bloat a pot I’m a large dog to win. With deep stacks and only a 400‑chip pot, pot control is correct: check to showdown and preserve my stack. If Flash leads out for a meaningful amount on the river I will fold.

I am on the button with J9o. In a heads-up situation, J9o is a strong enough hand to open for a raise to maintain pressure and take control of the pot. A standard 3x raise to 300 has been my consistent sizing throughout the match and should be effective here given my stack lead.

Heads-up in the big blind vs a standard button open to 300. Q♠4♠ is marginal but has suit and postflop playability against a wide opening range. Calling 200 (total commitment 300) is the best option: the call is cheap relative to deep stacks and preserves pot control OOP. I considered a min‑3bet to 500, but Q4s is too weak a hand to build a big pot or to 3-bet-bluff reliably against this opponent. Plan: see the flop and pursue flush/backdoor equity or top‑queen improvement; fold to heavy aggression if I miss and face significant pressure. If I hit a draw or pair, play aggressively; otherwise keep the pot small.

Heads‑up OOP on a dry, rainbow 6♦7♥T♣ flop with Q♠4♠ — I have two overcards and backdoor spade potential but no pair or meaningful draw. Flash opened wide preflop and will c‑bet frequently; facing that, I prefer pot control and to realize equity rather than barreling OOP with a marginal hand. Check keeps the pot small, lets me see a free turn, and allows me to call a standard small/medium c‑bet (I’ll call reasonable sizing around <= ~40% pot) but fold to heavy aggression or a raise. If I improve (pair the Q or pick up a spade) I can start betting for value or apply pressure on later streets; if I miss and face big pressure I can release.

I have a gutshot straight draw needing an 8, along with two overcards. Since Minni checked to me after calling my pre-flop raise, a continuation bet is the standard play here to maintain initiative and potentially win the pot immediately. A bet of 350, which is slightly over half the pot, is consistent with my previous betting patterns and applies pressure on a relatively coordinated board.

Heads‑up OOP on 6♦7♥T♣ with Q♠4♠. I checked to keep the pot small and Flash c‑bet small (350 into 950 ≈ 37% pot). Q4s has two overcards and backdoor spade potential but no pair or a real draw yet. Villain opens and c‑bets a wide range frequently, so folding to a small c‑bet would be too tight and a raise is too ambitious with this hand. Calling preserves pot control, realizes equity, and keeps options for the turn: if I hit a Q or pick up spade equity I can play aggressively; if the turn is a blank and Flash barrels large (or raises), I can fold. Call is the best balance of pot control and equity realization here.

Turn brings J♥. I hold Q♠4♠ — no pair and my backdoor spade possibility is gone (turn is not a spade), so I have only 3 outs (three queens) to improve on the river (~3/46 ≈ 6.5%). Pot is 1,300 and Villain is a wide, aggressive opener who c‑bet the flop. With almost no equity and out of position, I want to keep the pot small and avoid building a pot I’m a big underdog in. Checking preserves pot control and allows me to fold to a turn barrel. Concretely: I will check; if Flash bets a meaningful amount I will fold (given 3 outs I need ~11:1 pot odds — with P=1,300 a call is only break‑even vs bets ≲ ~90 chips), so I’ll only entertain calling very tiny probes (≈≤100). If Flash checks back I’ll evaluate the river and only continue with a made queen or a very specific bluff spot. No raise or lead now — too weak and too many worse cases (pairs, two‑pair, JT, Jx, Tx) that beat me.

On the turn, the Jack of Hearts improves my hand to top pair (Jacks) with a gutshot straight draw (needing an 8 for a 7-8-9-10-J straight). Minni has checked for the second time this hand. This is a good opportunity to bet for value and protection. I want to get paid by weaker hands like a Ten, a Seven, or various draws (like an 8 or 9-8) that might have called the flop. Betting also prevents Minni from realizing equity for free. A bet of 850 into a 1300 pot (approximately 65% pot) is a standard sizing to apply pressure and build the pot with what is likely the best hand.

Fold. I have Q♠4♠ with no pair on the turn and no backdoor flush after J♥ — only three outs (the remaining Queens), ~3/46 ≈ 6.5% to improve on the river. The pot was 1,300 before the turn bet; Flash-sized a ~650%? no — he bet 850 (~65% of the pot), making the current pot 2,150. Calling 850 would commit 850 to win a final pot of 3,000, so break-even equity needed ≈ 850/3,000 ≈ 28.3%, which is far above my ~6.5% actual equity. Raising (min-raise to 1,700) would be a large, high-variance bluff with poor blockers and is unlikely to be +EV versus Flash’s calling range (Jx, Tx, two-pair, sets, and some calls). Although Flash is aggressive and bluffs sometimes, this sizing is too large to continue with only three outs. I will fold and wait for a better spot to invest chips.

Heads-up on the button with J♠9♦ — a playable hand in position. Pot is 150 and the call is only 50, but I want the initiative and fold equity; a standard 3x BB open is consistent with table dynamics and reduces the frequency of aggressive 3-bets. I’ll raise to 300 total to steal the blind or play postflop in position. If Flash 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold or take a cautious line given stack depths); if called I’ll c-bet favorable flops and use position to extract value or fold when clearly beaten.

I am holding King of Clubs and Four of Clubs in the Big Blind. Minni has raised to 3BB (300). In a heads-up match, a suited King is well within the defending range, and I am getting 2:1 on a call. Given my stack lead and the playability of suited hands post-flop, calling is the standard and most prudent play here.

On a monotone board of nine of spades, deuce of spades, and ten of spades, I have no spades in my hand and no direct connection to the board with King of Clubs and Four of Clubs. Being out of position against Minni, the most standard play is to check and see how she reacts to this highly coordinated texture. If she bets, I have very little equity to continue and will likely look for a better spot to utilize my chip lead.

I check. I raised preflop with J♠9♦ and on 9♠-2♠-10♠ I only have a pair of nines with a J kicker and a single spade. This is a very wet, monotone board that heavily favours the caller’s defending range (many two-spade combos make a nut/near-nut flush, and tens/Jx beat my pair). With deep stacks I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position facing possible made flushes or big turns. Checking controls the pot and lets me use position to see what Flash does. Plan: if Flash checks back I’ll evaluate the turn and often bet thin for value on safe bricks; if Flash bets a small/medium size (≈≤1/2 pot) I’ll call to continue and re-evaluate on the turn; if Flash leads out big or check-raises I’ll fold most of the time because that line strongly represents a flush or better pair.

On a four-flush board where I hold no spades, my hand has zero showdown value against any hand containing a single spade. Since Minni checked back the flop, it is possible she does not have a high spade, but betting into a four-spade board without one is highly risky, as she could easily call with any spade or even a better high card if she suspects a bluff. Checking is the most prudent play to see a river for free and avoid inflating the pot with no equity.

I have J♠9♦ on 9♠-2♠-10♠-6♠ — I actually have a J‑high spade flush. That’s strong heads‑up but vulnerable to A♠/K♠/Q♠ combos that beat me. Flash checked flop and turn, so his range includes both missed hands/pairs and medium/strong spades (and some traps). I want to get value from worse spades and pairs (10x, 9x, lower spades) and charge any weak holdings — while keeping the bet small enough that worse hands will call but large enough to fold some pure bluffs. Betting ~2/3 pot (400 into 600) is the right balance. If Flash check‑raises very large I’ll give credit to a nuttier spade and fold/re‑evaluate; if he calls I’ll shove or value‑bet appropriately on the river depending on the card.

The board now shows four spades, and I hold no spades in my hand, meaning I have zero equity against any hand containing even a single spade. Minni checked the flop but is now betting 400 into 600 on the turn when the fourth spade arrived. While it is possible she is bluffing or betting a non-spade hand, I have no showdown value with King-high and no draw to improve. Calling is unprofitable as I would be purely guessing, and raising as a bluff is extremely risky on a four-flush board. I will fold and preserve my substantial chip lead.

I am holding 98 of diamonds, which is a strong suited connector in heads-up play. Since I am on the button (small blind), I have a positional advantage for the rest of the hand. Raising is the standard play here to put pressure on the big blind and build a pot with a hand that has good post-flop playability. I will raise to 300 chips.

Heads-up I should defend the big blind fairly wide. 8-7 offsuit is a playable hand against a standard 3x raise — it has decent connectivity and implied odds with these deep stacks. Calling 200 more (300 total committed) gives good pot odds and keeps the pot manageable out of position. A small 3-bet here would bloat the pot OOP with a marginal hand; folding is too tight in HU. Plan: call, evaluate the flop texture — proceed aggressively with strong pairs or strong draws, pot-control or check-fold on dry, unfavorable boards.

Heads-up I flopped middle pair (7s) with an 8 kicker on J-7-2. It’s a decent but vulnerable hand OOP — behind any Jx and beat by two-pair or sets. With deep stacks I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal made hand. Checking controls the pot, keeps worse hands and bluffs in that I can call, and lets me reevaluate on the turn. Plan: check now; call small-to-medium continuation bets to realize equity, proceed cautiously vs large aggression (fold to extreme sizing or raise/commit only with clear equity).

On this dry J-7-2 rainbow board, I have a backdoor flush draw and some backdoor straight potential. As the pre-flop aggressor, I will place a standard continuation bet of 50% pot to put pressure on Minni's calling range and attempt to take the pot down immediately.

Heads-up out of position with 8h7s on J♠7♦2♥. I have middle pair with a weak kicker — a hand worth seeing another street with but not strong enough to inflate the pot OOP. Flash’s 300 into ~600 is a standard half-pot c-bet from a wide HU range (lots of Jx, some overcards, bluffs, small pairs). Folding is overly tight; raising OOP would bloat the pot with a marginal holding. I will call 300 to realize equity, keep worse hands and bluffs in, and re-evaluate on the turn. Plan on pot control: fold to very large turn aggression unless I improve (two-pair/sets) or get clear pot-odds to continue; call small-to-medium turns and look for value or a cheaper showdown.

Heads-up out of position with 8♥7♠ on 7♦ J♠ 2♥ T♦ — I have middle pair with a weak kicker. The Ten on the turn helps the obvious parts of Flash’s continuing range (Jx, Tx, some two-pair combos) and there are no strong draws to fear, so I have very little fold equity and a hand I want to keep the pot small with. Pot-control is the right line: checking keeps worse pairs and bluffs in, avoids bloating the pot OOP, and lets me call a small/medium bet to see a cheap river. Plan: check here. If Flash leads a small sizing on the river I’ll call to realize equity; fold to very large bets or raises that represent two pair/sets.

On the turn Ten of Diamonds, my hand (9d 8d) has picked up significant equity. I now have an open-ended straight draw (needing a 6 or a Q) and a flush draw (needing any diamond). With 15 clean outs to a likely winning hand, I have approximately 32% equity against a made hand like a Jack. Since Minni checked to me, I will place a semi-bluff bet of approximately 70% of the pot. This exerts pressure on her one-pair hands and allows me to take the pot down immediately or build a large pot for the river if I hit one of my many outs.

I have 8♥7♠ on J♠-7♦-2♥-T♦ — middle pair with a weak kicker. The pot was ~1200 before Flash’s turn lead of 850, so calling 850 asks for ~29% equity (850 into a 2050 pot). Flash has been opening and c-betting widely, so his turn range will contain a lot of value (Jx, Tx, overpairs, occasional sets) but also some missed broadway/overcard bluffs. My hand is vulnerable to Jx and Tx, but it also fares OK against pure bluffs and small pairs. Raising OOP would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and fold is likely too nitty given the pot odds and Flash’s aggression frequency. I’ll call 850 to realize equity and keep worse hands and bluffs in; on the river I’ll fold to large bets unless I pair my 8 or hit a 7 (two-pair/trips) or the sizing/line suggests a bluff — I’ll call small/medium river bets for pot control and show down if checked to.

I have 8♥7♠ — middle pair (sevens) with a weak kicker on 7♦ J♠ 2♥ T♦ 5♣. Pot is 2,900 and Flash (wide HU opener) bet the turn (850) and I called; now he checked the river. His line (c-bet then turn stab, then river check) is consistent with a lot of Jx/Tx/pot-control hands and some thin value or missed bluffs. I beat only pure bluffs and some overcards; I lose to the majority of his continuing value range. Betting as a bluff is thin — to make a half‑pot or ~1,500 bluff profitable I'd need ≈34% fold equity (1500/(2900+1500)), which is unlikely vs an aggressive, calling HU opponent who showed willingness to call down on earlier streets. With a vulnerable made hand and free showdown available, the lowest-variance, highest-EV line is to check and see a showdown. If he now leads a small probe I’ll consider a fold vs large sizing and only call very small bets; but my action here is to check and preserve stack rather than turn a marginal holding into a risky bluff.

I have completed a Jack-high straight (7-8-9-T-J) using both of my hole cards (9-8) and three cards from the board (7-10-J). This is the absolute nuts on this board texture, as no higher straight, flush, or full house is possible. Minni has called both the flop and turn bets, suggesting she likely holds a Jack or a Ten. Since she checked to me on the river, I will place a strong value bet of 2000 chips into the 2900 pot, aiming to get a call from her top-pair or middle-pair hands.

I fold. I have 8♥7♠ for one pair (sevens) on J♠-7♦-2♥-T♦-5♣. The pot is 4,900 and Flash's river bet is 2,000, so calling would require ~29% equity (2,000 / 6,900). Flash opened wide preflop, c-bet the flop and barreled the turn, then makes a large, polarizing ~70% pot river bet. That line is heavy in value (Jx, Tx, occasional sets/two‑pair and completed straights like 9‑8) and contains only a limited number of bluffs my hand can beat. My 8 and 7 block only a few straight/7 combos and don’t materially improve my odds to the ~29% needed. Raising would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and calling is a thin, high‑variance play against an opponent who has shown willingness to value-bet. Preserving chips and folding to this large river sizing is the +EV, low‑variance decision.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with A♦9♠ — a strong heads-up hand. With stacks deep (≈142bb vs 256bb) I want initiative and fold equity while keeping the pot size reasonable for postflop play. A standard 3x BB open (raise to 300 total) extracts value from worse hands, pressures the BB, and defines my range. Calling (100 total) gives the BB easy odds and cedes initiative; min-raising to 200 is okay but 300 is a clearer, standard sizing. I’ll raise to 300 total (I’ve posted 50 SB, so this is +250).

I have Ten-Six of hearts in the Big Blind against a 3x open from Minni. I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call (200 to call into a 400 pot), and T6s is a hand that plays reasonably well in a heads-up environment, especially given the stack depths and my chip lead. I will call to see a flop.

I have middle pair (Sixes) on an Ace-high board. This board favors Minni's pre-flop raising range. I will check to see Minni's reaction. If Minni bets a standard amount, I have enough equity to call at least one street. Leading here doesn't accomplish much as I want to keep her bluffs in and minimize losses against an Ace.

Heads-up on a dry, two-diamond flop I have top pair with A♦9♠ (board 2♦ A♣ 6♦). My A♦ is a valuable blocker to strong diamond combos and I have range advantage as preflop raiser. Flash checked, so a half-pot c-bet (~300 into the 600 pot) extracts value from worse A-x, medium pairs and draws while denying correct odds to many turn/river chase plans. Bet size keeps the pot controllable; if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and proceed to protect or control the pot. If I face a large check-raise I’ll fold or proceed cautiously depending on sizing and reads.

I have middle pair (sixes) on an Ace-high board. Minni's 300 bet into a 600 pot is a standard continuation bet on this board texture. In heads-up play, middle pair is far too strong to fold to a single small bet, especially since I beat all of her bluffs and semi-bluffs. I will call to see how the turn develops and evaluate if she continues her aggression on later streets.

I have middle pair (sixes) on a board with an Ace. The nine of clubs on the turn is mostly a blank, though it is an overcard. In heads-up play, middle pair is a solid 'bluff catcher' or marginal showdown hand. I will check to the pre-flop aggressor to see if she continues her story of having an Ace or if she checks back. Leading out here doesn't serve much purpose as I'm unlikely to fold out better or get value from worse.

Turn: I hold A♦9♠ on 2♦ A♣ 6♦ 9♣ — that’s two pair (Aces and Nines), a very strong heads-up holding. Flash’s line (preflop call, flop check-call of my 300 c-bet, then checking the turn) maps to a range of Ax, medium pairs (6x), pocket pairs, and diamond draws. I want to extract value from worse A-x, 6x and pocket pairs while charging missed draws, but I don’t want to overcommit versus the relatively unlikely set of 9s. A 2/3-pot turn bet (~800 into 1200) achieves that: big enough to get calls from worse hands and fold equity against bluffs/draws, but not so huge that a check-raise by a set auto-commits me. If Flash check-raises large I’ll strongly consider he has a set and will re-evaluate (likely fold to an over-aggressive shove, call smaller raises), but if he simply calls I’ll re-evaluate river for additional value.

In heads-up play, a middle pair like sixes on an Ace-high board is a significant holding. While Minni's 800-chip bet on the turn represents strength or a committed semi-bluff (such as a flush draw), middle pair functions well as a bluff catcher. The nine is an overcard but doesn't change the board state dramatically for an Ace-high range. I am getting 2.5:1 on a call, and folding here would be overly tight and exploitable in a heads-up match. I will call the turn bet and evaluate the river, looking to reach a showdown cheaply or catch a bluff if the board doesn't get more coordinated.

I have middle pair (sixes) on a board of A-6-2-9-5. While an Ace is definitely in Minni's range as the pre-flop aggressor, she could also be double-barreling with various draws (like diamonds or clubs) that have now missed. My hand has decent showdown value in a heads-up match, but it is not strong enough to bet for value. Checking is the best option to either reach a cheap showdown if she checks back or to evaluate a potential bluff-catching opportunity if she bets.

I have A♦9♠ for two pair (Aces and Nines) on 2♦ A♣ 6♦ 9♣ 5♠. Villain’s line (BB: preflop call, flop check-call of my 300 c-bet, turn check-call of my 800) looks fairly passive — consistent with worse A‑x, medium pairs (6x), pocket pairs, or missed diamond draws. Very few hands (sets like 22 or 66) beat me and those are uncommon; I also block many strong diamond combos by holding the A♦. With a strong but not invulnerable holding in heads‑up, I want value from worse A‑x and second‑pair/mid pairs while keeping worse hands and missed draws in. A half‑pot value bet (~1400 into 2800) is the right balance: large enough to extract value and charge draws, small enough to get called by worse hands and not to overfold them. If I face a large raise I’ll re‑evaluate (sets are the main worry), but betting for value here is +EV.

I have middle pair (sixes) on an Ace-high board. While Minni has represented strength by betting three streets, the board is relatively disconnected and many draws (diamonds and clubs) have missed. Her river bet is 1/3 of the pot, giving me 3:1 odds. In heads-up play, middle pair is a strong bluff-catcher against a range that includes missed semi-bluffs. I will call to see the showdown.

I am on the button with A2o. In heads-up play, an Ace is a very strong starting hand. Minni has already put in 100 as the big blind. I will raise to 300 total to take control of the pot and apply pressure, as A2o is well ahead of an average random hand.

I have A♥ K♠ in the big blind facing a single open to 300 from the button/SB. Heads-up AK is a premium — I want to take the initiative, get value from worse Ax/Kx and deny cheap flops to a wide opening range. I’ll 3-bet to a standard size (1050 total) — big enough to generate fold equity and isolate, but small enough to leave plenty of postflop maneuvering room with deep stacks. If Flash calls I’ll proceed postflop for value and position reads; if he 4-bets I’ll re-evaluate (AK can call or 5-bet shove versus light 4-bets, fold to very large/clear 4-bets).

In heads-up play, A2o is a relatively strong starting hand. After I opened to 300, Minni 3-bet to 1050. While A2o is easily dominated by better Aces, it still holds significant equity against a wide 3-bet range in a heads-up match. I am in position, which allows me to play more effectively post-flop. I will call the 3-bet to see a flop and utilize my positional advantage, rather than folding or turning a hand with an Ace blocker into a 4-bet bluff.

I 3‑bet preflop with A♥K♠, so my range is strong and I should continue representing strength. The flop Qc‑7c‑6s gives me two overcards and no pair, and Flash’s cold‑call preflop can include many Qx, mid pocket pairs and some club holdings. I want to seize the initiative, deny free cards to his wide calling range, and fold out some worse hands (small pairs, Kx, Jx and unpaired broadways). A standard half‑to‑two‑thirds pot c‑bet is appropriate here — it applies pressure but keeps the pot controllable if I face resistance. I’ll bet 1,200 into the 2,100 pot (~57%). If he calls I can continue with equity to improve; if he raises I’ll re‑evaluate based on sizing (call a small raise to see a turn, fold or proceed cautiously to large/committed 3‑bets).

On a flop of Qc-7c-6s, I have Ace-high with no direct draws or significant equity. Minni, who 3-bet pre-flop, has led out with a substantial bet of 1200 into a 2100 pot. This board texture hits a 3-betting range quite well (overpairs, top pair with high kickers). My Ace-high is likely behind, and I have very little potential to improve to a winning hand on future streets. Continuing here would be a mistake against a strong range. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my chip lead.

Heads-up with the button/SB I want to be aggressive and take the initiative. Th4h is a playable, suited hand in heads-up — good equity vs a wide BB defending range and gives flush/straight/backdoor possibilities. The table standard open size here has been ~300, so I’ll raise to 300 (3x BB) to apply pressure and get fold equity while risking a very small portion of my ~18k stack. If Flash 3-bets large I’ll fold this marginal holding; if they call I’ll use position to continuation-bet appropriate flops and realize my equity. Calling 50 is too passive and misses the opportunity to steal the blind.

I am in the big blind with Ad8d, a strong suited Ace. Minni (the button/SB) has raised to 300. In a heads-up situation, Ad8d is well ahead of an average button opening range. While I could 3-bet for value, calling is also a very strong option to keep the pot controlled and play post-flop with a hand that has great playability and nut potential. However, given our stack depths and my position, I want to define the range and take the lead or take the pot down now. I will raise to 1,000 to put pressure on Minni and build a pot with a premium starting hand.

I opened to 300 on the button with T♥4♥ but Flash 3‑bet to 1000. Calling 700 into a 1300 pot requires ~35% equity (700/(1300+700)=35%), and T4s is a marginal holding that will frequently be dominated or have poor multi‑street equity versus a 3‑bet range. My prior plan was to fold this hand to a large 3‑bet; 4‑betting is out of the question. Preserve stack, wait for a better spot — fold.

I am on the button with a very weak hand, 42o. While the stack sizes are deep and I am in position, 42o is generally a fold in most heads-up ranges from the small blind unless the opponent is playing extremely tight and allowing a high frequency of steals. Looking at previous hands, Minni has been quite active and willing to call or 3-bet. Given the current hand count and my chip lead, there is no need to play such a weak hand out of position for the remainder of the hand. I will fold.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) I should be opening a very wide range. 8♥7♠ is a perfect hand to raise with in position — connected, good playability and strong implied odds with stacks this deep (17.8k). The pot is 150 and it’s 50 to call; I’ll take the initiative and min-raise to 200. That size (the stated min-raise) gives fold equity, denies a cheap flop to the BB, keeps the pot controllable, and preserves maneuverability postflop: c‑bet favorable flops, check/ pot‑control on bad/highly coordinated boards, and re-evaluate if Flash 3-bets.

I am in the big blind with 7c 4s and Minni has raised to 200 from the small blind/button. It's a small raise, requiring only 100 more to call into a pot of 300 (giving me 3:1 odds). However, 7-4 offsuit is an extremely weak hand. While I have the stack depth to defend, playing out of position with such a disconnected hand against an aggressive opponent like Minni often leads to difficult spots on later streets. Given the goal is to maximize stack over 150 hands and I am currently leading in chips, I will choose to fold this weak hand and wait for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with 7h5d, which is a weak speculative hand. However, we are heads-up and I have the positional advantage. Minni has been playing relatively aggressively, but has folded to pre-flop pressure several times. A standard 3x open (300 total) here is a reasonable play to try and take the blinds or control the pot with position, but given the hand strength and the fact that I am in the Small Blind, a fold is also acceptable. However, to maintain an aggressive image and utilize position, I will raise.

I'm in the big blind with Q♦8♠ facing a button open to 300 (pot 400, I must call 200). In heads-up play the opener's range is wide, and Q8o has reasonable equity against that range. The immediate pot odds (~200 to win ~600 total after my call) justify seeing a flop. I am out of position postflop, so I don't want to 3-bet with a marginal, often-dominated hand and inflate the pot unnecessarily. I'll call to 300 total to see a flop and proceed only with favorable boards (top pair, good draws); fold or play cautiously if I miss and face heavy action.

Check. I completely missed this dry, paired board with Q♦8♠ (no pair, no real draw). The 2–3–3 texture is low and often invites continuation bets, but I’m out of position and don’t want to inflate the pot with a marginal hand. Best line is to control the pot: check and see what Flash does. If Flash checks back I get a free turn to improve; if Flash fires a small c‑bet (roughly ≤1/3 pot ≈ 200) I can call occasionally to realize equity and look for a pair on the turn, but I’ll fold to larger c‑bets. I’ll avoid bluff check‑raises OOP here — preserve chips and wait for a better spot.

The flop of 2d-3c-3s is a very dry, paired board that is unlikely to have improved Minni's pre-flop calling range significantly. As the pre-flop aggressor, a continuation bet is standard here to put pressure on her hands that missed the board, such as broadway cards or high-card air. I will bet 300, which is half the pot, to represent strength and attempt to take down the pot immediately.

Flash (button) made a small c‑bet: 300 into a 900 pot on 2♦‑3♣‑3♠. I’m out of position with Q♦8♠ and have missed the board, but the bet is only ~1/3 pot. Calling 300 gives me a pot of ~1200 and requires ~25% equity to break even (300/1200). Versus a wide heads‑up c‑bet range I have roughly that equity and can still realize showdown value — folding would be too nitty and raising OOP with no pair or strong draw is a poor, high‑variance play. Plan: call to see the turn, fold to a large turn barrel if I still have nothing, and re-evaluate if I pick up showdown value or a favorable action.

I have Q♦8♠ on 2♦-3♣-3♠-6♦. I called the flop c-bet to see the turn and still have no pair and no real draw. I’m out of position and stacks are deep, so I want to keep the pot under control rather than bluff or inflate with nothing. Checking preserves chips and lets Flash show aggression — if he checks back I get a free river to improve; if he bets a small amount (~≤1/3 pot) I can consider a cheap call to see the river, but I’ll fold to a larger barrel. Raising or leading OOP with no showdown value or draw is unnecessary and high variance. Check and re-evaluate on the river.

On a board of 2d-3c-3s-6d, the turn 6d has given me a gutshot straight draw (any 4 makes a straight). Minni checked to me after calling my pre-flop raise and flop continuation bet. Given the board is still very dry and paired, her flop-calling range likely includes Ace-high or small pocket pairs. While a second barrel could exert pressure, checking back here allows me to realize my equity for free and potentially hit the nut straight on the river. If I miss the river and she checks again, I can re-evaluate a bluffing opportunity then. For now, checking is the most prudent play to control the pot.

The river is 2♦-3♣-3♠-6♦-J♦ and I hold Q♦8♠ — just queen-high with no pair and no completed flush. Pot is 1,200 and there is nothing to call. Flash opened wide preflop, fired a small flop c‑bet and then checked the turn and river; that line shows a lot of missed hands but also includes any made pair (2x, 3x), a jack, or two‑diamond combos that now make a flush. A river bluff would need substantial fold equity (roughly >40% if I sized ~2/3 pot) to be +EV, which I don’t trust here given the line and heads‑up calling tendencies. With deep stacks and little to gain, the lowest‑variance, highest‑EV play is to check and take my queen‑high to showdown rather than bluffing into likely callers or risking a raise.

I have 7-high and no showdown value. Minni has checked on the flop, turn, and river, suggesting a weak-to-medium hand like a small pocket pair or Ace-high. By betting 600 into the 1200 pot, I am representing a hand that improved on the river (like a Jack) or a slow-played overpair. This pressure should be enough to force her to fold her marginal holdings.

I checked earlier to keep the pot small with Q♦8♠ on 2♦-3♣-3♠-6♦-J♦. Flash’s line (wide HU open, small flop c-bet, check turn, small river stab of 600) contains a fair number of missed overcards and thin value hands. The pot odds are favorable — calling 600 to win the pot (~1800) requires ~25% equity. My queen-high has some showdown value vs a wide river-betting range and also blocks some diamond/high combos. Raising OOP with no pair or a real draw would be a pure bluff with poor fold equity; folding surrenders too much when he bluffs with reasonable frequency. I’ll call the 600 to realize showdown equity.

Heads-up on the button (I act first preflop but will have positional advantage postflop). 7♣6♥ is a loose but playable, connected hand that plays well in position and has reasonable equity against a wide BB defense. I want to seize the initiative and apply pressure rather than passively completing to 100. A minimum-sized raise to 200 (total) preserves deep stacks, gives good fold equity against Flash’s often-wide calling/raising tendencies in these games, and keeps pot-building controlled if he calls. If he 3-bets big I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold), but if he calls I can play the flop in position. Therefore raise to 200 total.

I am in the big blind with 9c 6c, a respectable suited semi-connector. Minni has opened from the button for a small raise of 2x (200 total). I need to call 100 more into a pot of 300, giving me 3:1 odds. Given the suited nature of my hand and the pot odds provided, this is a standard defend in a heads-up situation to see a flop.

On this paired board of Ac-6s-Ad, I have flopped two pair (Aces and Sixes) with a 9 kicker. While I have a piece of the board, the presence of two Aces makes it a polar board where my opponent either has an Ace (and I am crushed) or is likely bluffing/holding a smaller pair. Since I am first to act, checking is the standard and most effective play. It allows me to see how Minni reacts and keeps the pot manageable while I have some showdown value. I intend to check-call most reasonable bets to see if my pair of sixes can hold up or improve.

Board is Ac 6s Ad and I hold 7c6h — middle pair (sixes) on a double-A board. This texture strongly favors the caller’s range (many A‑x hands that crush my pair), so a continuation bet here would be thin value and exposes me to a costly check‑raise or big turn action from Ax. With position I prefer pot control and to realize equity against worse hands (Kx, Qx, small pairs) rather than inflate the pot out of position versus likely Aces. Plan: check and re-evaluate on the turn — call small to medium bets to see a cheap showdown, fold to large aggression, and look to extract thin value or bluff on later streets when the board or action is favorable.

The board has paired for the second time, providing three Aces. With my 6c and the 6s on the board, I have improved to a full house, Aces full of Sixes. Since Minni checked back on the flop, it is unlikely she holds the final Ace (which would give her Quads). By checking, I am giving her the opportunity to bluff or bet with a wider range of hands that I currently beat, such as King-high or a lower pocket pair. This strategy maximizes the potential to extract value from a range that might fold to a lead bet.

The turn just improved my hand: with 7♣6♥ and the board Ac 6s Ad Ah I now have a full house (Aces full of sixes, AAA66). That’s almost certainly the best hand except in the rare case Flash holds the remaining A♠ (quads). Flash checked the flop and checked the turn — which could be weakness or a trap. I want to extract value from the very small number of hands that can call (any 6x/full-house combos or stubborn pairs) while keeping the pot controlled against the remote quads possibility. A small-to-medium bet will get value and price out nothing important; a half-pot bet is a good compromise. Bet half-pot (200) to get called by worse and keep pot manageable if Flash jams or check-raises.

With four Aces on the board (Ac, Ad, Ah, and the implied fourth from the context of a 4-of-a-kind board), the hand is essentially a kicker battle. My best five-card hand is Quad Aces with a Nine kicker (A-A-A-A-9). Minni's bet of 200 into a 400 pot is a half-pot wager. I only lose if Minni holds a King, Queen, Jack, or Ten. If she has any card lower than a Nine, I win; if she has a Nine, we chop. Additionally, if the river is any card higher than a Nine, the board's fifth card will likely result in a split pot unless one of us holds an even higher card. Given the high probability of a split or having the best kicker against a button-opening range that includes many small cards, calling is the correct play to see the river.

On a board of Ac-6s-Ad-Ah-5c, everyone has Quad Aces. My best five-card hand is A-A-A-A-9. I am currently out of position on the river. Since there are four Aces on the board, this hand is entirely dependent on the kicker. My Nine is a decent kicker, but it is vulnerable to any Ten, Jack, Queen, or King. If Minni has any of those cards, she wins the entire pot; if she has a Nine, we chop; and if she has any card lower than a Nine, I win. Given that Minni opened from the button and checked the flop but bet the turn when the third Ace appeared, she could easily be holding a high card kicker. Checking is the most prudent play here to reach showdown as cheaply as possible, as betting is unlikely to get a better kicker to fold and will only be called or raised by hands that beat or chop with mine.

Board: Ac 6s Ad Ah 5c; my hand 7c6h = Aces full of sixes (AAA66) — extremely strong; only the unseen A♠ (quads) beats me. Flash’s line (check-check on flop/turn, call my small turn bet) is consistent with a wide range: Ax, some 6x, and many weaker hands. Checking river would lock up the current pot (800) but forgo value from A-x and stubborn pairs that will call a value bet. Betting a small-to-medium size maximizes value from Ax while keeping the loss limited if Flash jams (which would usually indicate the quads). I will bet half‑pot (400): large enough to get called by most Ax and worse, but small enough that if Flash raises/shoves I can fold and only lose my bet rather than the whole pot. If Flash calls I collect extra value; if he folds I still take the 800. Therefore: bet 400 on the river and fold to any large shove.

On a board with four Aces, the best five-card hand for both players is Quad Aces plus the highest available kicker. My best hand is A-A-A-A-9.
