Minni, opening from the button, has a wide range that includes many cards lower than a Nine, but also several higher cards (Ten through King). Since I am getting 3:1 odds on a call (400 to win a total pot of 1600), I only need to have the best hand or chop more than 25% of the time. Given the wide range of a button opener and the fact that she checked the flop, a Nine kicker is sufficient to justify a call to see if she is betting with a lower kicker or a split pot.

In heads-up play, any King is a strong starting hand. Being on the button (Small Blind), I want to maintain aggression and take the initiative. A standard 3x raise to 300 puts pressure on Minni in the Big Blind and allows me to play the hand in position if she calls.

AQs (A♣Q♣) is a top heads‑up holding against a button open to 3bb. Flash (button/SB) will have a wide opening range, and with deep stacks (~190bb) I want to 3‑bet for value and fold equity rather than allow a cheap flop. I’ll raise to 900 total (a standard ~3x 300 open) — big enough to get value from worse aces, Kx and medium pairs and to apply pressure, but not so large as to commit the stack to a single street. If Flash 4‑bets very large I’ll re‑evaluate and likely fold; if called I’ll continue postflop with a range advantage and c‑bet selectively on favorable textures.

I opened with a standard raise to 300 with K3o from the button. Minni has 3-bet to 900. While a King is decent heads-up, K3o is fairly weak against a 3-bet range. Minni has shown some aggression and selective pressure. Calling here out of position (post-flop) with a weak King is often a losing proposition, and 4-betting is too risky given the hand strength. The best disciplined move is to fold and wait for a better spot, as I still have a healthy stack lead.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) with 4♣ T♦. Pot is 150 and it costs me 50 more to see the flop, but in heads-up with position I should be opening a wide range. Raising takes the initiative, picks up the blinds sometimes, and lets me play postflop in position rather than giving the aggressive BB free option to dictact play. A standard 3BB open to 300 (total) is the right size here — small enough to keep pots manageable against deep stacks but large enough to generate fold equity. Plan: if Flash folds I win the blinds; if he calls I’ll c-bet favorable flops and play cautiously on heavy high-card/3-card coordinated boards; if he 3-bets to a large sizing I’ll fold this marginal hand and wait for a better spot.

In a heads-up situation, Ace-Four offsuit is a strong hand to defend from the Big Blind against a standard button/small blind open. Minni has been quite active, and while A4o is ahead of a wide range of hands, calling allows me to keep the pot size manageable and see a flop. Since we are deep-stacked, a call is a balanced play that doesn't overcommit pre-flop with a marginal Ace.

On this dry, low-connected board, my Ace-high currently has decent showdown value and some backdoor potential (gutshot to a wheel). Minni opened pre-flop, and as the pre-flop aggressor, she is likely to continuation bet a wide range of hands. By checking, I am giving her the opportunity to bet with her air, allowing me to decide whether to call based on the bet sizing or take the pot down later if she checks behind. There is no need to lead out here as it reveals too much strength or a vulnerable bluff; checking is the standard play for the Big Blind on this texture.

Heads-up I was the preflop raiser and we see a dry, low flop 5♥ J♣ 2♥. I hold T♦4♣ — no pair and no heart draw, so this is essentially a bluff candidate but I have position and the initiative. Flash checked, so I should continuation-bet a high frequency to leverage fold equity and deny equity to broadway and heart draws. Pot is 600; a half-pot c-bet (300) applies pressure while keeping the pot controllable against a caller. Plan: bet 300 to take it down vs many one-pair/air hands and to get called by worse Jx/medium hands; fold to a significant check-raise since I have very little showdown equity; if called I will re-evaluate on the turn and barrel only with a clear improvement or a very favorable card/line.

On a board of 5h-Jc-2h, my Ace-high has reasonable showdown value and a gutshot straight draw to the wheel. Minni is the pre-flop aggressor and is expected to continuation bet a very wide range on this dry texture. I am getting 3:1 pot odds (300 to call a 900 pot) which makes a call mathematically sound with my overcards and backdoor equity. Folding here would be too exploitable given her high frequency of betting.

Minni checked back the turn after I checked, which often indicates she has a marginal hand or is giving up on a bluff. My Ace-high has decent showdown value in a heads-up pot, and I also have a gutshot straight draw to a wheel. By checking, I'm content to take a free river card and see if I can improve or win at showdown if the action remains passive.

Turn has given me top pair (T♦) with a weak 4-kicker on a fairly dry board: 5♥ J♣ 2♥ → T♣. Flash check-called my half-pot c-bet on the flop, so his continuing range is wide — Jx (dominates me), small pairs (5x/2x), one-heart draws (Ah/Kh/x♥), and some floats. I want to charge the heart draws and extract from worse pairs while keeping the pot under control versus Jx. A half-pot value bet (≈600 into 1200) is the right balance: it gets called by draws and weaker made hands, denies free cards, and preserves stack flexibility. Plan: bet 600; if he check-raises big I’ll give credit to a strong made hand and likely fold (or re-evaluate by sizing), if he calls I’ll re-evaluate the river based on the river card and his tendencies.

Minni, the pre-flop aggressor, has continued her aggression with a second barrel on the turn. The board of 5h-Jc-2h-Tc is quite coordinated, and the Ten of Clubs is a card that interacts well with her range, potentially giving her two-pair or improving straight draws. My current hand is only Ace-high with a gutshot straight draw to a wheel. Mathematically, I am being offered 3:1 pot odds (600 to call an 1800 pot), requiring roughly 25% equity. With only four certain outs for the straight and potentially three Aces that may not even be clean outs, my equity is likely around 15-18%. Being out of position makes it even harder to realize my equity on the river. Given the situation, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button with King-high suited (Kh 4h). This is a strong starting hand in heads-up play and certainly warrants a raise to take control of the pot and apply pressure on the big blind. I'll stick to my standard open-raising size of 300.

Facing a 300 open from the button in a heads-up spot, the pot is 400 and I need to add 200 more. 5♥4♠ is a marginal but defensible hand heads-up — it has reasonable equity versus a wide SB opening range and is cheap to see given the pot odds (~3:1). I'm deep-stacked (~20k) so I don't want to inflate the pot with a thin 3-bet with this holding. I'll call the 200 (300 total) to see a flop, then plan to proceed cautiously: continue aggressively on flops that hit me (pairs, two pair, or strong draws) and fold to heavy pressure when completely missed to preserve my stack.

I have 5♥4♠ on a 3♥ Q♣ 9♦ flop — no pair and only weak backdoor hearts/runner-runner straight possibilities. Flash was the preflop raiser and historically c-bets a wide range in heads-up, so he’ll often bet here. Pot is only 600 and both stacks are deep (~19.7k) — I don’t want to inflate the pot with a bluff or chase marginal backdoors. I’ll check to exercise pot control and see what Flash does: if he checks back I get a free turn to reevaluate; if he fires a small probe (very small, ~<=1/3 pot) I may call for the price of a turn, but I will fold to a standard/large c-bet (≈1/2 pot or bigger). For now the correct action is to check.

On a board of 3h-Qc-9d, I have King-high with a backdoor heart draw. As the pre-flop aggressor, I will make a standard continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to take advantage of the initiative and represent a Queen or a pocket pair. This should be enough to force folds from Minni's weaker hands that missed the flop.

I checked the flop planning to fold to a standard/large c-bet. Flash fires 300 into the pot; I must call 300 to win a 900 pot, so I need ~25% equity. 5♥4♠ on 3♥ Q♣ 9♦ has only ~6 clean outs to pair (≈24% to hit by river), no real flush possibility and only tiny runner-runner straight chances. Even when I improve I’m often behind Qx/9x or other pairs. Facing a half-pot c-bet out of position with deep stacks, the price and future streets don’t justify a marginal call. If the bet had been a small probe (≤1/3 pot) I’d consider calling, but at this size I fold to preserve stack and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) with 6♥3♦ — a weak unsuited hand, but heads-up I should be opening extremely wide. The pot is 150 and it costs me 50 to complete. I’ll raise to the minimum allowed (200 total) to take the initiative and apply fold equity against Flash while keeping the pot small and manageable. If he 3-bets big I’ll fold (poor equity), if he calls I plan to continuation-bet small on dry flops and slow down on coordinated or dangerous boards. Calling 50 is cheaper, but raising maximizes EV in heads-up play by forcing decisions and allowing me to control postflop. Raise to 200.

Hand 121: I am in the Big Blind with Queen of Spades and Eight of Diamonds. Minni raises to 200 from the small blind/button. Given the pot odds and the relative strength of Q8o in a heads-up match against a frequent opener, I will call the 100-chip raise to see a flop and defend my big blind.

I have flopped top pair with an 8 kicker on a board of Qd-Td-2c. In heads-up play, top pair is a very strong hand. As I am first to act on the flop, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) to see if she follows up with a continuation bet. This allows me to keep her bluffs in or extract value from weaker hands that might bet but would fold to a lead. I will check and plan to call or raise depending on her bet size.

I opened very wide heads‑up and was called; on Td Qd 2c I have 6♥3♦ — no pair and no real draw. The board is relatively dry aside from diamond draws, and my preflop range contains many Q/T and broadway combos, so I have a range advantage. I'll continuation‑bet small to apply pressure, fold out missed hands, and charge diamond draws while keeping the pot manageable. Bet size ~150 (~35% of the 400 pot) gives good fold equity without overcommitting. If I'm called I'll re‑evaluate on the turn and will generally fold to significant aggression since I have very little showdown equity.

I have top pair with an eight kicker on a board of Qd-Td-2c. Minni's bet of 150 into a 400-chip pot is a standard continuation bet that could come from a wide range of hands, including bluffs and draws. While my kicker is mediocre, top pair is very strong in heads-up play. I will call to see the turn and keep the pot controlled, allowing Minni to continue with her bluffs or weaker holdings while I re-evaluate on the next street.

I still have top pair with an eight kicker on a board of Qd-Td-2c-7h. The Seven of Hearts is essentially a brick, as it doesn't complete any of the primary draws (diamonds or open-ended straight draws like J9 or KJ). Since I checked and called the flop, checking the turn is the most consistent and strategic play. It allows Minni to continue betting with her bluffs or semi-bluffs (like diamond draws or straight draws) while keeping the pot manageable in case she has a stronger Queen or a slow-played monster. I plan to call a standard-sized bet to reach the river.

Heads‑up I opened extremely wide preflop and c‑bet a small 150 on the dry Td Qd 2c flop to pick up the pot and charge diamond draws. On the 7h turn I have 6h3d — no pair and no diamond draw — but I still have a range advantage heads‑up and the board is non‑scary. Double‑barreling a small bet keeps the initiative, applies pressure to missed hands and draws, and still keeps the pot manageable if called. Pot is 700 so I choose a ~1/3–40% pot second barrel to maximize fold equity without overcommitting. Plan: fold to significant raise (I have very little showdown equity), call/continue only vs very small turn raises if appropriate, otherwise re‑evaluate if called on river.

I have top pair with an eight kicker on a board of Qd-Td-2c-7h. Minni has bet 250 into a 700-chip pot on the turn, which is approximately a 1/3 pot-sized bet. This small sizing could indicate a variety of hands: a semi-bluff with a diamond or straight draw, a thin value bet with a weaker Queen or a ten, or an attempt to control the pot with a medium-strength hand. While my kicker is not the strongest, top pair is a very high-ranking hand in heads-up play and certainly warrants a call given the excellent pot odds (4.8:1). I will call to see the river and re-evaluate based on the final card and Minni's action.

On the river, the board has paired the Ten, giving me two pair: Queens and Tens with an eight kicker. Throughout the hand, I have played a check-calling line to control the size of the pot and allow Minni to continue with her range. Post-flop in heads-up play, the Big Blind (me) acts first. Leading out now might fold out her bluffs and only get called by hands that have me beat, such as a Ten for trips or a Queen with a superior kicker. By checking, I induce bluffs from missed draws (like the diamond flush or straight draws) and can potentially reach a cheap showdown. I will check and evaluate Minni's response.

Heads‑up I opened extremely wide and barreled flop and turn; the river paired the board (Td Qd 2c 7h Tc) and I hold 6h3d — no pair and essentially zero showdown equity vs a calling range that includes Qx and Tx. Pot is 1,200. Flash called both streets, so his range is weighted toward queens, tens, medium pairs and missed diamond draws. A small river bluff targets the missed draws and weaker holdings while keeping risk small. I size to ~1/3 pot (400). Break‑even fold frequency for a 400 bluff is 400/(1200+400)=25%, which is realistic against missed draws/weak hands in his calling range. If he folds I win the 1,200 pot; if he calls I lose a small amount relative to stacks. I will fold to significant raise. Bet 400 to apply final pressure and attempt to take down the pot.

On the river, the board pairs the Ten, giving me Two Pair, Queens and Tens, with an Eight kicker. Minni bets 400 into a 1200 pot, offering me pot odds of 4:1. While the Ten is a potential card for her to have trips, her sizing is small enough that she could be value betting a weaker Queen or bluffing with missed draws like diamonds or a straight draw (KJ, J9). Given the strength of top pair in heads-up play and the favorable price, I will call to see the showdown.

I am on the button with King-Six offsuit, which is a strong enough hand to open for a raise in a heads-up situation. My goal is to apply pressure to the big blind and potentially win the pot pre-flop or take control of the betting lead going into the flop. I will follow my standard opening size of 300.

Heads-up vs Flash (button/SB) who opened to 300. I hold A♥9♠ in the big blind — a strong heads-up hand but out of position postflop and we’re very deep (~187bb). A 3-bet is a playable option, but I prefer pot control and to keep weaker aces and broadways in his range by just calling. Calling 200 more (300 total committed) keeps the pot manageable (≈600) and lets me realize equity and leverage position dynamics postflop: if I hit an Ace or a strong draw I can extract value; on dry boards I can float or fold to heavy aggression. I’ll call here and evaluate the flop before committing more chips.

Heads-up on a dry, low board (4c 2d 3d) I have A♥9♠ — a decent Ace-high but no pair and no diamond draw. Flash opened wide from the button preflop so his range contains lots of Ax, small pairs and diamond draws; many of those will continuation-bet. I'm out of position and stacks are very deep, so I don't want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand that is vulnerable to draws and sets. Checking controls the pot size, keeps his weaker Aces and bluffs in his range, and lets me call a small-to-medium c-bet to realize equity. Plan: check; if he bets smallish (≈≤1/2 pot, ~ ≤300) I will call and reevaluate on the turn; if he bets big (>~2/3 pot, >400) I will give him credit and fold. If he checks back I can take a free turn card and potentially barreI or take another line depending on the card.

On a board of 4c-2d-3d, I have overcards (King and Six) and a backdoor diamond flush draw with the Six of Diamonds. Minni has checked to me. This board is very low and connected, which could hit some of Minni's defending range, but I still have a significant range advantage as the pre-flop raiser. A continuation bet here is standard to maintain aggression and potentially take down the pot immediately, or to build the pot if I improve on later streets. I will bet 300, which is 50% of the pot.

Flash c-bets a small 300 on a very dry 4c-2d-3d flop. I hold A♥9♠ — Ace-high with an Ace blocker but no diamond draw. Heads-up the button opened wide preflop so his range contains lots of weaker Aces, small pairs and bluffs. Given the small bet size and deep stacks, calling is the best line: it keeps his weaker hands and bluffs in, gives me good pot odds to see a turn, and avoids bloating the pot out of position with a marginal hand. I will call 300 and reevaluate on the turn — if I pair my Ace I can extract value; if a diamond or another scary card comes and he barrels large I’ll fold; if he checks back I can consider taking the initiative next street.

I will check. I’m out of position with A♥9♠ on 4♣ 2♦ 3♦ J♦ and we’re very deep (≈180bb). I have only Ace‑high and no diamond, while the turn brings a third diamond and also helps many hands in Flash’s wide opening range (Jx, two‑card diamond combos, small pairs). Betting OOP with a marginal hand would bloat the pot and get me committed vs many better hands or two‑card diamond flushes; checking controls the pot and lets me realize showdown value. Plan: check and call a small/medium turn bet to see the river (I still have some showdown value vs bluffs and single‑pair hands), but fold to a large bet/raise that represents a made hand or nut diamond. If Flash checks back I’ll re-evaluate the river — value bet if I improve to a pair, otherwise often check to showdown.

On the turn, the board now shows 4c-2d-3d-Jd. I hold the Ks and the 6d, giving me a flush draw (with the 6d) and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 5). While the Jack of Diamonds is an overcard, it also completes a potential flush if Minni was holding two diamonds. Minni has checked to me again. Given the texture of the board and the fact that I have significant equity with both the flush and straight draws, checking back is a solid play. It allows me to realize my equity for free and potentially hit one of my draws on the river without risking a check-raise on the turn. If I improve on the river, I can then value bet; if I miss, I can decide whether to bluff or fold based on the final board and Minni's action.

Pot is 1,200 and both of us checked the turn; I hold A♥9♠ — a weak showdown hand with some blocker value to Ax/A5 straights but no diamond. Flash opened wide preflop, c-bet the flop small and then checked turn, so his range contains a lot of missed draws and weak pairs that will often fold a river bluff. Betting gives me fold equity and is preferable to simply checking and conceding the pot. I’ll make a half‑pot bluff (600): that size requires Flash to fold only ~33% of the time to make the bluff profitable even with no showdown equity, and it credibly represents a K/J or a diamond. If he calls I’m prepared to show or release to further aggression; if he raises I’ll fold — I’m not committing with just Ace‑high.

On the river King of Clubs, I have top pair (Kings) with a 6 kicker. The board is quite dangerous with several possible straights (any 5 makes a straight) and a completed diamond flush (three diamonds on board). Minni has led out for 600 into a 1200 pot after checking the turn. While my King is strong, the 6d in my hand is significant because it was a blocker to the flush on the turn, but now that there are four diamonds, any single diamond in Minni's hand beats me. However, her line (check-call flop, check turn, lead river) could also represent a missed straight draw or a weaker King that she's trying to value bet now that she hit. Given the odds (calling 600 to win 2400), I only need to be right 25% of the time. I will call to see the showdown.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with KcTd — a strong, playable hand versus a very wide BB range. I want initiative and fold equity with position, so I’ll open-raise rather than just complete. I raise to 300 (≈3x the BB): a standard heads-up sizing that pressures the BB, builds the pot with a hand that plays well postflop, and prevents giving too many free flops. Postflop plan: continuation-bet frequently on dry boards, extract value when I hit top pair or good draws, and exercise pot control or fold versus large 3-bets depending on sizing and texture.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Ten offsuit (Kd Th). My opponent, Minni, has raised to 300 from the Button/Small Blind. KTo is a very strong hand in a heads-up situation and is well ahead of a typical button opening range. While I could 3-bet to exert pressure, calling allows me to keep the pot manageable and keep my opponent's weaker hands in the range. I have a significant chip lead and will look to outplay Minni post-flop.

On a flop of Jd 9s 6d, I have King-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a Queen to complete a 9-10-J-Q-K straight). As the Big Blind against the pre-flop raiser, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. My hand has enough potential to see a turn card, especially since any Queen, King, or Ten could improve my hand significantly. Checking allows me to see how Minni values this board and preserves my options to either call a standard continuation bet or fold if faced with extreme aggression.

Heads-up, I opened preflop to 300 and Flash just checked the flop (9♠ 6♦ J♦). This board is only semi-wet (two diamonds) but is otherwise fairly dry, and Flash’s BB calling range is wide—many unpaired hands and single overcards will fold to pressure. I hold K♣T♦: a useful hand here with a Q-gutshot (4 outs), a backdoor diamond possibility, and a live overcard. I’ll make a standard continuation bet ~2/3 pot (400) to fold out weak holdings, deny equity to diamond/straight draws, and extract value from worse hands that will still call. Plan: if he folds I take the pot; if called I re-evaluate on the turn and only continue aggressively on a Q/K/T/diamond or to bluff when odds and blockers are favorable; fold or pot-control if he check-raises large.

On a flop of Jd 9s 6d, I have a gutshot straight draw with my King and Ten, plus two overcards that could potentially be the best hand if they hit. Minni's bet of 400 into a 600 pot gives me roughly 2.5:1 on a call. Given my chip lead and the fact that my King of diamonds provides a back-door flush blocker and some playability on diamond turns, I have sufficient equity and implied odds to see a turn card. Folding would be too tight in a heads-up match, and raising would likely only get called by better hands while forcing out bluffs I can currently beat or outdraw.

On the turn, the Ten of Spades gives me second pair (Tens) with a King kicker and maintains my gutshot straight draw (Queen). While my hand has improved significantly, it is still second-best to any Jack or better. In heads-up play, checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard move to control the pot and evaluate their reaction to the turn card. My hand now has enough showdown value to call a reasonable bet, and checking allows Minni to continue bluffing with missed draws or weaker hands while keeping the pot manageable.

I opened preflop and c‑bet the flop (9♠ 6♦ J♦); Flash called. The turn T♠ pairs my T♦, so I now have a solid second pair (tens) with a King kicker. Flash’s flop‑call range is wide (Jx, diamond draws, 9x/6x, some overcards), so betting here both extracts value from worse pairs/queen/king highs and charges diamond/straight draws that otherwise get a free river. I don’t want to build an overly large pot when Jx or sets are in his range, so I choose a constructive ~60–70% pot bet to get value and fold equity while keeping the pot manageable. Bet 900 and re‑evaluate on the river; fold to a large check‑raise, call smaller aggression and proceed cautiously if called.

On the turn, the Ten of Spades improves my hand to second pair with a King kicker, and I retain a gutshot straight draw (needing a Queen). Minni's bet of 900 into a 1400 pot gives me approximately 2.5:1 odds on a call. Given my improved showdown value and the additional equity from the straight draw and overcard, I have a clear call. Raising would likely isolate me against stronger hands like a Jack or two-pair, whereas calling allows me to realize my equity and potentially catch a bluff or hit a winning hand on the river.

I have second pair (Tens) with a King kicker on a board of J-9-6-T-2. While my hand improved on the turn, it remains a medium-strength showdown hand. Betting here likely won't get called by many worse hands, but checking allows Minni to potentially bluff with missed draws (like diamonds) or check back weaker pairs. Since I am first to act on the river after calling bets on the flop and turn, checking is the most prudent play to reach showdown or evaluate a final bet.

I have Kc Td — second pair (pair of tens) with a King kicker on 9♠ 6♦ J♦ T♠ 2♠. Flash called my flop and turn bets and then checked the river, which looks like a fairly wide check‑back range: Jx (which beats me), 9x/6x and some weaker Tx/kicker combos (which I beat), plus missed draws. There is a small chance of a spade flush (board has three spades) but those require two spades in his hand and are relatively infrequent. Checking gives up value to worse pairs and weak Tens; a value bet will charge draws, fold pure air, and get called by the worse hands I beat. I'll make a constructive half‑pot value bet (≈1,600) — big enough to get calls from 9x/6x and weaker Tx, but not so large as to fold out most thin calls. If he check‑raises large I’ll assume he has Jx/two‑pair/flush and fold; if he calls I’m comfortable going to showdown.

On a river board of 9s-6d-Jd-Ts-2s, I hold second pair (Tens) with a King kicker. Minni has bet three streets: 400 on the flop, 900 on the turn, and 1600 on the river. In a heads-up match, this line can represent a strong value hand like a Jack or two-pair, but it is also the standard line for a triple-barrel bluff with missed draws. Many draws have missed, specifically the diamond flush draw and various straight draws (like 7-8 or Q-x). I am getting 3:1 pot odds (1600 to win a total pot of 4800), which means I only need to be right about 25% of the time. Given the high-stakes tournament context and the frequency of bluffs in heads-up play, my hand is a viable bluff-catcher. Folding here would be overly exploitable, so I will call to see the showdown.

I have Jack-Ten of clubs on the button in a heads-up situation. This is a very strong starting hand with excellent playability and post-flop potential. My strategy throughout the tournament has been to open for 3x the big blind (300) from the button to exert pressure and build pots with strong holdings. Given the current stack depths and the strength of my suited connector, a standard raise to 300 is the most effective play to either win the blinds immediately or play a pot in position with a premium range.

Flash opened to 300 from the button — a standard, often-wide heads-up raise. I have K♥J♠, a strong heads-up hand that fares well vs a wide opening range. I want to apply pressure and get value/fold equity rather than passively playing OOP, so I’ll 3-bet to isolate and seize initiative preflop. I choose a 1200 total raise (clear, credible size that folds out weaker Ax/Tx and small pairs often, but still allows me to fold to a large 4-bet). If Flash 4-bets big (e.g. > ~4000) I’ll fold; if he calls I’ll c-bet on favorable flops and control the pot on dry boards, value-betting when I hit K or J.

I hold JcTc on the button, which is a premium suited hand in heads-up play. I opened for 300, and Minni 3-bet to 1200. With 164 big blinds effective, JcTc is a very strong hand to call a 3-bet with, especially while in position. Calling allows me to use my positional advantage post-flop and keeps Minni's entire range, including potential bluffs or dominated hands, in the pot. The pot odds (900 to call into a total pot that will be 2400) are excellent for a hand with this much playability.

I 3-bet preflop to 1200 and Flash called, so his continuing range is wide (broadways, suited connectors, some Kx and medium pairs). The flop K♠ 7♦ 2♠ gives me top pair with a good kicker (K♥ J♠). I'm out of position, so I want to seize initiative, charge draws and extract value from worse Kx, Jx and many of his calling hands. There are two spades on board so sizing needs to charge flush draws while still getting called by worse; betting ~2/3 pot is appropriate. I'll bet 1600 to deny equity to spade draws, get value from weaker kings/second pairs and avoid letting him see a free turn. If he raises big I will re-evaluate (he's representing sets or strong spade combos), but on this flop a value-forward c-bet is the best line.

On this flop of K-7-2, I have completely missed with my Jack-Ten of clubs. Pre-flop, Minni showed strength with a 3-bet to 1200, and she has followed through with a significant bet of 1600 on a board that favors a 3-betting range (containing hands like AK, KQ, and AA/KK). I have no direct draws and very little backdoor equity. Calling would be a pure float with almost no equity, and raising into a range that likely contains top pair or better is too risky given the stack sizes and tournament stage. The most disciplined play is to fold and preserve my chip lead.

Heads-up on the button with KcTc suited — a strong, positionally advantaged hand. Pot is 150 and it costs 50 to call; min-raise is 200 but I'll open to 3x the BB to take the initiative and get more fold equity. Raising to 300 (3BB) builds the pot without committing too many chips on these deep stacks (~187 BB), isolates the hand vs a wide BB defending range, and allows me to use position to c-bet and extract value or fold to heavy aggression. I avoid limping (which gives cheap flops and cedes initiative). If Flash 3-bets big I’ll re-evaluate (likely tighten / fold depending on sizing); if he flats I’ll play for position and look to barreI on favorable runouts.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Jack offsuit, which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. Minni has raised from the button to 300. Given our deep stacks and my position, calling is the most standard play. It allows me to see a flop with a hand that performs well against a wide opening range without inflating the pot while out of position.

I am out of position on a paired board (6s 7d 6d). While Queen-Jack offsuit has some potential with two overcards, it hasn't connected with the board. Checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard and most prudent play here to see how Minni proceeds and to keep the pot manageable while I have only high-card value.

Flop is 6s 7d 6d — a paired, dangerous texture with immediate full-house/quads possibilities (66, 77, 67 combos). I raised pre and Flash just called, then checked to me. I hold KcTc: no pair, no club draw, very little equity vs a calling range that can contain pocket pairs, 7x, 6x, or strong broadway hands. Betting here turns a marginal hand into a bloated pot and invites check-raises or big calls from hands that dominate me or already have the nuts. With deep stacks and only K-high I prefer pot control and to use position to see another card for free. Plan: check back the flop, evaluate the turn — if I pick up a king/ten or a harmless rag that thins his range I can apply pressure; if Flash leads into me I’ll fold most turns unless I improve. Checking preserves chips and keeps weaker parts of his range in so I can bluff later or extract value when I actually pair up.

After both players checked the flop, the turn 10 of diamonds is an interesting card that doesn't significantly help my Q-high hand but provides a flush draw. I will check again to see if Minni continues her passivity. My goal is to either reach a cheap showdown or see if I can improve on the river without risking more chips out of position against a range that still contains many A-high and K-high hands.

I just paired my Ten on the turn (KcTc) — turn is Td, board 6s 7d 6d Td. Villain checked flop and checked the turn, so his line contains both weak hands (missed broadways, small pairs) and slow-played monsters (sets/full houses) or rare two-card diamond flushes now that there are three diamonds on board. With top pair + good kicker I have value vs lots of his checking range (88/99/Kx/Ax/Tx) but I'm vulnerable to sets, full houses, quads and two-card diamond combos. I want to extract value and charge worse hands while keeping the pot manageable if I face aggression, so I bet a half-pot (300). This size is big enough to get called by worse one-pair hands and to charge/price out some equity, but small enough that I can fold to a strong check-raise (which would usually represent a boat/quads/flush). If called I’ll re-evaluate on the river: continue for thin value on safe rivers, check/protect or fold to heavy action on rivers that pair the board or complete obvious two-card diamond holdings.

Minni's decision to check the flop suggests she likely doesn't have a 6 or a strong 7. Her turn bet of 300 into a 600 pot appears to be a standard stab at the pot after the flop was checked through. I am holding Queen-Jack with the Jack of Diamonds, which gives me four-to-a-flush on this board, along with two overcards that could be good if they hit. Given that I'm getting 3-to-1 pot odds (calling 300 to win a total pot that will be 1200), I have more than enough equity and potential to justify a call here.

On the river, the Ace of Clubs completes the board. I have missed my flush draw and currently hold only Queen-high. Having checked the flop and called a bet on the turn, my range includes some hands that hit this Ace, but since I am out of position against Minni, checking is the most prudent play. I have no showdown value with Queen-high, but betting as a bluff into a board that likely connects with an opponent's turn-betting range is risky. I will check and likely fold to any significant bet.

River is A♣ on a very wet/paired board (6s 7d 6d Td Ac). I hold Kc Tc — I have top pair with a good kicker, but the Ace on the river now beats any A‑x and the flop texture allows many full‑house/quads combos (66, 77, 6+7, TT, etc.). Flash’s line (call pre, check flop, call my small half‑pot turn bet, check river) is ambiguous: it includes weak/medium hands I beat (88/99/Kx/etc.) but also can include slow‑played monsters. Betting here would extract thin value from some worse hands but also opens me up to a costly check‑raise from a boat/quads or induce calls from Ax that now beat me. With deep stacks and a large portion of his range that currently beats me, pot control is the prudent play. I’ll check to the showdown (preserve stack, avoid committing to a bluff/half‑pot that turns into a big value loss against monsters). If he bets I’ll re‑evaluate based on sizing (call small thin value bets, fold to large polarizing raises).

In a heads-up situation from the button, holding a suited Queen (Qd 2d) is a strong enough hand to open for a raise. I have a slightly larger stack than Minni and have been consistently raising to 300 from this position to apply pressure and build the pot with hands that have post-flop potential.

Heads-up as the big blind with 8♠6♠ versus a button open to 300. Pot is 400 and I must add 200 to call (I've already posted 100). Stacks are deep (~192 BB), so postflop playability and implied odds matter. Versus a button opening range this wide, 8s6s has respectable equity and good multi-street potential. A min-raise to 500 gains little fold equity and only bloats the pot out of position with a marginal hand; folding is too tight heads-up. I’ll call the 200 (total committed = 300) to see a flop and proceed cautiously OOP: continue aggressively if I hit or pick up strong draws, otherwise concede to heavy pressure.

I have a very strong hand: trips (6♠) on 6♦-4♦-6♣. Villain opened wide from the button, so their range contains many worse hands (Ax, Kx, medium pairs), single-6 combos (which can beat me only with a better kicker) and a few combos that already beat me (pocket 44 full houses). There are two diamonds on board, so there are also 4-card diamond flush draws to charge. Out of position with deep stacks, I should both extract value and deny equity to draws. A ~2/3 pot value bet (400 into 600) hits that balance: it gets called by worse pairs, worse 6x kickers and draws, charges diamond draws, and keeps turn play manageable. If Flash check-raises large I’ll narrow their range to strong full houses/6x with better kickers and re-evaluate; vs a call I’ll continue extracting on blank turns. Betting 400 now maximizes value while protecting my hand.

On a flop of 6d-4d-6c, I have a Queen-high flush draw. Minni has led into me for 400 chips into a 600-chip pot. My pot odds for calling are approximately 28.5%, while the probability of hitting a flush by the river is about 35%. Although the board is paired, which increases the risk of a full house if I hit the flush, the Queen-high draw is strong enough to continue. Calling allows me to realize my equity and see the turn without inflating the pot too much on a board that might have hit Minni's range (like a 6 or a small pocket pair).

I have trips (6♠ with 6♦-4♦-6♣ on board). Pot is 1,400 after my 400 flop bet was called. The turn K♦ is a somewhat blank card for many of Flash’s calling hands (Kx, medium pairs, worse 6x) but it does bring a 3-diamond board — so the main hands that now beat me are two-diamond combos that just made a flush and the small number of full-house combos (pocket 44 or a 6+4). Those premium combos exist but are relatively few compared to Flash’s wide calling range. I want to keep extracting value from Kx, one-pair hands and weaker 6x while keeping the pot manageable against the hands that crush me. A value-oriented turn bet of about 2/3 pot (~900) achieves that: it charges worse hands and the preflop diamond draws that called the flop, while leaving room to fold/re-evaluate if Flash check-raises big (which will narrow him to flush/full-house). If Flash just calls I’ll plan to continue extracting on a blank (non-diamond) river and pot-control or fold to heavy action if the river completes obvious beating combinations.

I have completed a Queen-high flush on the turn with the King of diamonds. Although the board is paired (6-6), which makes a full house possible, and the Ace of diamonds remains a threat, a flush is a very strong hand in heads-up play. Minni has led out on both the flop and the turn, which likely indicates she has trips (a six) or a King. I will raise to 2200 to extract value from those hands while the board is still relatively clean, as a check-call would miss out on potential value if the river brings a fourth diamond or another board pair.

I have trips (6♠) on 6♦-4♦-6♣-K♦. I bet the turn 900 and Flash check‑raised to 2200; the pot is 4500 and I must call 1300. The raise is polarizing — it often represents a diamond flush (many combo candidates given his wide button raising range) or the rarer full houses (KK or 44), but his range still contains Kx/medium pairs and some bluffs. Calling 1300 into a 4500 pot gives me ~22% pot odds (1300 into a 5800 final pot), so I only need to be ahead ~22% of the time to justify a call. Raising would bloat the pot and is disastrous if he has a flush/full house (he will call or shove), while folding trips here would be too nitty. I’ll call to keep worse hands and bluffs in and control risk; then re-evaluate on the river — fold to a shove that polarizes to flush/full house, value-bet a blank river if he checks, and call small river bets selectively.

I hold trips (6♠) on 6♦-4♦-6♣-K♦-9♠. Flash’s large check‑raise on the turn was polarizing — mostly two‑diamond flushes or the rarer full houses (KK/44) — but he checked the river, which gives me a chance to extract from weaker Kx, one‑pair hands and worse 6x that will call. A modest value bet keeps the pot controlled against flushes/full houses while getting called by the hands I beat. I’ll bet 2,000 into the 5,800 pot (~34%) to take value and allow an easy fold if he raises big (I’ll respect a shove as heavily polarized). If he simply calls I’m collecting value from worse hands; if he folds I win the pot without bloating the stacks against probable flush/full‑house hands.

I have a Queen-high flush on a paired board (6-4-6-K-9). After I raised the turn, Minni called and has now led the river for 2000 into a 5800 pot. While a Queen-high flush is a very strong hand in heads-up play, the paired board makes a full house (44, 64, 69, 6K) possible, and the Ace of diamonds would give her the nut flush. Her decision to lead into the turn-raiser often signals significant strength or an attempt to set the price with a hand like trips. Calling is the most disciplined play here; raising would likely only get called by hands that beat me (boats or the nut flush) and would risk a large portion of my stack if she shoves, while folding a flush in this spot would be too exploitable.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with 8♣7♣ — a strong, connected suited hand that plays very well in position postflop. With deep stacks (~144bb) and only one opponent, I want to be aggressive and take the initiative: a standard 3x BB open (300 total) denies the big blind good pot odds to float wide, builds the pot when I have good implied odds, and preserves fold equity. If Flash 3-bets big I can fold or proceed cautiously; if he calls I’ll use position to continuation-bet favorable, dry boards and to check/float and realize equity on coordinated textures. Raise to 300 to maximize my heads-up edge while keeping pots manageable with a speculative but playable hand.

I am currently in the Big Blind with 7d 4h, which is a very weak hand. Minni has raised to 3x the blind from the Button/Small Blind. While I have favorable pot odds to call (200 to win a total pot of 600), 74o performs poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Given my significant chip lead (approximately 25,425 to 14,175) and the fact that we are approaching the final hands of the tournament (Hand 127 of 150), there is no reason to defend with such a marginal hand and risk being outplayed on later streets. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack advantage.

I am on the button with T3o, which is a very weak hand even in heads-up play. Given my significant chip lead and Minni's recent active play, there is no need to play marginal or poor hands like this. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to utilize my stack advantage.

Heads-up on the button with A♠Q♥ — a very strong hand. With deep stacks (≈146bb) I want the initiative and to avoid giving the BB a cheap flop. A standard 3x BB open to 300 (total) builds value against worse aces and broadways, isolates the BB, and keeps postflop decisions in position. Plan: continuation bet frequently on favorable flops; if Flash 3-bets large I will re-evaluate (call or 4-bet depending on sizing and read). This size balances value and fold equity while keeping maneuverability with deep stacks.

In this heads-up situation, I am the chip leader by a significant margin. Minni has raised to 3x the big blind. While defending the big blind is common in heads-up play, Queen-Trey offsuit is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Given my goal is to maintain my stack lead as we approach the end of the tournament (hand 129 of 150), I will choose the conservative path and fold this marginal hand rather than risking chips in a potentially difficult spot.

I am currently holding 7-2 offsuit, which is statistically the weakest hand in Texas Hold'em. While I am the chip leader and playing heads-up, there is little incentive to play such a poor hand out of position post-flop, especially since Minni has been active with pre-flop raises. Folding here preserves my significant stack lead as we approach the end of the tournament (hand 130 of 150).

Heads-up on the button with Q♠9♠ (Qh9h) — a strong, suited hand in position. I want to take the initiative, apply pressure to the big blind, and avoid giving a free flop. With deep stacks a standard 3x BB open is appropriate: raise to 300 chips to build the pot and get fold equity. If he calls I'll C-bet favorable flops and use position; if he 3-bets I'll re-evaluate based on sizing and his tendencies.

I am in the Big Blind holding 8s 2c, which is a very weak hand. Minni has raised to 300 from the Small Blind. Although I am getting 2:1 odds to call, this hand has very poor playability and I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand. Given my current chip lead and the proximity to the end of the tournament, there is no need to defend with such a weak holding. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

In heads-up play, QJ offsuit is a premium starting hand on the button. I have a significant chip lead over Minni and positional advantage for the rest of the hand. I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300 to exert pressure and build the pot with a strong range.

Flash opened to 300 from the button. I have 3♦9♠ in the big blind; facing 200 to call into a 400 pot (≈33% break-even equity) and heads-up the opener's range is wide, so this hand has enough equity and showdown value to defend. I won't 3-bet out of position with such a weak, non-blocker hand — calling keeps the pot manageable and lets me see a flop where I can continue with a pair or a strong draw and fold to heavy aggression when missed. Amount shown is the total I'll commit (300 chips including my posted BB).

I defended preflop with 3♦9♠ to see a flop; the board is A♦ 4♣ 2♥ and I have no pair. I only have a gutshot to the wheel (the 5s) — roughly ~16% to make by the river — and very little showdown value versus a preflop raiser who often contains many A‑x and connected hands. Out of position, I want to keep the pot small and avoid bluffing without blockers, so I will check and give Flash the opportunity to c‑bet. If Flash fires a meaningful continuation bet I’ll fold (only consider calling a very tiny probe to see a cheap turn), but I won’t barreling or raising OOP with this weak holding.

I was the pre-flop aggressor and have positional advantage on a board that is very favorable for the raiser's range. The presence of the Ace makes this an ideal spot for a continuation bet to take down the pot immediately, as it is difficult for Minni to continue without an Ace or a strong draw. A half-pot bet is sufficient to achieve this goal while minimizing risk.

I defended preflop with 3♦9♠ to see a flop against a wide button opener, but I completely missed the A♦ 4♣ 2♥ board. I only have a 4‑out gutshot to a 5 for the wheel (≈16–17% to hit by the river) and essentially no showdown value or meaningful blockers to Flash’s Ace‑heavy c‑betting range. The pot is 900 and Flash’s bet is 300; calling 300 to win 1200 requires ~25% equity, which I don’t have. Raising as a bluff OOP with this hand is a losing line. With insufficient equity and out of position against a likely Ace‑containing range, the correct play is to fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with K♠J♥ — a strong, playable hand and I have positional advantage postflop. The pot is 150 and I only need to call 50 to see a flop (total 100), but I want to seize initiative, deny a cheap flop, and build the pot with a value hand. Minimum raise is 200 but that's small; sizing to 3x the BB (300 total) is standard here — large enough to apply pressure and get folds from weaker holdings, but not so large that I commit an excessive portion of my 14.5k stack if Flash 3-bets. If he calls I’ll play postflop in position and c-bet appropriate flops; if he 3-bets I’ll re-evaluate based on size and his tendencies. Raise to 300 to maximize EV in this heads-up spot.

I have A3o in the Big Blind. Minni (the small blind) has raised to 300. This is a standard heads-up spot. A3o is ahead of a wide button-opening range and is more than strong enough to continue. However, the stack sizes (I have about 250bb, she has 142bb) and our recent history suggests she can be aggressive. While I could 3-bet here for value and to take control, calling is a solid option to keep her wide range in and evaluate the flop. A3o doesn't play particularly well in 3-bet pots compared to A8s+, but it's a clear defend. I will call the 200.

On this 4-7-5 rainbow board, I have Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 2). As the Big Blind in a heads-up pot, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. Leading into Minni on this board doesn't accomplish much as it hits many of the small connectors and pairs she would raise from the button. I will check and evaluate her action.

Heads-up I was the preflop aggressor with K♠J♥ and Flash just checked the flop (4♥ 7♣ 5♣). This board is relatively dry but has two clubs and lots of unpaired hands in his calling range. KJ has two overcards and ~6 clean outs to pair (≈24% by river), so I prefer to continue and take the initiative rather than allow a free card. A small c-bet of ~1/3 pot (200 into 600) will fold many weaker hands and charge club draws while keeping the pot manageable with a marginal holding. If I get a raise I’ll strongly consider folding to a large check-raise; if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and proceed (or check back) depending on the card and villain sizing.

I have Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a six for a 3-4-5-6-7 straight) on a low, coordinated board of 4-7-5. Minni has bet 200 into a 600-chip pot, offering me 4-to-1 pot odds. Given the small sizing of her continuation bet and the fact that I have some backdoor equity and an overcard, calling is the most efficient play. I am not yet ready to raise as this board texture hits a lot of her button opening range, but I'm certainly not folding for this price.

On the Turn, the board is 4h-7c-5c-Kh. I am out of position as the Big Blind. The King is a card that typically favors the pre-flop aggressor's (Minni's) range. I currently hold Ace-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a six). Leading into the aggressor here doesn't make much sense as I don't have a made hand, and I want to keep the pot manageable while I try to realize my equity or see if I can get to a cheap showdown. I will check and see how Minni reacts to the King.

I turned top pair with K♠J♥ on K♥ — a clear improvement and a ‘range’ card for my preflop raising range. Flash checked the turn after calling a small flop c‑bet, so his continuing range is heavy with club draws, weak pairs (7x/5x/4x), floats and some overcards. I want to charge those draws and extract value from worse pairs and Kx while keeping the pot controllable. A ~1/2‑pot bet denies free river cards to clubs, gets value from calls, and leaves room for river play given my 14k stack. If he folds I take it down; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on river (and fold to a large, polarized check‑raise on the turn which usually represents two‑pair/set). Bet half the pot (500).

On the turn, the board is 4h-7c-5c-Kh. I am holding Ace-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a 6). Minni has fired a second barrel for 500 into a pot of 1000. While I have 3:1 pot odds, the King is a card that significantly favors her range as the pre-flop aggressor. My equity with a gutshot (approx. 9%) and potentially three live Aces (if she doesn't have a King or better) is not sufficient to justify a call against a 50% pot-sized bet, especially since I'm out of position and have no guaranteed showdown value. It's best to fold here and look for a better spot.

I am the chip leader with approximately 24,875 chips against Minni's 14,975. In a heads-up situation with 17 hands remaining, maintaining pressure from the button is important. Queen-seven offsuit is a playable hand in this position. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds immediately, or play the hand in position if called.

Holding 7♣2♠ — essentially the worst hand. Flash opened to 300 from the button; I’m in the big blind and must call 200 into a 400 pot. That call requires >33% equity to be +EV (200/(400+200)=33.3%). 7-2o has very poor equity and terrible post‑flop playability out of position, and it’s frequently dominated by the opener’s range. 3-betting as a bluff is also a poor use of chips with this holding. With deep stacks and many better spots ahead, preserve my stack and fold this clearly losing hand.

Heads-up on the button with K♣T♣ — a very playable, suited Broadway hand. Pot is 150 and I only need 50 to call, but I want to take the initiative, build the pot with a hand that plays well postflop and apply pressure to the BB's wide defending range. A standard 3x BB open (raise to 300 total) gives good fold equity, isolates the opponent, and keeps the pot manageable if called. If Flash 3-bets large I'll often fold KTs; against a small 3-bet I may call and play postflop in position, c-betting favorable flops and checking/controlling size on heavy Ace or connected boards.raise to 300 to take the lead preflop.

I have been dealt a pocket pair of Sixes in the Big Blind. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. With 66, I have a strong enough hand to either call or 3-bet. Looking at our stack depths, I have about 250bb and Minni has about 147bb. This is a classic set-mining or value situation. Given that Minni has been active and we are heads-up, I want to build a pot but also maintain some control. Raising to 1000 (a 3.3x 3-bet) is a standard play here to take the initiative and extract value from weaker hands in Minni's opening range. If they call, I can evaluate on most flops.

Heads-up on the button with K♣T♣ facing a 3-bet to 1000 after I opened to 300. KTs has strong postflop playability and decent equity versus a BB’s wide 3-bet range; we also have positional advantage and deep stacks (~147 BB effective). Calling keeps the pot and SPR manageable, lets me realize equity and apply pressure on favorable flops. A 4-bet would be too polarizing/committal with this holding and folding is too passive. I will call the 3-bet and play the flop in position (commit 700 more for 1000 total).

On a board of 2c-Jh-Qd, there are two overcards to my pocket sixes. Since Minni called a pre-flop 3-bet, her range is dense with hands like AQ, KQ, QJ, and JJ-TT which either beat me or have significant equity. Betting into this board as the pre-flop aggressor is risky because it's hard to get better hands to fold or worse hands to call. Checking allows me to control the pot size and see how Minni reacts.

Flop: Q♦ J♥ 2♣, pot 2000, villain (Flash) checked after 3-betting pre. I have K♣T♣ — no pair but a very playable heads‑up hand: a broadway open‑ended straight draw (A or 9 completes; 8 outs — ~17% to hit the turn, ~31.5% by the river) plus backdoor club potential and I block some Kx combos. Flash’s 3‑bet range heads‑up is relatively wide, and his check gives me an opportunity to take the initiative and fold out many missed hands (A‑highs, weaker Kx, some bluffs). I’ll make a standard continuation bet of ~½ pot (1000) — big enough to generate fold equity and charge drawing hands, but small enough to keep the pot manageable if called given deep stacks. Plan: if he folds I win the pot; if he calls I re‑evaluate on the turn (raise/commit only if I improve or get a very favorable card; check/control on blanks); if he check‑raises the flop large I’ll concede most of the time since I currently have just a draw/no pair.

I am currently the chip leader with a significant advantage. In this hand, I 3-bet pre-flop with pocket sixes and was called by Minni. The flop (Q-J-2) is quite unfavorable as it contains two overcards that hit a typical 3-bet calling range (hands like KQs, QJs, AJs, or JTs) very well. After I checked to the aggressor, Minni bet half the pot. Given the board texture and my position, 66 is little more than a bluff catcher here. Calling would likely force me to face another difficult decision on the turn without much potential to improve. To protect my stack and avoid high-variance situations with a marginal hand late in the tournament, I will fold.

I am currently holding 9c 3h, which is a very weak starting hand. With a significant chip lead of approximately 24,000 to 16,000 and only 15 hands remaining in the tournament, there is little incentive to play marginal or poor hands out of position or in situations where I lack initiative. Minni has shown aggression in recent hands, so I will fold this trash hand to wait for a better spot to utilize my stack advantage.

I’m on the button (posted the SB) with Qs7s — a playable, suited hand with good post‑flop playability in position. With blinds 50/100 the pot is 150 and I only need to call 50, but I prefer to seize the initiative heads‑up and apply pressure to the BB. Raising to 300 (≈3bb) is a standard, natural sizing at this table: it gives fold equity, keeps the pot manageable with deep stacks, and matches common opening sizes I’ve seen here. If Flash 3‑bets big I’ll fold or re-evaluate based on size; if he calls I’ll use position to c‑bet or realize equity. Total committed after the raise will be 300.

I am in the Big Blind with 8c 4h. Minni, on the Button/Small Blind, has raised to 300. 84o is a very weak hand even for heads-up play. Given my current chip lead and the fact that we are nearing the end of the tournament (hand 137 of 150), there is no strategic reason to defend such a marginal hand out of position against an aggressive opponent. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack advantage.

I have 9h7h on the button (Small Blind) in a heads-up situation. This is a strong suited connector that plays very well post-flop and is definitely within a standard opening range. I will raise to 300 to take control of the hand and build the pot.

Heads-up from the big blind with 3♦4♠ — a weak, low, unsuited connector with some postflop playability but little preflop equity as a 3-bet bluff. Flash (on the button/SB) opened to 300; the pot is 400 and I need to call 200 more (300 total). I’ll call. Rationale: heads-up the SB opening range is wide so defending is standard, and the immediate pot odds (400:200 = 2:1) justify a call to see a flop. I avoid a 3-bet bluff because the hand is too marginal and Flash shows frequent preflop/streets aggression — a small 3-bet will often get called or 4-bet. Postflop plan: fold to heavy pressure on dry boards if I miss; continue/raise if I pair up or pick up strong draws, and use position to control pot size and extract when appropriate.

Flop: A♠ 7♦ 4♦. My hand: 3♦4♠ — bottom pair (paired the 4 on the board) with a weak kicker and one diamond. Heads‑up vs Flash’s wide opening range, he will C‑bet a lot, but the Ace on board and two diamonds make my hand vulnerable to A‑x, overpairs and flushes. I want to keep the pot under control with this marginal made hand and induce bluffs. Checking is best: it preserves my stack, lets villain barrel with air so I can call and realize equity, and keeps me from bloating the pot when I’m often behind. Plan: check; call small‑to‑medium continuation bets to see the turn and re‑evaluate, fold to large raises/overbets, and only lead/raise if I improve to two‑pair/trips or pick up a very strong draw.

On the flop of As-7d-4d, I have middle pair (sevens) with a Nine kicker. My opponent, Minni, has checked to me. While the board contains an Ace, which is often in a defender's range, I have a piece of it and want to bet for protection and value against draws (like diamond draws or gutshots). A bet here also helps define where I am in the hand. I will bet 300, which is half the pot.

I have bottom pair (4s) with a weak kicker and one diamond (3♦). The board A♠ 7♦ 4♦ is hostile — lots of Ax, 7x, and diamond combos beat me — but heads‑up Flash opened wide and will c‑bet a lot. His bet (300 into a ~900 pot) is a small/medium sizing that gives good pot odds to see the turn. Raising here would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and has poor fold equity against a wide but often calling button. Plan: call the 300 to realize equity and keep weaker hands and bluffs in his range; fold to a large turn barrel unless I improve to two‑pair/trips or pick up a clear, strong draw. If I pair the 3 or hit trips/two‑pair on the turn I can extract value or raise then.

Turn (A♦) paired the board — I now have two pair (Aces & Fours) with 3♦4♠, but it’s a vulnerable two‑pair: any A‑x in Flash’s wide opening/continuation range now makes trips and beats me, and 7x or a 4 in his hand can also beat me. Betting here would bloat the pot and mainly get called by hands that already beat me while folding the weaker hands I want to keep. I want pot control and to induce bluffs/small bets I can call, so I’ll check. Plan: check to keep the weaker part of his range and bluffs in; call small‑to‑medium turn bets to realize equity (I also have a diamond river draw), re‑evaluate on the river, and fold to very large overbets that strongly represent trips/full house.

On the turn, the board has paired the Ace and the diamond flush has completed. I have a pair of sevens with a nine kicker. Minni checked to me. While I have some showdown value, betting here doesn't accomplish much; better hands (Aces, flushes) will call or raise, and I'd likely only get folds from hands I already beat. Checking back allows me to control the pot size and realize my equity to the river.

I currently have two pair (Aces and Fours) with 3♦4♠ on A♠ 7♦ 4♦ Ad 2♠. This is a vulnerable two‑pair: any Ax gives trips and many 4x/4x+kickers can beat me. Villain (Flash) opened wide preflop and c‑bet the flop, but checked the turn after the board paired Aces — that check makes him having an Ace (trips) less likely and increases the share of missed draws/bluffs in his range. Betting the river would extract value from very thin hands but also bloats the pot and risks a raise from the small fraction of hands that beat me (trip Aces or full houses). I want to keep pot size controlled and induce bluffs/calls from worse hands. Therefore I’ll check/peace to showdown. Plan if Flash bets: call small-to-medium river bets (they’ll often be bluffs or worse pairs) but fold to large overbets/raises that strongly represent trips/full houses. If he checks back I show and collect when he missed.

I have two pair, Aces and Sevens, with a Nine kicker on a board where the Ace is paired and a flush completed on the turn. My opponent has checked three times. While I might have the best hand, betting is unlikely to get called by many worse hands and could expose me to a check-raise from a slow-played flush or a stronger Ace. Given the board texture and my position, checking back is the most prudent play to realize my showdown value and win the pot against missed draws or smaller pairs.

Heads-up on the button I should be opening very wide. 4♣5♣ (4h5c) is marginal, but in heads-up position it has enough playability and fold equity to raise rather than just limp/call. I’ll apply pressure with a standard open to 300 (about 3x the BB) — big enough to take fold equity and to define the pot, but not so large that I’m committing too many chips with a weak hand. Postflop plan: c-bet most flops and use position to float and take pots; if I face heavy resistance on dangerous high-card boards or I miss and face a large raise, I’ll give up and preserve stack. Against a wide-defending BB this aggressive open should be +EV.

I am holding Ten of Hearts and Four of Clubs in the Big Blind. Minni has raised to 300 from the Small Blind. Given that Ten-Four offsuit is a very weak hand and I am out of position for the rest of the hand, there is no reason to defend this against a raise, especially since I am leading the tournament with only about 11 hands remaining. Folding is the safest way to preserve my stack and avoid unnecessary risks.

I am holding 9-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. With a substantial lead of nearly 9,000 chips and only 11 hands remaining in the tournament, there is no strategic justification for playing this hand. Protecting my chip lead and minimizing unnecessary risk is the priority as we approach the end of the 150-hand limit.

Heads-up on the button with A4 offsuit — a playable, often-best hand in heads-up. I want initiative and to leverage position, so I’ll open-raise rather than limp/call. Standard 3x-ish sizing (raise to 300 total) puts pressure on the big blind’s wide range while keeping the pot at a comfortable level with ~156bb effective stacks; it sets up c-bets on many flops and lets me fold to a big 3-bet if Flash shows heavy aggression. Calling 50 is too passive here; raising to 300 maximizes fold equity and postflop edge.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Ten offsuit, which is a strong hand to defend against a 3x raise in a heads-up situation. The pot odds are 3:1, and with my current chip lead and the tournament nearing its end (hand 141 of 150), calling to see a flop is a standard and safe play without overcommitting chips out of position.

I have an open-ended straight draw with overcards on a very connected board. Being out of position as the Big Blind, checking is the standard play to see how the pre-flop aggressor responds. Given my equity with the draw, I plan to continue in the hand if faced with a bet.

Heads-up I was the preflop aggressor with A♥4♠. Flop 7♠6♠8♥ gives me an overcard (Ace) and a backdoor spade — decent blocking value and reasonable equity, but the two-spade, connected texture is somewhat dangerous. Flash checked; I should continue to use my initiative and fold out pure air and many weak pairs/draws. I’ll c-bet ~2/3 pot (400 into the 600) — large enough to charge spade/straight draws and extract value from worse A-x hands, but small enough to keep the pot controllable with a marginal holding. Plan: if he folds I take the pot; if he calls I re-evaluate on the turn (value-bet big when I pair the Ace or improve, pot-control or check back on blanks). If I face a sizeable check-raise I’ll give strong credit to sets/two-pair and fold most of the time.

I have an open-ended straight draw on a very connected board. With 8 outs to a straight (any Nine or Queen), I have roughly a 32% chance of hitting my hand by the river. Minni's bet of 400 into a 600-chip pot gives me pot odds of approximately 28.5% (400 to win 1400). Since my equity is higher than the pot odds and I have a significant chip lead late in the tournament, calling is the most prudent move to see a turn card and potentially realize my equity without risking a large portion of my stack in a check-raise.

On this highly connected turn of 7s-6s-8h-4d, the board is extremely coordinated. My Jack-Ten offsuit currently has a gutshot straight draw to a nine, while any five on the river would complete a straight for anyone. Being out of position against Minni, and given the wetness of the board which likely connects with a wide range of hands, checking is the most appropriate action. This allows me to see a river card for free if she checks back or control the pot size if she decides to bet. With a significant chip lead and the tournament winding down, there is no reason to play a high-variance line here.

Turn pairs my 4: I now have a weak made hand (pair of 4s) with the A kicker after the 4♦. Flash called my sizable flop c-bet, so his continuing range is wide (spade draws, weak pairs, A‑x, some connectors). Checking lets him see a free river and gives live draws a cheap card; betting charges the obvious hands that still have equity (two‑spade flush draws, 5/9 straight draws) and extracts value from worse A‑x and small pairs. I’ll bet ~1/2 pot to maximize value vs draws and marginal pairs while keeping the pot controllable with deep stacks. If he jams/raises big I’ll give credit to sets/two‑pair and fold; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate the river.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Ten offsuit on a highly coordinated board of 7s-6s-8h-4d. I currently hold an open-ended straight draw, needing a Nine to complete a Jack-high straight (J-T-9-8-7) or a Five to complete an Eight-high straight (8-7-6-5-4). While the Five would likely result in a split pot as it puts the straight on the board, hitting a Nine would give me the nuts against most of Minni's range. Minni's bet of 700 into a 1400 pot gives me 3:1 pot odds (25%), and while my raw equity to hit on the river is approximately 17.4%, the implied odds—especially if I hit the Nine—justify a call. Given my substantial chip lead and the fact that we are nearing the end of the tournament (hand 141/150), calling to see the river is a sound strategic move that balances risk with the potential to win a significant pot.

I am the Big Blind with Jack-Ten offsuit. The board is 7s-6s-8h-4d-8d. I have missed my straight draws (needing a Nine or a Five) and currently hold only Jack-high. Minni has been the aggressor throughout the hand, betting both the flop and the turn. Given that I have no showdown value and the board is highly coordinated, making it likely that Minni has at least a piece of it or a made straight, I cannot justify a bluff. Furthermore, I have a significant chip lead (22,925 vs 14,275) with only nine hands remaining in the tournament. My priority is to minimize risk and preserve my stack. I will check and plan to fold if faced with a bet.

River pairs the 8s (board 7♠ 6♠ 8♥ 4♦ 8♦). I hold A♣4♠ (pair of 4s with an Ace kicker) — a very marginal showdown hand now. Flash called both my flop and turn bets, so his continuing range contains a lot of hands that beat me on the river (any 8 for trips/quads, any 7x/6x for two pair, 5x straights, plus stronger pairs). A river bluff is thin — it will fold only air/A‑high often and get called or check‑raised by most of the range that beats me. With a relatively small pot (2,800) versus deep stacks I’d rather avoid bloating the pot and risk a big loss; checking and going to showdown is the prudent +EV line here.

I am currently the chip leader with 22,875 chips against Minni's 16,975, and we are entering the final stage of the tournament (hand 142 of 150). Holding 8h-3s on the button (small blind) is a very weak starting hand. While I have position post-flop, the quality of this hand is too low to justify even a small call or a steal attempt at this stage. My priority is to preserve my lead and avoid unnecessary variance or giving Minni opportunities to double up. Folding here is the safest play to maintain my stack advantage.

Heads-up on the button (I posted SB). I hold A♦4♥ — a strong, open-raising hand in heads-up. Facing only the BB and with deep stacks (~170bb effective), I want to take the initiative, price out speculative hands, and play the pot in position. Flash has shown an active preflop/defensive style, so a standard open is appropriate. I will raise to 300 total (3xBB): large enough to apply pressure and consistent with my usual opening sizes, but small enough not to commit the stack. Plan: if Flash folds I scoop the blinds; if he calls I use position to c-bet on dry flops and extract value when I pair the Ace; if he 3-bets big I will usually fold this hand (or re-evaluate vs small sizing/read).

I am holding 7 of Clubs and 5 of Clubs in the Big Blind. Minni has raised to 300 from the Small Blind. In a heads-up scenario, a suited connector like 75s is a solid hand to defend with, especially given the pot odds of 2:1 (calling 200 to win a total pot of 600). I will call to see a flop and utilize my stack advantage.

I have middle pair (fives) on a relatively dry King-high board. Since Minni was the pre-flop aggressor, I will check to see how they react. Fives are strong enough to call a standard continuation bet, but not strong enough to lead out with on this texture. Checking allows me to control the pot size and evaluate Minni's betting pattern.

I opened preflop with A♦4♥ and the BB (Flash) just called. Flop K♠ 5♥ 3♦ is very dry and I missed, but as the preflop aggressor my range is stronger here than his and this board will fold out a lot of his missed hands. Flash checked, so a small continuation bet preserves fold equity without bloating the pot out of position — I’ll c‑bet ~1/3 pot. Size: 200 into the 600 pot. If he folds I scoop; if he calls I still have ace‑high showdown value, a 4‑card wheel draw to a 2 (4 outs) and a backdoor heart possibility. If he check‑raises strongly I’ll give up A4 (fold) and re‑evaluate only vs a very small raise.

I have middle pair (fives) on a very dry King-high board. Minni's 200 bet into a 600 pot is a small continuation bet which could represent a King, but is also frequently a standard play with air or a smaller piece of the board. I am getting 4:1 pot odds to call, which makes this an easy continue to see a turn card and re-evaluate based on the next street's action.

I have a middle pair (fives) and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 6). Minni checked the turn after leading the flop with a small bet. This check often indicates either a realization that their air isn't working or a desire to pot control with a marginal hand. By checking back, I realize my equity for free and can potentially improve on the river or reach a cheap showdown. There is little value in betting here as I am unlikely to be called by worse hands, and I have enough showdown value to avoid turning my hand into a bluff at this stage.

I hold A♦4♥ and the turn (4♦) pairs my 4 — I now have second pair with the Ace kicker. I was the preflop aggressor and c‑bet the dry K♠‑5♥‑3♦ flop; Flash called the flop and then checked the turn, which often indicates weakness or pot‑control. This is a clear value/protection spot: the board is fairly dry, there are very few river cards that will beat me (mainly a 2 completing a 2‑3‑4‑5 wheel, or a turned/rivered K/5 that already beats me), and a bet will charge floats and worse pairs while folding out equity hands. I will make a size ~2/3 pot to get value and deny correct odds to draws. If Flash check‑raises large I will usually fold this hand and re‑evaluate only versus a very small raise. Bet 700 into the 1000 pot to extract value and protect my hand.

On the turn, the 4 of Diamonds adds a gutshot straight draw (needing a 6) to my existing middle pair of fives. Minni has increased their betting sizing to 700 into a 1000-chip pot (70% pot), which represents strength, likely a King or a strong draw. However, with middle pair and a gutshot, I have enough equity and potential to improve to continue. I am getting roughly 2.4:1 odds on a call. Given my stack lead and the double-edged nature of the board (straight and flush draws), I will call to see the river and evaluate based on whether I improve or if Minni continues their aggression.

I have a pair of fives (middle pair from the flop), which holds some modest showdown value in a heads-up pot. The board is King-high, and the Ten of Diamonds on the river is another overcard to my pair, potentially improving hands like AT, QT, or JT if Minni was semi-bluffing. As I am first to act on the river, I will check. My hand is not strong enough to bet for value, and checking allows me to see if Minni checks back for a showdown or to evaluate the situation if they decide to bet.

I check. Reasoning: I hold A♦4♥ — I paired my 4 on the turn so I only have a single pair (4s) with an Ace kicker. The action line (Flash called my 200 flop c‑bet and my 700 turn bet, then checked the river) is consistent with medium‑strength made hands (Kx, 5x, 3x, mid pocket pairs) or cautious river protection rather than pure air. The river T♦ is important: it completes a lot of diamond combos (any two diamonds in his hand now make a flush) and also gives some players a ten to beat my 4s. A river bet here would be a thin value spot — worse hands that will call are limited, while many of the hands that call beat me. Given the completed diamond, the call history, and the relatively small pot vs deep stacks, the lowest risk / highest EV line is to check back to showdown and avoid turning a marginal hand into a bluff that is likely to be called by better hands.

In a heads-up situation, 6s 8s is a strong speculative hand with good connectivity and flush potential. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to take initiative from the button and put pressure on Minni's big blind.

Flash opened to 300 from the button. Pot is 400 and I need 200 more to call (300 total). 6c2c is a very weak hand but it’s suited, so it has some backdoor/flush and low-pair potential. The break-even equity for a call here is ~33% (200 to win ~600). Against a typical wide heads-up open I estimate this hand has roughly 35–40% equity, so a defend is justified. I don’t want to 3-bet bluff with such a poorly connected/low hand — I’ll be dominated when called and a larger 3-bet is too risky. Plan: call cheaply to see a flop and fold to heavy aggression unless I pair or pick up a meaningful draw; press when I hit. Call to 300 total.

I flopped middle pair (6c2c on 4d-6h-9s). Out of position vs a wide heads-up raiser, my hand is vulnerable to overcards and to 9x/6x in his range, so I want pot control. Checking lets Flash c-bet with bluffs and weaker pairs so I can call without bloating the pot OOP. Plan: check and call small-to-medium continuation bets (roughly up to ~1/2 pot); fold or strongly re-evaluate to large raises/overbets that show significant strength. If Flash checks back I’ll take a free turn and look to extract value on safe runouts or continue controlling the pot. Action: check to keep the pot manageable with a marginal made hand.

On a flop of 4d-6h-9s, I have middle pair with an 8 kicker. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and Minni checked to me, a continuation bet is standard to maintain initiative and protect my hand against overcards or straight draws like 7-8 or 5-7. I will bet 300, which is half the pot, to see where I stand while keeping the pot manageable with a marginal made hand.

Call 300. I have middle pair (6x) with a very weak kicker (6c2c) and I'm out of position, so I want to control the pot and realize equity rather than bloat the pot with a marginal hand. The pot is 900 and Flash's bet is 300 — calling requires ~25% equity (300/(900+300)). Versus a wide heads-up c-bet range my pair of six is often good or has reasonable showdown equity, so a flat call is justified. I won't raise here (I'd be dominated too often), and I will re-evaluate on the turn: fold to strong, polarized aggression unless I improve to two pair/trips or pick up a meaningful draw; call small-to-medium barrels and extract value on safe runouts.

Board is 4d-6h-9s-Qs and I hold 6c-2c: middle pair with a very weak kicker, out of position.
