I called the flop and the small turn bet to control the pot and realize equity; pot is 1200 and there is currently no bet to face. Betting OOP here would bloat the pot with a marginal hand, expose me to check-raises, and get me in trouble vs Qx/9x or a spade. Checking keeps the pot manageable and lets me see the river cheaply — I can call small-to-medium river bets for value/protection but fold to large polarized aggression. If the river improves me to two pair/trips or is a very safe card, I can extract value; otherwise I’ll keep pot control and avoid risking a big chunk of my stack with a weak kicker. Action: check.

On the turn, I have third pair (sixes) and have picked up a spade flush draw with the Queen of Spades. Minni has checked to me again. While the Queen is a scare card that could have hit her range, it also gives me significant additional equity. By checking behind, I realize my equity for free, ensuring I see the river. This also controls the size of the pot with a marginal made hand that has strong potential to improve to a flush or two pair, while avoiding a potential check-raise from a Queen or a slow-played Nine.

I hold 6♣2♣ on 4♦-6♥-9♠-Q♠-K♦ — just middle pair with a terrible kicker, out of position. Flash barreled the flop and I called; both checked the turn, which showed weakness or a marginal/check‑behind line. The river K is precisely the kind of card that helps his value combos (Kx) and doesn’t improve me. Betting as a bluff here will be called by most hands that beat me (Kx/Qx/9x) and won’t extract value from many worse hands. The pot is small (~1,200) relative to stacks, so the highest EV play is to control risk and take this to showdown. Action: check and see his river decision (call small thin bets if he fires, fold to large polarized river bets).

On a river board of 4d-6h-9s-Qs-Kd, I have a pair of sixes, which is currently the fourth pair. Minni has checked to me on the river after calling my flop bet and checking the turn. While the King and Queen are overcards that could have hit her range, her repeated checking suggests she may be holding a weak made hand or a missed draw. I have some showdown value with my pair of sixes, especially against missed straight draws like 5-7 or 7-8. Betting here is unlikely to get a better hand to fold, and it's also unlikely that a worse hand will call. Therefore, checking behind to reach showdown is the most sensible play to minimize risk while maintaining my chip lead.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with 2♠4♥: a very weak, unsuited hand, but the cost to see a flop is tiny (50 more). With deep stacks (~159bb) and positional advantage postflop, calling is the low-variance, +EV choice — you get ~3:1 pot odds to see the flop and can fold cheaply if you miss. Open-raising to 200 is a pure bluff here that risks a 3-bet or difficult postflop spots against an opponent who shows aggression; folding is too tight given the cheap price and position. Plan: call to 100 total, evaluate the flop in position and fold to heavy action unless we pick up a pair or strong draw.

I am in the Big Blind with Q7s, a hand that is well above average in heads-up play. Minni has completed the small blind, suggesting a wider or potentially weaker range than a raise. While I will be out of position post-flop, raising here allows me to take initiative, extract value, and potentially end the hand pre-flop. I will raise to 400 to define the pot.

I’ll call to 400 total. I’m on the button (SB) with 2♠4♥ — a very weak hand, but the extra 300 to call is tiny relative to my ~158bb stack and keeps me in position postflop. The pot is 500 now; after my call it will be 800, so I’m paying 300 to win 800 (I need ~37.5% equity to justify a pure call). Versus Flash’s aggressive, often-wide raising tendencies this is a low-variance defend: I can fold cheaply to heavy action on dry boards or use position to realize equity and apply pressure when I pick up a pair or a real draw. I won’t 3‑bet with this holding (too thin) — plan is to check/fold most misses, continue only with a pair/two pair/strong draw, and look for spots to exploit his tendencies on later streets.

I am the pre-flop aggressor in a heads-up pot. The flop of Th-6h-3s is relatively disconnected, although there is a heart flush draw available. My hand, Qs7s, contains two overcards to the middle and bottom board cards. Given the board texture and my initiative from pre-flop, a continuation bet is the standard play here to represent a strong Ten, an overpair, or high cards like AJ/AK. I will bet 400, which is 50% of the pot, to put pressure on Minni's likely capped limp-calling range and to fold out hands that missed the flop entirely.

Call to 800. I have 2♠4♥ on T♥6♥3♠ — no made hand but reasonable equity. Flash’s small c‑bet (400 into the pot) is likely a wide heads‑up continuation bet. Calling 400 to win ~1200 gives me ~25% pot‑equity required. I have ~10 clean outs (three 2s + three 4s + four 5s) to make a pair or the straight (~38% by river), plus a small runner‑runner heart/flush chance — comfortably above the required threshold. In position I can fold to heavy turn aggression if I miss, or continue/raise when I pair or complete a draw. I’ll call and reevaluate on the turn.

The turn brings the Ten of Spades, pairing the top card of the board. As the pre-flop aggressor and flop bettor, I've represented a strong range including overpairs and Tens. However, Minni's call on the flop indicates she likely has a piece of the board (like a Six) or a heart flush draw. While the pairing Ten is theoretically a good card for me to continue bluffing, Minni has shown a tendency to be sticky in previous hands (e.g., Hand #126 and #14). With only five hands remaining in the tournament and a comfortable chip lead, I prefer to check here to control the pot size. This allows me to see a river card potentially for free and prevents me from being forced out of the hand by a semi-bluff raise if she is on a draw.

Check. I have nothing (2♠4♥) on T♥6♥3♠ / Ts and only ~10 clean outs (3 twos + 3 fours + 4 fives) — ~10/46 ≈ 21.7% to improve on the river. Flash checked the turn after firing the flop; that line can be pot‑control with a ten or a medium pair, a missed c‑bet, or a trap. Betting here would be a thin bluff: my fold equity is limited against his wide calling/raising range and I’d prefer not to build a bigger pot with only ~22% equity. With deep stacks and position I’ll control the pot, take my free card, and fold to meaningful river aggression if I miss (or value line/raise if I pair or complete the straight).

On the river, the board reads Th-6h-3s-Ts-Jc. My hand is Queen-high, which holds very little showdown value. Although I was the aggressor pre-flop and on the flop, the turn checked through, suggesting both players might be looking for a cheap showdown. Minni's limp-call pre-flop and call on the flop could indicate a mid-pair or a heart draw that has now missed. While the Jack is a potential scare card I could represent, Minni has demonstrated 'sticky' behavior in previous hands. With only five hands remaining in the tournament and a substantial chip lead, my primary objective is to preserve my stack and avoid high-variance situations. Checking allows me to see if she checks behind with a hand I might beat (unlikely) or simply fold if she bets, without further depleting my stack on a risky bluff.

Heads‑up river on T♥‑6♥‑3♠‑T♠‑J♣. Villain (preflop raiser) c‑bet the flop, I called; both checked the turn and villain checked the river. I hold 2♠4♥ — no pair, no meaningful blocker to Tens or Jacks, and essentially zero showdown value. Villain’s check‑back line is consistent with a pot‑control Ten, a Jack, or a medium/weak pair — exactly the range that calls a river bluff. My fold equity here is very small and a bluff will be called by all the hands that beat me while folding only the very weakest air. The pot is 1,600 and there’s no reason to throw chips away with no value and poor blocker coverage. Plan: preserve chips, check to showdown, and fold to any river aggression.

I am currently holding Eight of Clubs and Deuce of Diamonds, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. With only five hands remaining in the tournament and a significant chip lead over Minni, there is no strategic reason to play such a weak hand out of position or waste chips. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to protect my lead.

Heads-up on the button with K♠T♠ — a strong, suited broadway and very playable in position. With deep stacks and the table tending to use 3x opens, I'll raise to 300 (3 BB) to take the initiative, build a pot I can play in position, and get value from worse hands while generating fold equity. If Flash 3-bets to a reasonable size I'll continue in position; I will re-evaluate or fold to a very large 3-bet depending on sizing and reads. Calling 50 would be too passive here — opening is the optimal play.

With a significant chip lead and only four hands remaining in the tournament, I am looking to avoid high-risk situations while still playing fundamentally sound poker. Minni has been opening frequently from the button. My hand, Ten-Four suited, is a marginal defense but is playable heads-up for a single raise, especially getting 2:1 on the call. I will call to see a flop and proceed cautiously, as I do not need to take unnecessary risks to maintain my lead.

With only four hands remaining in the tournament and a significant chip lead, my primary objective is to avoid losing large pots. On a paired flop of 7s-Qc-Qs, I have missed completely with Ten-Four of clubs, holding only a backdoor flush draw. As the big blind, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. If Minni bets, I will evaluate based on the bet size, but I have no incentive to bluff or commit chips to this pot with a weak hand given the tournament context.

Flop: 7♠ Qc Qs. I have K♠ T♠ — no pair but two spades (four spades on table+hand) so I have a robust flush draw (9 outs) and an overcard (K) that can make top pair. Against a checked pot heads-up I should continue to take the initiative: a half-pot c-bet applies pressure to many missed hands, gets value from spade draws and worse Qx or broadways that call, and builds the pot for when I hit. Betting 300 (≈1/2 pot) is small enough to get called by a wide range but big enough to fold a lot of pure air. If Flash check-raises large (polarized) I’ll respect it and re-evaluate/fold; I’ll call smaller raises to realize my ~35% equity to the river and play position. Therefore: bet 300.

I have a significant chip lead of approximately 10,000 chips with only four hands remaining in the tournament. My goal is to protect this lead and avoid any unnecessary large confrontations. On a paired board of 7s-Qc-Qs, my Ten-Four of clubs has missed entirely, offering only a marginal backdoor flush draw. Minni has placed a standard continuation bet of 300 into a 600-chip pot. While the pot odds are 3:1 to call, there is no strategic benefit to continuing with air in this situation. Folding is the safest and most disciplined play to ensure I maintain my lead for the final few hands of the tournament.

I am on the button with Qc 4s. With the blinds at 50/100, I am the small blind and have already committed 50 chips. My hand is relatively weak, but in a heads-up situation, a Queen-high hand has some potential. Minni is in the big blind with a smaller stack than mine (15,375 vs 24,475). Looking at the match history, I have been fairly active with opening raises. Given that we are near the end of the tournament (hand 148 of 150), I want to maintain my lead. However, Qc 4s is not a strong enough hand to warrant a significant raise if I expect a lot of resistance. Folding is too tight for heads-up, but raising might be unnecessary risk. Calling the additional 50 to see a flop is a balanced approach that allows me to evaluate the board without over-committing with a sub-par hand.

Heads-up vs a limp from the button, K♥T♥ is a strong, playable hand — good top-pair/straight/flush potential and ahead of much of a limp/call range. I want to take the initiative and isolate Flash while keeping the pot controlled. Raising to 300 (a standard ~3xBB sizing) gives fold equity, charges worse hands and draws, and still leaves plenty of stack behind to play post‑flop. If he 3-bets big I can fold or re-evaluate; if he calls I’ll C-bet appropriate flops and extract value when I improve. This is an exploitative, low-variance aggression to maximize expected value heads-up.

I am on the button with Qc 4s and have already committed 100 chips to the pot. Minni has raised to 300, so it costs me another 200 to call. In heads-up play, Q-high has reasonable equity, and I will have the advantage of acting last on all subsequent betting rounds. The pot odds are 3:1 (200 to call into a 600 pot), which makes a call justifiable to see a flop. Given my significant chip lead and the fact that only three hands remain in the tournament, I want to avoid unnecessary large confrontations unless I hit a strong hand, but I can afford this small call to see if I can improve on the flop.

Flop: Q♥ 6♥ 6♠. I hold K♥ T♥ — I don’t have a made pair but I have a very strong semi‑bluff: a 9‑card heart flush draw (~35% to hit by river) and a high overcard (a King, ~12% to pair by river). Heads‑up, Flash’s caller range contains a lot of missed hands that fold to a C‑bet (broadways, small pairs, unpaired connectors) as well as some Qx / 66 / heart draws. I want to take the initiative, charge draws, and fold out many of his non‑made hands while keeping the pot manageable given we’re deep. A half‑pot C‑bet (~300 into the 600 pot) is the right size: large enough to get folds and deny equity, small enough to get called by worse draws and Qx so I can realize my equity. Plan: if he folds I win; if he calls I proceed with the draw and re‑evaluate on turn; if he raises big I’ll fold or re‑evaluate depending on size (call small raises for pot odds, fold large bluffs).

I have flopped top pair with my Queen on a paired board (Qh 6h 6s). In heads-up play, top pair is a very strong holding. Minni led out with a 300 bet into a 600 pot. While my kicker is weak (4s), I am likely ahead of a wide range of hands, including bluffs, draws, and lower pairs. Raising here might drive out her bluffs and only get called by hands that beat me (like a 6 or a Queen with a better kicker). By calling, I keep the pot manageable and allow her to continue betting with air or draws on the turn. Given the tournament stage and my chip lead, I want to maximize value while minimizing the risk of a massive blow-up.

Turn K♣ pairs my K♥ giving me top pair with a T kicker plus the four‑card heart combination (9 hearts left). Flash called my half‑pot flop c‑bet, so his range has a lot of Qx, some 6x (trips), heart draws and broadways — many of which I now beat. I want to extract value from worse pairs and charge/deny equity to heart draws while keeping the pot manageable against the small number of trip/monster combos. Betting ~2/3 pot (800 into the 1200) achieves that: good value vs Qx and worse hands, forces some draws to pay to see the river, and keeps a clear plan if I face a raise (fold or re‑evaluate to a shove depending on sizing; call small raises for pot odds). If called I’ll re‑evaluate on the river and size a value bet or pot‑control as appropriate.

On the turn, the King is an overcard to my pair of Queens, but in heads-up play, top pair remains a very strong hand. Minni's turn bet of 800 into a 1200 pot represents strength, but it could also be a continuation of a bluff or a semi-bluff with a heart draw, especially using the King as a scare card. My kicker (4s) is weak, but folding here would be too exploitable. I have a significant chip lead and can afford to call to see the river. I will evaluate her action on the river, but for now, I am getting 2.5:1 on a call and my hand still has significant showdown value.

River: Q♥ 6♥ 6♠ K♣ A♥. With K♥T♥ I make the nut heart flush (A K Q T 6 — no heart higher than my K is available), so almost every hand that beats me is a very narrow set (quads/full house: pocket 6s, pocket QQ, pocket AA, or combos that make a full house). Flash’s line — calling my flop c‑bet and my sizable turn bet — is consistent with Qx, Kx, missed draws, or single red‑heart draws; many of those hands will call a river value bet. Those monster/full‑house combos are possible but relatively rare and would have called/raised earlier or will shove any river raise, so I should be extracting value from the bulk of his calling range.

I will bet for value. Size: ~2/3 pot (bet 2000 into 2800). That’s large enough to get value from one‑pair hands, worse hearts and Kx/Qx, but not so enormous that I overcommit my stack versus a river raise. If Flash raises big I’ll assume a full house/quads and fold or re‑evaluate depending on sizing; if he just calls, I win the pot more often than not.

The river card is disastrous for my hand. The Ace of Hearts completes a possible flush (three hearts now on board) and is a second overcard to my pair of Queens. Minni has led out on every street (pre-flop, flop, turn, and river). This line is very strong, especially on a board that ran out Queen-Six-Six-King-Ace. My pair of Queens is now at best the fourth-best possible pair (behind Aces, Kings, and Trip Sixes) and loses to any flush. Given the tournament context—I have a substantial chip lead and only three hands remain—making a risky call with a hand that has been significantly demoted by the runout is unnecessary. I will fold to preserve my stack and maintain my lead for the final two hands.

Heads-up on the button (I am SB) with K♥Q♠ — a top heads-up holding. Pot is 150 and it only costs 50 to see the flop, but I want to seize initiative and apply pressure to Flash’s wide BB defending range. A standard 3x BB open to 300 (total committed) gives good fold equity, builds value when I hit, and keeps the pot a manageable size deep-stacked. Plan: c-bet on favorable flops, control pot or check when board looks dangerous; fold or fold-heavy against a large, polarized 3-bet.

I have been dealt Jack of Spades and Jack of Hearts (JJ), which is a very strong premium pocket pair, especially in a heads-up situation. Minni has opened from the small blind/button with a raise to 300. Given the strength of my hand and the fact that we are late in the tournament (Hand #149), I want to build a large pot and potentially end the hand pre-flop or get value from weaker hands. A 3-bet is the standard and most effective play here. I will raise to 1000 total to apply pressure and define the hand.

I opened to 300 on the button with K♥Q♠ and Flash 3-bet to 1000. This is a decent heads-up holding but not strong enough to 4-bet for value vs a likely polarized 3-bet range. I have position and calling keeps the pot manageable while allowing post‑flop play. Calling costs 700 more into a 1300 pot (pot ≈2000 after my call) — I only need ~35% equity and KQ performs well vs a wide 3-bet range. Plan: call 1000 total, play the flop in position, c-bet favorable textures and proceed cautiously (check/fold to heavy aggression) on destructive boards.

On a flop of Kc-Ah-Jd, I have flopped a set of Jacks. While this is a very strong hand, the board is extremely 'wet' and coordinates well with Minni's likely range for calling a 3-bet (Ax, Kx, QJ, and potentially QT for a straight). As the pre-flop aggressor, I want to continue building the pot and extract value from hands like AK, AQ, AJ, or KQ. I will lead with a bet of 1000, which is half the pot, to charge any draws and get value from top-pair or two-pair hands while keeping the pot manageable if I am currently behind a straight or a higher set.

Board Kc Ah Jd; I hold KhQs — top pair with a decent kicker but vulnerable to any Ace. Flash was the pre‑flop 3‑better (wide heads‑up range) and now made a small flop bet (~1/3 pot). Pot is ~3000 and it costs 1000 to see the turn (pot odds ~3:1 → ~25% equity required). Calling is the best line: I keep a lot of worse hands and bluffs in the pot, use my position to control size, and realize good equity with KQ against a wide 3‑bet/c‑bet range. Raising (min to 2000) would bloat the pot while I’m often behind to Ax and is unnecessarily high variance here. Plan: call 1000, reevaluate on the turn — fold to clear strength if an Ace arrives and Flash shows aggression, but look to extract or apply pressure if the turn is a blank or improves my relative hand.

I have a set of Jacks on a board of Kc-Ah-Jd-6h. The 6h turn is largely a brick, though it does open a backdoor heart draw. My hand remains extremely strong, although I must be wary of QT which completed a straight on the flop. However, I must continue to bet for value against Minni's range, which likely includes Ax, Kx, and two-pair hands like AJ or KJ. By betting 2000 into a 4000 pot, I am building the pot for value while maintaining a stack that allows for flexibility on the river. If I am behind to a straight, I still have 10 outs (any Ace, King, Six, or the final Jack) to improve to a full house or quads on the river.

I have top pair (K) with a decent Q kicker on Kc-Ah-Jd-6h. Flash was the preflop 3-bettor and has been betting small (1k on flop, 2k on turn into a 6k pot) — a line consistent with a wide, mixed range that contains Ax and sets but also a lot of thinner value and bluffs. Calling 2,000 gets ~3:1 pot odds (~25% equity required) and keeps worse hands and bluffs in the pot while preserving position. Raising would bloat the pot out of position and is thin against Ax/sets that dominate me; it also gives Flash a chance to isolate with strong hands or shove. I’ll call to realize equity, control size, and re-evaluate on the river (fold to clear strength if an Ace/monster is shown, extract value or bluff selectively on a blank).

On the river, the board has completed with a third heart (3h), meaning a backdoor flush is now possible. Additionally, the broadway straight (QT) has been possible since the flop. While my set of Jacks is extremely strong, the board is quite coordinated and dangerous. Since I have been the aggressor throughout the hand, checking the river is a prudent move. It allows me to control the pot size and potentially induce a bluff from weaker hands like Ax or two-pair that might have been calling my previous bets. If Minni checks behind, I likely have the best hand and win a healthy pot. If Minni bets, I will have to evaluate the size and her tendencies to decide on a call, but checking is the best way to navigate this specific river card.

The river 3h makes the board Kc Ah Jd 6h 3h. I hold K♥Q♠ — top pair with a decent kicker, but I’m crushed by any Ax, two pair, or sets and the river completes a three‑heart board that gives any two‑heart combo a flush. Flash was the preflop 3‑better and barreled flop and turn, then checked the river — a line consistent with either medium strength (pot‑control Ax/Kx) or a missed/indifferent hand. Betting here risks folding out thin calls (small Kx) while getting value from worse only rarely, and it also opens me up to a raise from the hands that beat me. I have position and can realize showdown value without bloating the pot, so the prudent line is to check back and go to showdown.

I am currently the chip leader by a significant margin (27,075 vs 12,775) going into the final hand of the tournament. With Qh Ts on the button in a heads-up situation, I have a very strong starting hand. My goal is to maximize my stack while maintaining my lead. A standard raise to 300 chips (3x the big blind) is appropriate here to build the pot and exert pressure on the big blind. Given my chip advantage, I can afford to play aggressively to increase my final score.

I have 7♣2♠ — basically the worst non-pair hand. Flash (button/small blind) opened to 300; the pot is 400 and it costs me 200 to call. Calling gives me ~3:1 pot odds (need ~25% equity), but 7-2 offsuit is heavily dominated by a typical BTN open range and has far less equity than that. With deep stacks (SPR > 20) a call would also leave me in tough multi-street decisions with a junk hand. A light 3-bet bluff or shove is thin here (no meaningful blockers and opponent is deep), so the prudent play is to fold and wait for a better spot.
